This review mapped the main actors and partners influencing national policies, evaluated existing practices and challenges, and addressed the development of participatory strategies for identifying the needs of marginalised communities. 

 

At the institutional level, Angolan researchers have participated in webinars and training sessions on open peer review and collaborative preprint evaluation, aiming to increase transparency in the review process. The report also identifies activity in the valuing and sharing of negative scientific results, participatory methods such as citizen science and crowdsourcing, and open innovation practices, though it does not elaborate on these areas.

Angola promotes international scientific collaborations on open science through partnerships with UNESCO, Redalyc/AmeliCA and UNCTAD. These partnerships centre on capacity building, policy alignment and knowledge-sharing. Angolan universities and research institutions are also engaging in open-access publishing platforms and open research data repositories, and some are reported to be working towards institutional open access policies to align them with international best practices. 

 

Regarding a strategy for cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration, the report indicates that a fully formalised national strategy is still in progress. Angola is aligning its policies with the 2021 Recommendation and engaging with Redalyc/AmeliCA to support international collaboration. Training programmes are being conducted to improve researchers' skills in open science practices, and government-backed initiatives encourage knowledge-sharing between Angolan and international research institutions. 

 

Angola reports involvement in discussions on regional and international funding mechanisms for open science through FUNDECIT.

Belarus reported several state-funded conferences, forums and exhibitions as awareness-raising activities on open science; however, the report does not clarify how open science is integrated into these activities. Reported examples include the annual International Scientific Conference "Problems of Forecasting and State Regulation of Socio-Economic Development" at the Scientific Research Economic Institute of the Ministry of Economy, the annual conference "Youth in Science" at the National Academy of Sciences of Belarus (NAS of Belarus), the annual International Forum on Information and Communication Technologies  “TIBO”, the annual International Scientific and Practical Conference "Intellectual Property in the Modern World: Challenges of the Time and Development Prospects" and the Congress of Technology and Innovation Support Centres.

 

In addition, two thematic events dedicated to open science were organised in 2024: the "Open Science and Artificial Intelligence: Ethical Issues" webinar, organised by the Institute of Philosophy of the NAS of Belarus, and the Open Science Week, organised by the National Library of Belarus and Belarusian academic libraires, in partnership with the State Public Scientific and Technical Library of the Siberian Branch of the Russian Academy of Sciences.

Belarus reported several national science, technology and innovation (STI) policy documents, including the Strategy "Science and Technology: 2018–2040", developed by the NAS of Belarus, the State Programme of Innovative Development 2021–2025, the National Strategy for Sustainable Development until 2035,  the Programme of Socio-Economic Development 2021–2025 and the State Programme "Science-Intensive Technologies and Equipment" 2021–2025. These documents are reported as being based on open science principles, covering elements such as open access to scientific knowledge, open scientific infrastructure, open participation of social actors, open dialogue with other knowledge systems.

 

Coordination of open science policy at the national level is described through the existing Law No. 157-З “On State Forecasting and State Planning”, under which all planning documents are hierarchically coordinated in terms of goals, priorities, implementation activities, resources and indicators.

 

Open science practices are integrated into existing public research funding mechanisms. The Belarusian Institute of System Analysis and Information Support for Science and Technology (BelISA) registers reports from publicly funded research and development (R&D) projects and provides access to the R&D results upon request. The Belarusian Republican Foundation for Fundamental Research (BRFFR) requires grant recipients to make the results of their work publicly available by publishing them in scientific journals and to include a mandatory acknowledgement of the BRFFR grant. In addition, the shared research infrastructure, including industry laboratories and centres for the collective use of unique scientific equipment, facilitates R&D, pilot testing and the implementation of innovative projects.   

 

Public-private partnerships are fostered through the State Programme for Innovative Development, the Belarusian Innovation Fund and the involvement of private-sector representatives in public advisory councils and working groups for national planning.

 

No national monitoring framework for open science is reported.

National and institutional open science infrastructures consist principally of a network of electronic libraries and repositories maintained by higher education institutions and NAS of Belarus. University-based repositories include those of the Belarusian State University of Informatics and Radioelectronics (BSUIR), the Belarusian National Technical University (BNTU) and Belarusian State University, among others. NAS of Belarus maintains an electronic archive of scientific periodicals. The Republican Scientific and Technical Library (RSTL) is a national depository for domestic and foreign literature on engineering, technology and industrial economics, and a state repository of patent documentation and technical regulatory documents.

 

Belarus does not report hosting or funding any federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science. Existing open science infrastructures at the international level are reported as not accessible to relevant actors in the country.

 

International collaborations to optimise infrastructure use include cooperation within the Union State of Belarus and Russia, the Eurasian Economic Union (EAEU) and the Commonwealth of Independent States (CIS), alongside developing partnerships with Asian countries.

According to the report, systematic capacity building on open science in higher education and research institutions takes several forms, including electronic libraries at universities, technology parks such as the Scientific and Technological Park "Polytechnic" of the Belarusian National Technical University (BNTU) and student science and technology laboratories. The National Centre of Intellectual Property conducts courses on intellectual property literacy. A National Children's Technopark also operates.

 

The report does not describe capacity building programmes for policymakers on integrating open science values into STI policies.

 

Open science competencies have been incorporated into higher education curricula. Bachelor's degree programmes include the disciplines "Fundamentals of Intellectual Property Management" and "Methodology of Scientific Research". However, the report does not clarify whether open science is explicitly integrated into these courses.

 

The report states that open educational resources as defined in the 2019 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Educational Resources are not used as an instrument for open science capacity building.

Regarding initiatives to support science communication, the report highlights international scientific cooperation, noting that in 2024 Belarusian scientists implemented over 90 joint projects with partners from seven countries, including Azerbaijan, Armenia, India, China, Moldova, Serbia and Uzbekistan. It also refers to the Strategy for Scientific and Technological Development of the Union State of Belarus and Russia to 2035, which promotes a unified, shared scientific and educational space with openness as a key principle.

Regarding initiatives to review research assessment and evaluation, the report cites the Methodological Recommendations for Assessing the Effectiveness of Scientific, Scientific-Technical and Innovative Developments, approved by the State Committee on Science and Technology (SCST) in 2017. These recommendations are used in planning and evaluating state scientific research programmes, scientific and technical programmes and innovative projects. However, the report does not clarify whether open science is explicitly integrated into these processes.

 

In terms of the integration of open science into career evaluation and progression, the report refers to the Database of Gifted Youth, a centralised system for recording information about high-achieving students who receive benefits such as priority in job placement, mentorship, internships and advanced training, and also mentions financial incentives for researchers with academic degrees under Presidential Decree No. 450 (2007). However, the report does not indicate that open science-related criteria are explicitly integrated into these systems.

 

No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.
The information provided in the report does not directly address the questions covered by this section.

Regarding a strategy for cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science, Belarus reports cooperation within the Union State of Belarus and Russia, the Eurasian Economic Union (EAEU) and the Commonwealth of Independent States (CIS), alongside developing partnerships with Asian countries.

 

Belarus reports involvement in regional funding mechanisms for open science through its participation in programmes financed from the Union State budget. Examples include the Union programmes "SG Complex", which concerns multi-satellite constellations for Earth observation; "Intelauto", focused on on-board electronics for motor vehicles; and "Component-F", addressing photonic materials and components for engineering, communications and aerospace.

Awareness-raising activities on open science address its elements, benefits and challenges. In 2024, the Swiss National Science Foundation (SNSF) hosted a "Research on Research" event to showcase key relevant national and international projects in the field and facilitate

community building in Switzerland. That year's Open Science Days, sponsored by swissuniversities and the SNSF, brought together the University of Lausanne, the University of Applied Sciences and Arts Western Switzerland (HES-SO), the University of Teacher Education Vaud (HEP Vaud) and the Swiss Federal Institute of Technology Lausanne (EPFL) for a week-long programme covering open access (OA), open research data (ORD), open educational resources (OER), citizen science and the impact of artificial intelligence on research and education. A January 2025 event by swissuniversities reviewed the first phase (2021–2024) of the Open Science Programme, which implements the national strategies for OA and ORD. The Annual Symposium of the Swiss European Research Infrastructure Consortium (ERIC) community, organised by the State Secretariat for Education, Research and Innovation (SERI) for April 2025, explored research infrastructures as enablers of open science. Within the ETH Domain, which groups several Swiss federal universities and research institutions focused on science, technology, engineering and related fields, the Measure 1 Workshop in June 2025 presented outcomes of funded ORD projects.

Switzerland does not have a single science, technology and innovation (STI) policy. The main federal instrument is the Federal Act on the Promotion of Research and Innovation (RIPA), operationalised through multi-year Education, Research and Innovation (ERI) Dispatches. According to the report, this framework addresses such elements as open access to scientific knowledge and open science infrastructure.

 Two national policies are specifically dedicated to open science. The Swiss National OA Strategy, adopted in 2017 and revised in May 2024, covers publishing transformation, infrastructure and monitoring, with the vision of making all publicly funded scholarly publications

openly accessible. The Swiss National ORD Strategy, adopted in 2021, applies the Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable (FAIR) principles to publicly financed research data. Both were developed by swissuniversities and the SNSF, entrusted by SERI. The ORD Action Plan, adopted in 2022, concretises the measures of the National ORD Strategy, its four action areas are coordinated by the Swiss National ORD Strategy Council.


 Switzerland has established two specific policy instruments functioning as national funding mechanisms for open science. The swissuniversities Open Science Programme II (2025–2026, CHF 6.45 million) supports OA, ORD and knowledge transfer. The ETH Domain ORD Program (CHF 15 million) defines five measures including field-specific ORD actions, research data management (RDM) training, legal aspects and career paths. The SNSF additionally funds OA publishing of articles, books and book chapters.

 Open science practices have been integrated into existing research funding: most Swiss research funding instruments include OA and ORD requirements.

 Several institutions have adopted open science policies. The University of Zurich introduced an open science policy in 2021 covering public access to resources, research methods and cultural change in assessment. EPFL and the Swiss Federal Institute of Technology Zurich (ETHZ) adopted OA policies in 2019 and 2018, requiring deposit in institutional repositories.

 Equitable public-private partnerships on open science have not, according to the report, been structured into a coherent national framework.

 A national monitoring framework for open science is under development. The Swiss Open Access Monitor, developed through the National Open Access Monitoring project, tracks OA progress across journals and repositories. The report notes no corresponding national framework for ORD, though the SNSF monitors uptake across its funding schemes.

Switzerland operates a diverse array of open science infrastructures, though the report notes that no comprehensive overview exists given their quantity and diversity. Most institutions maintain their repositories, which vary in research outputs coverage and metadata completeness.

 For example, federated information technology infrastructure includes Zenodo, a non-commercial repository for data, software and research artefacts operated by the European Organization for Nuclear Research (CERN), and the Worldwide LHC (Large Hadron Collider) Computing Grid, connecting over 170 centres in 42 countries. SWITCH, the national research and education network, provides cloud computing through SWITCH Engines, data storage through SWITCH Drive and a federated digital identity through SWITCH edu-ID. The Swiss National Supercomputing Centre (CSCS) supports simulations and complex data analysis.

 Domain-specific and community-managed infrastructures include the Swiss National Data and Service Center for the Humanities (DaSCH), a national SNSF-funded open data infrastructure for the humanities field applying the FAIR principles, and Materials Cloud, operated by the National Centre of Competence in Research MARVEL (Materials' Revolution: Computational Design and Discovery of Novel Materials) and hosted at the CSCS, which provides open-access computational tools and datasets for materials science. Citizen Science Zurich, hosted by the University of Zurich, facilitates co-creation of knowledge between researchers and the public. These infrastructures are non-commercial.

 Switzerland hosts and funds federated regional and international infrastructures. It participates in six ERICs, including the DARIAH (Digital Research Infrastructure for the Arts and Humanities), and holds observer status in four more, including the CLARIN (Common Language Resources and Technology Infrastructure).

 North-South collaborations include the CSCS providing data infrastructure for the Square Kilometre Array Observatory, with arrays in South Africa and Australia. The Swissuniversities Development and Cooperation Network (SUDAC) supports collaboration between Swiss higher education institutions and Global South partners. The Excellence in Africa programme, launched by EPFL in 2019, provides African science, technology, engineering and mathematics students with digital education and remote experimentation resources.

Systematic capacity building on open science in higher education takes both formal and informal forms. The swissuniversities Open Science Programme II funds the training of ORD specialists and data stewards across Swiss universities. The ETH Domain ORD Program builds ORD knowledge and skills through its five defined measures. National initiatives have also supported networks for data stewards and research software engineers, and the Swiss Library Network for Education and Research has built open science capacity within libraries.

 The report states that capacity building programmes for policymakers on integrating open science values into STI policies require further development, citing competing priorities and awareness gaps within policymaking bodies.

 No national framework of open science competencies has been incorporated into higher education curricula. The report notes that individual institutions have integrated aspects such as RDM and OA publishing, primarily at doctoral level.

 OER are used for open science capacity building. The SwissMOOCService, founded by ETHZ and EPFL, provides online courses across disciplines. Switch OER is Switzerland's OER repository for higher education. The ETH Domain ORD Program is collecting ORD training materials as OERs on Zenodo for a massive open online course to be released on SwissMOOCService.

 Science communication aligned with open science values is supported through several channels. For example, the SNSF awards grants for scientific meetings and projects that promote dialogue between science and society. Events such as Pint of Science bring researchers to local venues to share discoveries with the public.

Initiatives to review research assessment and evaluation processes are underway at both national and institutional levels. Nineteen Swiss institutions have signed the Coalition for Advancing Research Assessment (CoARA), including EPFL, ETHZ, the SNSF and swissuniversities. Each signatory is expected to submit an action plan aligning its research evaluation practices with CoARA's principles, of which open science is a component. The Swiss National Chapter of CoARA coordinates research evaluation reform across Switzerland's diverse higher education landscape.

 Specific clauses recognising open science practices have been incorporated into career evaluation systems. From 2022, the SNSF introduced a narrative curriculum vitae format across its funding schemes, allowing researchers to describe contributions to knowledge generation, peer development and broader society beyond publications.

 Initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices include the National Prize for ORD, awarded since 2023 by the Swiss Academies of Arts and Sciences as part of the ORD Action Plan. The 2024 edition focused on collaborative data reuse and on educational and outreach activities related to data reuse. The SNSF provides additional funding incentives for researchers implementing open science principles.

 The report does not indicate any unintended negative consequences of open science practices.

The SNSF mandates open access for all publicly funded publications, supporting dissemination through various channels, including preprints. While the report notes no explicit preprint policies, the reported data indicate growing preprint engagement among SNSF-funded researchers. The ORD Strategy and Action Plan also create conditions for sharing research data aligned with the FAIR principles, encompassing the sharing of negative scientific results.


Citizen Science Zurich, based at the University of Zurich in collaboration with ETHZ, promotes participatory methods by involving diverse participants in research across sectors such as health, energy and environment. Projects have engaged cancer survivors, elderly individuals and indigenous communities, including a collaboration with Wounaan people from Colombia and Panama to explore cultural artefacts at the University of Zurich's Ethnographic Museum.

 Open innovation is advanced through the swissuniversities Open Science Programme II, which promotes knowledge and technology transfer between universities, businesses and society. The first generation of Innosuisse (the Swiss Innovation Agency) Innovation Booster initiatives, launched in 2021 and concluded in 2024, promoted co-creation across innovation hubs. Innosuisse also offers innovation cheques of up to CHF 15,000 to connect small and medium-sized enterprises with research institutions.

Switzerland promotes international scientific collaboration through its highly connected research system. An evaluation prepared for SERI found that, among eight European countries examined between 2007 and 2017, Switzerland had the highest share of collaborative publications every year, reaching 66% in 2017. SERI provides support for international research collaborations.


 The report states that no specific strategy exists for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science. Swiss actors are, however, involved in European and international networks that facilitate the exchange of best practices and capacity building.

 Switzerland is not reported to be involved in specific discussions on the establishment of regional or international funding mechanisms for open science. The report notes, however, that Switzerland participates in Horizon Europe (2021–2027), which contains several mechanisms for open science funding.

Awareness-raising activities have taken multiple forms. Within the Open Science for Ukrainian Higher Education System (Open4UA) project, two seminars on forming a national consensus on open science were held in 2024 (1st seminar, 2nd seminar). A 2024 panel discussion titled “Law and Open Science: The Regulatory Framework of Open Science and its Principles in Legal Research”. Since 2022, the annual international conference “Open Science and Innovation in Ukraine” has been held under the auspices of the Ministry of Education and Science. Its 2022 edition, timed to coincide with International Open Access Week, brought together international experts to discuss the best practices of implementing open science. The 2023 edition focused on access to research infrastructure, reform of research evaluation and Ukraine's integration into the European Research Area. The 2024 edition addressed policy formation, infrastructure provision and the human factors supporting long-term open science development. A fourth edition was planned for October 2025.​

Ukraine's national science, technology and innovation policy partly addresses the four open science elements (open access to scientific knowledge, open science infrastructure, open engagement of societal actors and open dialogue with other knowledge systems) through a range of legislative and strategic instruments, including the Law on Priority Areas of Development of Science and Technology, the Law on Priority Areas of Innovation Activity in Ukraine, the Law on Scientific and Scientific-Technical Activity, a draft law on amendments to the Law on Scientific and Scientific-Technical Activities (under consideration), the Law on Scientific and Technical Information, the Strategic Action Plan of the Ministry of Education and Science to 2027 and the Strategy for Digital Development of Innovative Activity of Ukraine until 2030.

The National Plan for Open Science, adopted in 2022 and aligned with the 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science, serves as both the principal policy instrument and the national open science implementation plan; the Ministry of Education and Science oversees its implementation.

Regarding institutional policies, two EU-funded consortium projects promote open science at institutional level: Open Practices, Transparency and Integrity for Modern Academia (OPTIMA)”, which implements open science practices in higher education, including capacity building and support for disadvantaged academic communities; and Open Science for Ukrainian Higher Education System (Open4UA), which supports national and institutional reform by fostering open science policies, advancing research assessment practices and contributing to higher education system modernisation and EU integration. In 2024, Sumy State University has adopted an open science policy.

The report indicates that no specific national funding mechanism for open science is currently in place, as budgetary financing options are constrained by the ongoing war. Nevertheless, it notes that open science practices have been integrated into existing research funding mechanisms.

No efforts to foster equitable public-private partnerships on open science are reported.

The Ministry of Education and Science annually monitors implementation of the National Plan for Open Science and reports to the Cabinet of Ministers by 20 January of each year.

National open science infrastructures are reported as under development, encompassing community-managed infrastructures, platforms for exchange and co-creation of knowledge between scientists and society, and community-based monitoring and information systems. For example, the National Repository of Academic Texts is a nationwide distributed electronic database in which academic texts are accumulated, stored and systematised.  A national data repository is planned for creation in 2025 at the Ukrainian Institute of Scientific and Technical Expertise and Information. The National Academy of Sciences of Ukraine also operates an open science portal.

 

Ukraine does not report involvement in North-South, North-South-South or South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use or develop joint strategies for shared open science platforms.

Systematic capacity building on open science takes place at several higher education institutions. For example, the Scientific and Technical Library of the National Technical University of Ukraine regularly holds educational events on various aspects of open science. It has developed recommendations for research data management plans and instructions for posting research data in Zenodo. The library has also delivered webinars on research data management and a course of open lectures titled “Must-have for research activities”.

 

No capacity building programmes for policymakers on integrating open science values into science, technology and innovation policies are reported.

 

The report states that a framework of open science competencies has not been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula, owing to low awareness of open science and wartime restrictions. The Ministry of Education and Science has, however, developed methodological recommendations on scientific data management for higher education institutions and scientific institutions.

 

Open educational resources are used for open science capacity building. No initiatives to support science communication in line with open science values are reported, attributed to the war and low awareness of open science principles outside the circle of open science practitioners.

No initiatives to formally review research assessment and evaluation processes to align them with open science principles are reported at either national or institutional level; the Ministry of Education and Science states it did not receive proposals in this area. The report also indicates that specific clauses recognising open science practices have not been yet incorporated into career evaluation and progression systems. However, it also points to emerging developments in 2024, including the introduction of a new methodology for the state assessment of research institutions and higher education institutions, which incorporates indicators for open access publications, preprints and research datasets; in addition, the National Research Foundation of Ukraine has developed an action plan supporting the Coalition for Advancing Research Assessment (CoARA), and competitive research project selection by the Ministry of Education and Science also takes open science indicators into account.

 

No initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices are reported; the report attributes this to the lack of open science infrastructure, particularly data repositories.

 

No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.

The report states that no initiatives promoting innovative, participatory methodological or procedural changes at different stages of the research cycle exist at either national or institutional level. The absence is attributed to the current low level of implementation of open science practices.

International scientific collaborations on open science are not promoted or facilitated at the national level, according to the report, owing to a lack of available resources during the ongoing war. The Ministry of Education and Science does, however, actively participate in grant programmes that advance open science, particularly through the Horizon Europe programme.

 

No strategy or plan for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science is reported.

 

Ukraine reports involvement in discussions on regional and international funding mechanisms for open science through two projects: “Open Science for Ukrainian Higher Education System (Open4UA)” and “Open Practices, Transparency and Integrity for Modern Academia (OPTIMA)”.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted by the former Ministry of Science, Technology and Innovation (now the Secretariat of Innovation, Science and Technology), the National Scientific and Technical Research Council (CONICET) and the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, all of which shared news of its adoption on their webpages. It was also posted on the websites of national universities, including the University of Buenos Aires and the National University of Córdoba, the two largest by student enrolment.

 Awareness-raising activities span several years and involve multiple institutions. The former Ministry organised a series of workshops and conversations on open and citizen science between 2018 and 2019, covering themes such as citizen science, open research data and open hardware. In 2023 it organised the 1st National Meeting on Citizen Science. Universities have also been active: the Argentine Meeting on Open and Citizen Science, launched as a workshop in 2017, has held successive editions. In 2024 the Research Data Alliance hosted an International Conference on Research Data at the National University of Rosario. Several ministries, science and technology institutions and universities participate in international campaigns such as Citizen Science Month and Open Access Week.

Argentina's science, technology and innovation policy addresses open science elements through the National Science, Technology and Innovation Plan 2030, which was enacted as Law 27.738 in October 2023 and emphasises open access and citizen science, particularly within its “Citizenship and Scientific Culture” section. Law 26.899 of Open Access Institutional Digital Repositories (2013), implemented by Resolution 753-E/2016 of the Ministry of Science, Technology and Innovation, provides the national legal framework for the open access policy. It mandates that publicly funded institutions within the National Science, Technology and Innovation System (SNCTI) develop interoperable open access repositories  and requires that scientific and technological outputs derived from publicly funded research be deposited in them. The law also requires policies to ensure public access to primary research data, as well as the long-term management and preservation of these resources.


 Several policy instruments promote open science, including the National System of Digital Repositories (SNRD), created in 2011 to build an interoperable repository network, and the National Citizen Science Program, established in 2022 by Ministerial Resolution 620/2022.

 

Regarding a national roadmap, an Advisory Committee on Open and Citizen Science set up in 2021 by Ministerial Resolution 132/2021 issued the Diagnosis and Lineaments for an Open Science Policy in 2022, providing a baseline for a forthcoming National Open Science Strategy.

 At institutional level, 18 SNCTI institutions have implemented open access policies under Law 26.899, 13 hold draft versions and 81 operate at least one digital repository. Several universities have incorporated open science, mainly in connection to open access, in their strategic plans, including the National University of La Plata.

 National funding mechanisms for open science include the National Citizen Science Program and the SNRD, which fund citizen science projects and repository creation. Open science practices have been integrated in research funding: the Under Secretariat of Science and Technology (SSCYT) and the National Agency for the Promotion of Research, Technological Development and Innovation (Agencia I+D+i) require compliance with the Open Access Law 26.899 in their project calls.

 The report indicates that CONICET is developing efforts to promote equitable public-private partnerships on open science, though no document has yet been published.

 A national monitoring framework for open science is under development. The National Directorate of Information and Studies is creating an Open Science Monitor, beginning with a monitor for Law 26.899 compliance. A first National Mapping of Citizen Science Initiatives was conducted in 2023.

National open science infrastructures are reported as under development. High-performance computing resources include Clementina XXI, a supercomputer at the National Meteorological Service available to the entire science and technology community; access to its large-scale computing capacity is provided through the Accelerated Computing Projects Initiative (IPAC), which provides competitive open access to these resources. The National System of High Performance Computing coordinates 28 affiliated centres, each committing 20% of computing time to shared access. Over 600 items of large-size equipment enrolled in the National Systems programme are available to the scientific community.

 The SNRD comprises 79 digital repositories, 52 of which are harvested in its portal using the Open Archives Initiative Protocol for Metadata Harvesting (OAI-PMH). Open data services include the Open Access Primary Data Portal for Science and Technology in Argentina (DACyTAr) and datasets from institutions such as the National Meteorological Service and the CONICET Research Data Repository. Public engagement infrastructure includes the Cultural Science Centre (C3), which facilitates access to scientific culture, and TECNÓPOLIS, a large-scale science, technology, industry and art exhibition; both are free of charge. Several citizen science projects are supported by computer platforms that facilitate collaboration between scientists and society.

 Argentina hosts and funds a federated regional infrastructure through the Latin American Network for Open Science (LA Referencia), established by a cooperation agreement signed in 2012 to support national open access strategies in Latin America and Spain. The SNRD is one of its 12 national nodes. LA Referencia also constitutes a South-South collaboration for shared open science platforms.

Systematic capacity building on open science has taken place in Argentine higher education and research institutions. Between 2021 and 2024, approximately 30 activities related to open science were organised by over 20 institutions, mainly universities.

 The report indicates that no capacity building programmes for policymakers on integrating open science values into science, technology and innovation policies have taken place. Such training is expected once a National Open Science Policy has been developed.

 Open science competencies have been incorporated into higher education curricula through individual institutional initiatives rather than central planning. Library science programmes at several national universities include such elements, among them the University of Buenos Aires, the National University of La Plata and the National University of Córdoba.

 Initiatives to support science communication in line with open science values include Science Culture Projects, funded under the National Science Popularization Program, which demonstrate the social value of science through associative and inter-institutional efforts. “Scientists go to School” promotes collaborative work between teachers and the scientific community. The annual Argentine Science and Technology Week hosts simultaneous public outreach activities across the country. The Cultural Science Centre (C3) engages audiences in scientific projects through its “At Open Science” initiative, while the National University of Córdoba produced the Open Science in Pandemic Times and Citizen Science Dissemination podcasts.

Initiatives to review research assessment and evaluation processes include the National Science, Technology and Innovation Plan 2030's Agenda of Institutional Change, which envisions designing an evaluation model with indicators that recognise open access science and technology outputs. CONICET has incorporated open science elements into its evaluation criteria across all knowledge areas and formally adhered to the Declaration on Research Assessment (DORA) in 2022. It is working to embed these principles in assessment stages covering entry, continuity, promotion, funding and grants.

 Regarding specific clauses recognising open science practices in career evaluation and progression systems, the report indicates that a forthcoming National Open Science Policy is expected to address this area, with CONICET working along similar lines.

 Initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices include the national data visualisation competition “Contar con Datos”, hosted by the SSCYT and the Data Science Lab of the University of San Andrés, which promotes the use of scientific data visualisation and public communication of science. The Open Institutional Repository (Repositorio Institucional Abierto, RIA) Awards at the National Technological University recognise the most visited works, most searched authors and authors with the largest number of deposits.

 No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.

At both national and institutional levels, Argentina reports initiatives promoting innovative and participatory approaches at different stages of the research cycle. Regarding the promotion of preprints, Article 5 of Law 26.899 requires publicly funded researchers to deposit a copy of the final version of their scientific and technological production in their institution's open access digital repository within six months of official publication or approval.

 Participatory strategies addressing the needs of marginalised communities have been developed under climate policy. As part of the National Climate Change Adaptation and Mitigation Plan, the former Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development conducted four regional workshops with indigenous community leaders, called Intercultural Dialogues, to identify climate change risks and impacts from an indigenous worldview. Indigenous peoples were also consulted for the design of the National Action Plan for Forests and Climate Change.

 Open innovation is promoted through training programmes, including the Ibero-American Diploma in Open Innovation offered by the Secretariat of Knowledge Economy and the National Technological University, and a virtual course on Open Innovation led by the Ministry of Productive Development. The National Citizen Science Program supports participatory methods that value inputs from societal actors.

Argentina promotes international scientific collaboration on open science through several channels. Through its membership in LA Referencia, it is also part of the Confederation of Open Access Repositories (COAR). In 2023 LA Referencia signed a cooperation agreement with RedCLARA (Latin American Cooperation of Advanced Networks) and Latindex (Regional Cooperative Online Information System for Scholarly Journals from Latin America, the Caribbean, Spain and Portugal) to consolidate a Regional Open Science Ecosystem. LA Referencia also signed a memorandum of understanding in 2021 with the Arab States Research and Education Network (ASREN), the West and Central African Research and Education Network (WACREN) and UbuntuNet Alliance to promote open science activities and the potential adoption of its open-source discovery software in Africa.

 Bilateral and multilateral engagements complement these regional ties. Under Blue Pampa (Pampa Azul), an inter-ministerial initiative, Argentina participates in CoastCarb, a project with over 100 experts from 10 countries creating an open access data portal for coastal ecosystems. CONICET and the University of Antioquia of Colombia jointly studied the status of open access and article processing charges, releasing their report in 2022. An Open Science Group established under Mercosur's Special Meeting on Science and Technology (RECyT) at the end of 2022 is working on a Recommendation on Open Science.

 The report does not describe a formal strategy or plan for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science.

 Argentina is involved in discussions on regional and international funding mechanisms for open science. Within Mercosur-RECyT, the Open Science Group has been discussing the creation of funding mechanisms for regional multi-centre projects and regional instruments to fund open science initiatives.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted through the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology (MEXT), the Cabinet Office (CAO) and the Ministry of Foreign Affairs MoFA). According to the report, awareness-raising activities on open science were undertaken in relation to the Sixth Basic Plan for Science, Technology and Innovation adopted by Cabinet decision in 2021.

Japan's science, technology and innovation policy, as set out in the Sixth Basic Plan for Science, Technology and Innovation, includes open science elements, such as open access to scientific knowledge and open science infrastructure.

 

In addition, two national policies adopted by the Cabinet Office's Integrated Innovation Strategy Promotion Council address open access to scientific knowledge and open science infrastructure. The Principle on Management and Utilization of Publicly Funded Research Data (April 2021) outlines a national approach to research data management balancing openness with national interests. The Basic Policy on Promoting Open Access to Publicly Funded Scholarly Publications and Scientific Data (February 2024) establishes a national policy mandating the immediate open access of such outputs. These two policies also constitute Japan's national roadmap for open science implementation.

 

Two specific policy instruments also function as national funding mechanisms for open science. The Open Access Acceleration Project, managed by MEXT with a budget of 10 billion yen (2023), provides subsidies to universities preparing for mandatory immediate open access to scholarly publications and scientific data from fiscal year 2025. MEXT also funds the Project for Developing a Research Data Ecosystem for the Promotion of Data-Driven Science (approximately 1 billion yen per year, 2022–2026), which supports the National Institute of Informatics (NII) in developing nationwide infrastructure for data sharing and utilisation.

 

At the institutional level, the report notes that universities and research institutions have established their own research data and open access policies, guided by the 2021 Recommendation and the above-mentioned national policies, but provides no further details.

 

The report indicates that open science practices have not yet been integrated into existing research funding mechanisms. No efforts to develop equitable public-private partnerships on open science are reported.

 

A national monitoring framework operates under the Integrated Innovation Strategy, using indicators such as the number of institutions with research data policies and the number of institutional repositories established.

Japan's national open science infrastructure includes the NII Research Data Cloud (NII RDC), a non-commercial platform supporting open science, research integrity and data-driven research. It comprises three components: GakuNin RDM for research data management, WEKO3 for publishing research outputs in a repository and CiNii Research for the discovery of academic information.

 

The Scientific Research Digital Platform, established in 2022, is an infrastructure combining Science Information NETwork 6 (SINET6), the national ultra-high-speed network and the NII RDC that promotes the utilisation, distribution and management of research data. Primarily aimed at domestic researchers, it also supports international initiatives in areas such as high-energy physics, nuclear fusion and asteroid probe operations while also promoting interdisciplinary collaboration.

 

Japan does not report involvement in North-South or North-South-South  collaborations to optimise infrastructure use or develop joint strategies for shared open science platforms.

Two public initiatives provide systematic capacity building on open science. Under the Project for Developing a Research Data Ecosystem for the Promotion of Data-Driven Science, the University of Osaka leads efforts to define standard skills for research data management personnel, develop training curricula and prepare online learning courses. NII also offers online courses on research data management for researchers and research assistants through its GakuNin Learning Management System (GakuNin LMS).

 

The report does not describe capacity building programmes for policymakers on integrating open science values and principles into science, technology and innovation policies. And a framework of open science competencies has not yet been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula, according to the report.

 

The report confirms the use of open educational resources for open science capacity building but provides no further detail.

 

On science communication, the Scholarly Publishing and Academic Resources Coalition Japan (SPARC Japan) conducts advocacy activities related to open access and open science and collaborates with international initiatives related to open access.

The report does not describe any initiatives to review research assessment and evaluation processes to align them with open science principles. No specific clauses recognising open science practices have been incorporated into career evaluation and progression systems, according to the report.

 

No initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices are reported.

 

No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.

Japan promotes innovative approaches to increase the openness of the scientific process at both national and institutional levels, with a focus on preprints and open innovation. Notably, Jxiv, operated by the Japan Science and Technology Agency (JST), is a national preprint server that enables posting across all research fields in Japanese and English. Preprints published on Jxiv are assigned a Digital Object Identifier (DOI) and released as open access content under a Creative Commons licence.

Japan promotes international scientific collaboration on open science through multilateral engagement. Through the 2023 G7 Leaders' Communiqué and G7 Science and Technology Ministers' Communiqué, participating countries confirmed a shared direction on open science and committed to cooperation. As co-chair of the Open Science Working Group (OSWG) under the G7 Science and Technology Ministers' meeting, Japan presents discussion points on inter-system federation with enhanced interoperability and evaluation indicators that will become international standards. The government is also considering support for a project on human resource development related to open science in the Asia-Pacific region, to be carried out by the UNESCO Jakarta office using the Japan Fund-in-Trust to UNESCO.

 

The report states that no formal strategy exists for cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science. It also indicates that Japan is not involved in discussions on the establishment of regional or international funding mechanisms for open science.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted and shared with the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, the Ministry of Science and Higher Education and the Russian Academy of Sciences.

 The report indicates that no awareness-raising activities on open science have been organised or are foreseen by the end of 2025.

The Scientific and Technological Development Strategy of the Russian Federation (official text in Russian), adopted in 2016 and last updated in 2024, sets out the national science, technology and innovation policy and includes elements related to open science.

 The Concept of International Scientific and Technical Cooperation of the Russian Federation, adopted in 2019 by the Presidential Council for Science and Education, establishes that international scientific and technical cooperation operates on the principle of openness. This encompasses freedom of scientific and technological communication and free, equal and non-discriminatory access to scientific information, research results, scientific infrastructure and competencies, subject to reasonable measures for intellectual property protection. An update to the Concept is planned for 2025.

 The report does not describe specific policy instruments on open science, a national implementation plan or roadmap for open science or institutional-level open science policies.

 According to the report, no specific national funding mechanisms for open science exist, nor have open science practices been integrated into existing research funding mechanisms.

 The report indicates no efforts to foster equitable public-private partnerships on open science and no national monitoring framework for open science.

National open science infrastructures include megascience class scientific facilities, categorised in the report as federated information technology infrastructure. As of 2023, eight such projects existed, created pursuant to the Executive Order on National Goals and Strategic Objectives of the Russian Federation through to 2024. Among them, for example, are the international centre for neutron research based on the high-flux research reactor PIK, the Kurchatov specialised synchrotron radiation source and the Siberian ring photon source.

 As a federated international information technology infrastructure, the Russian Federation hosts the Joint Institute for Nuclear Research (JINR), an intergovernmental organisation established in 1956. JINR conducts research in particle physics, nuclear physics and condensed matter physics, maintaining laboratories, experimental facilities and a high-performance computing environment. It also produces open access publications. Participation is open through membership or bilateral and multilateral agreements, and international organisations may be granted observer status.

 The report does not indicate involvement in North-South, North-South-South or South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use or joint strategies for open science platforms.

The report does not indicate that systematic capacity building on open science has taken place in higher education or research-performing institutions. Nor does it describe capacity-building programmes for policymakers on integrating open science values into science, technology and innovation policies.

 A framework of open science competencies has not been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula according to the report. The report explains that the development and approval of educational programmes fall within the competence of individual educational organisations, which design them in accordance with federal state educational standards.

 Open educational resources are reported as partly used for open science capacity building.

 In the area of science communication, the Decade of Science and Technology in the Russian Federation, declared by the President in 2022, aims to attract young people to research, involve researchers in addressing nationally significant issues and increase public access to information about Russian science. In 2024, over 12,000 federal and regional events took place under its framework, engaging more than 43 million participants. Its initiatives include "Science is Nearby", "Popular Science Tourism", the "Scientific Children's Playgrounds" project and the Congress of Young Scientists. The Ministry of Science and Higher Education has further organised grant competitions supporting authors who cover science and technology and organisers of popular science events.

The report does not describe initiatives at the national or institutional level to review research assessment and evaluation processes to align them with open science principles, nor are such reviews foreseen by the end of 2025. Specific clauses recognising open science practices have likewise not been incorporated into career evaluation and progression systems according to the report.

 No initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices are reported.

 

No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.

The report indicates the absence of initiatives at the national or institutional level to promote innovative, participatory methodological or procedural changes at different stages of the research cycle to increase openness in the scientific process.

The report notes that there is no strategy or plan for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science. It also indicates that the country is not involved in discussions on establishing regional or international funding mechanisms for open science.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted through working groups convened to formulate the National Policy on Open Science and Strategic Research. These groups brought together delegates from across the National System of Science, Technology, Innovation and Ancestral Knowledge, spanning government bodies, higher education institutions, public research institutes and civil society. Government participants included the Secretariat of Higher Education, Science, Technology and Innovation (SENESCYT), the Open Government Directorate of the Presidency and the National Intellectual Rights Service (SENADI), alongside ministries responsible for the environment, public health and education. Higher education regulatory bodies such as the Council for the Assurance of the Quality of Higher Education (CACES) and the Higher Education Council (CES) contributed alongside the Openlab Ecuador Foundation, the Ecuadorian Corporation for the Development of Research and Academia (CEDIA) and public research institutes including the National Institute of Agricultural Research (INIAP). Co-creation roundtables held in Quito, Guayaquil and Cuenca drew teachers, researchers, university authorities, scientific societies and knowledge networks such as the Ecuadorian Researchers Network (Red de Investigadores Ecuatorianos, REDI).

 Awareness-raising activities on open science are foreseen rather than already completed. Following the expected approval of the National Policy on Open Science and Strategic Research, a dissemination process will target all actors in the National System of Science, Technology, Innovation and Ancestral Knowledge, aiming to raise awareness about open science, its benefits and its challenges.

Ecuador's national science, technology and innovation policy is the National Plan for the Social Economy of Knowledge, Creativity, Innovation and Ancestral Knowledge, adopted in 2023 under SENESCYT's authority. It addresses open science infrastructure and open dialogue with other knowledge systems as open science elements.

 A National Policy on Open Science and Strategic Research is under development as a commitment under the Second Open Government Action Plan (Plan de Acción de Gobierno Abierto, PAGA), led by the Open Government Directorate of the Presidency. The policy is projected for approval in the second half of 2025 following review by CACES, CES and the Higher Education System Assembly (Asamblea del Sistema de Educación Superior, ASESEC). No institutional-level open science policies are reported; the forthcoming national policy is intended to affect all actors across the National System of Science, Technology, Innovation and Ancestral Knowledge and the Higher Education System.

 No specific national funding mechanisms for open science are reported. The report also states that budget allocation by entities has not to date integrated open science practices into research funding. The forthcoming national policy aims to develop funding mechanisms incorporating such practices.

 Regarding equitable public-private partnerships, SENESCYT has been a founding member and political authority of LA Referencia, a federated Latin American network of institutional repositories, since 2010. Since 2019, a technical-scientific cooperation agreement between SENESCYT and CEDIA, renewed every two years, has supported LA Referencia's operational management and produced national initiatives including a network of digital repositories, a metadata application project and training in repository standardisation.

 The report states that no national monitoring framework for open science exists.

Several national open science infrastructures operate in Ecuador. The national open data portal, operated by the Ministry of Telecommunications and the Information Society (Ministerio de Telecomunicaciones y de la Sociedad de la Información, MINTEL), shares research data with citizens. SciELO Ecuador offers open access to Ecuadorian scientific journals as part of the SciELO network, which promotes free access to scientific production in Latin America, the Caribbean, Spain and Portugal. The Open Access Repository Network of Ecuador (Red de Repositorios de Acceso Abierto del Ecuador, RRAAE) manages open access academic and scientific content from the country's research community, functioning as the national node of the Federal Network of Institutional Repositories of Scientific Publications of Latin America (LA Referencia). Since 2011, CES has required universities to maintain institutional repositories for theses and degree projects, a mandate reinforced by the Organic Law of Higher Education (Ley Orgánica de Educación Superior, LOES) of 2010.

The report describes all these infrastructures as compliant with Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable (FAIR) and Collective Benefit, Authority to Control, Responsibility and Ethics (CARE) principles.

 Ecuador hosts a federated regional information technology infrastructure for open science through SciELO Ecuador, which has been part of the international SciELO network since 2015.

 Regarding North-South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use, Ecuador has participated in Latindex (Regional Online Information System for Scientific Journals from Latin America, the Caribbean, Spain and Portugal) since 2001. SciELO Ecuador's membership of the wider SciELO network also supports cross-border collaboration.

Capacity building on open science in higher education and research settings has been driven by civil society initiatives. The Openlab Ecuador Foundation has organised the Discovering Open Science Conference (Jornadas Descubriendo la Ciencia Abierta), an event that began in Ecuador in 2020 and became a regional conference with support from the Ibero-American open science community. Four editions had been held by 2024, taking place at several Ecuadorian universities. In September 2024, the LATAM Open Science Forum was held in Quito, bringing together academics, researchers, students and activists to discuss open science challenges in Latin America.

 Capacity building programmes for policymakers have taken the form of webinar training delivered in 2023 and 2024 by SENESCYT's strategic partners, covering recommendations for open science public policy in Ecuador and research data management.

 The report indicates that no framework of open science competencies has been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula.

The report indicates that open educational resources are not used as an instrument for open science capacity building.

 The report does not describe science communication initiatives aligned with open science values. The forthcoming National Policy on Open Science and Strategic Research will propose guidelines in this area.

The report states that no specific clauses recognising open science practices have been incorporated in career evaluation and progression systems. Similarly, no initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices are reported. This is attributed to open science being a relatively new approach whose knowledge and application are still being socialised.

 

However, there is an initiative to review overall research assessment and evaluation in the country. CACES and CES participated in the working groups convened to formulate the National Policy on Open Science and Strategic Research, where they agreed to incorporate criteria recognising and positively valuing the adoption of open science principles, values and practices in the evaluation of the quality of scientific research, as well as of teaching, research staff and higher education institutions. These changes are linked to the policy's projected approval in the second half of 2025.

 No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.

Ecuador reports initiatives at both national and institutional levels that promote innovative, participatory changes at different stages of the research cycle. The report identifies several areas being addressed: participatory methods that value inputs from societal actors, the sharing of negative scientific results, participatory strategies for identifying the needs of marginalised communities, and open innovation practices.

The specific examples provided concern citizen science and participatory engagement in biodiversity research. The Wildlife Conservation Society (WCS) Ecuador, in collaboration with the National Institute of Biodiversity (Instituto Nacional de Biodiversidad, INABIO), has promoted citizen participation through workshops training students and the general public in species registration using tools such as iNaturalist and Ictio. The Darwin200 project, a global initiative to inspire young leaders in environmental conservation, collaborated with San Francisco de Quito University (Universidad San Francisco de Quito, USFQ) and the Galapagos Science Center on research and species observations in the Galapagos Islands, involving both the academic and local community. The Sustainable Environmental Investment Fund (Fondo de Inversión Ambiental Sostenible, FIAS) has delivered citizen science training in educational institutions, teaching students and educators to record and share biodiversity observations through platforms such as iNaturalist.

Ecuador promotes international scientific collaboration on open science through multiple channels. The report states that Ecuadorian universities and research centres have participated in the EU's Horizon Europe and that cooperation agreements have been signed with international organisations such as UNESCO and the Economic Commission for Latin America and the Caribbean (ECLAC) to strengthen open science and knowledge transfer.

 Ecuador has established partnership with AmeliCA (Open Knowledge for Latin America and the Global South), a cooperative non-commercial infrastructure for scholarly communication, to improve scientific visibility and strengthen research capacity for adoption of open science practices. Events such as the Discovering Open Science Conference, organised by the Openlab Ecuador Foundation, and the LATAM Open Science Forum, co-organised by the Foundation and CEDIA, have facilitated regional exchange of best practices and the formation of strategic alliances.

 Ecuador reports involvement in discussions on regional and international funding mechanisms for open science. For example, the LATAM Open Science Forum held in Quito in September 2024 included discussions on creating regional funding mechanisms to facilitate collaboration and knowledge sharing between countries in Latin America.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted through the Ministry of Education and Culture, the Research Council of Finland, Business Finland and the Secretariat for the National Open Science and Research Coordination within the Federation of Finnish Learned Societies (TSV), a national cooperative body for 295 learned societies.

 Awareness-raising activities have been organised by national authorities and entities. In 2018, the Ministry of Education and Culture awarded funding to TSV to coordinate and promote open science. Since then, the National Open Science and Research Coordination, supported by its Secretariat within TSV and financed by the Ministry, has actively promoted open science. In addition, the Research Council of Finland has long-established practices in promoting open and responsible science.

The report notes that Finland's Multiannual Plan for Government Research and Development Funding, which forms its national science, technology and innovation strategy, does not include open science elements.

 The Declaration of Open Science and Research, developed by the Finnish research community, serves as the principal national policy framework and roadmap. Four thematic policies support it, including Policy for Open Scholarship, Policy for Open Access to Scholarly Publications, Policy for Research Data and Methods and Policy for the Open Education and Educational Resources. The research community holds executive responsibility for implementation, while TSV coordinates. Legislative support includes the Act on the Openness of Government Activities (621/1999) and the Act on the Reuse of Publicly Funded Research Data (713/2021), among others.

 

At institutional level, 44 of 47 responding research performing organisations had openly published their open science policy documents in the 2024 national monitoring. Examples include University of Helsinki, Turku UAS and the Natural Resources Institute of Finland.

 Open science has been integrated into research funding through several policy instruments. The Research Council of Finland requires immediate open access to peer-reviewed publications in line with Plan S and expects research data to follow FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable) principles. Its €30 million Finnish Research Infrastructure (FIRI) 2021 call included open science among evaluation criteria of applications.

 Regarding specific national funding mechanisms for open science, the report indicates that a working group under the National Steering Group on Open Science and Research is designing a roadmap with recommendations for key stakeholders to fund open science.

 Regarding equitable public-private partnerships, a national “Open science recommendation and checklist for research, development and innovation activities in collaboration between research organisations and companies” was published in 2022.

 National monitoring was conducted in 2022 and 2024, based on the Monitoring Model for Open Science and Research, with results on the Research.fi portal.

The Finnish national roadmap for research infrastructures (FIRI) 2021-2024 lists 29 national research infrastructures funded by the Research Council of Finland, which expects their services and data to be openly available. CSC – IT Center for Science, a non-commercial company owned by the state (70 per cent) and higher education institutions (30 per cent), operates federated IT infrastructure for open science. Its Fairdata services, developed in accordance with FAIR principles, provide tools for research data storage, description, discovery and open publishing. The Research.fi portal aggregates information on funded projects, research outputs (publications and research data) and researchers from across the Finnish research system.

 

Finland hosts federated international IT infrastructure. LUMI (Large Unified Modern Infrastructure), a pre-exascale supercomputer at CSC's data centre in Kajaani, was procured through the European High-Performance Computing Joint Undertaking (EuroHPC JU) and is hosted by a consortium of eleven countries. The LumiGPT-Vision project, for instance, is developing multilingual multimodal foundation models with all tools and outputs to be made openly available. Researchers outside Europe can access LUMI through partnerships with European research groups.

 

The report does not indicate Finland’s involvement in North-South or North-South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use.

Systematic capacity building in higher education includes a pilot training programme for Data Stewards at Tampere University in 2025, funded by the Ministry of Education and Culture. The programme comprises 15 European Credit Transfer and Accumulation System (ECTS) credits, targets doctoral researchers and data management practitioners, and covers open science and FAIR data. The report describes this training as the first of its kind in Finland, noting a previous lack of qualification-oriented training in data management.
 
Capacity building for policymakers has taken several forms. For example, the working group on Interaction with Decision-makers and Open Administration, operating within the Culture of Open Science Expert Panel, produced the recommendation The Criteria for a Good Knowledge Base (2024), aimed at drafters of legislation and comprising best practices for a knowledge base grounded in principles of responsible research.
 
The report states that a national framework of open science competencies is not applicable, as Finnish universities and universities of applied sciences have statutory autonomy. Nevertheless, 66 per cent of higher education institutions had a policy on open science skills or training in the 2024 national monitoring. Nine of 13 universities and 11 of 23 universities of applied sciences indicated that their curricula include an open science dimension.
 
Open educational resources are used for open science capacity building. In the 2024 national monitoring, 89 per cent of responding higher education institutions had guiding documents on open educational materials, and the same proportion recommended publishing such resources on open platforms.
 
Science communication initiatives include the 2018 recommendations Bold Communication, Responsible Influence by the Committee for Public Information in Finland, which identify science communication as part of an open science culture. Finland's national CoARA chapter, with 19 organisations holding action plans, includes recognition of diverse research contributions such as science communication among its commitments.

Research assessment reform is under way at both national and institutional levels. The national recommendation Good Practice in Researcher Evaluation (2020) identifies open access to research as one evaluation criterion. In the 2024 national monitoring, 58 per cent of responding research performing organisations had a policy enabling open science to be considered in assessments related to recruitment or career development. A Career Assessment Matrix (FIN-CAM), currently under development, incorporates open science among qualitative and quantitative criteria and accounts for differences in career stages, paths and disciplines.

 

Open science practices have been incorporated in career evaluation through the Finnish National Board on Research Integrity (TENK) researcher curriculum vitae template, first developed in 2012 and updated in 2020. Its section on scientific and societal impact includes promoting open science and the responsible distribution of research materials and datasets. In the 2024 national monitoring, 51 per cent of responding research performing organisations had enabled researchers to provide information on merits beyond research outputs using this template, with adoption highest among universities, where it reached 100 per cent.

 

Open science awards have been presented annually since 2019 by the Finnish Open Science Coordination, managed by TSV with funding from the Ministry of Education and Culture. Between 2015 and 2017, the Ministry distributed awards for activities promoting research openness.

 

Unintended negative consequences of open science practices have been reported. Grey area journals exploiting the article processing charge (APC) model prompted action from the Publication Forum (JUFO), a national rating system for publication channels. The Forum downgraded 271 journals from MDPI and Frontiers to level 0 from 2025, identifying business models aimed at maximising publication volume at the expense of editorial quality. A further 60 journals were downgraded based on community feedback on quality assessment defects.

Finland reports initiatives at both national and institutional levels spanning multiple stages of the research cycle. Areas addressed include promotion of preprints, exploration of open peer review, sharing of negative scientific results, citizen science, strategies for marginalised communities and open innovation.

 

Citizen science is embedded in the national Policy for Open Scholarship and supported by a dedicated Recommendation for Citizen Science. In the 2024 national monitoring, 57 per cent of responding research performing organisations had a citizen science policy or one in preparation. Support services exist across organisations, with 62 per cent providing legal services and 43 per cent assisting with planning and implementation of citizen science projects.

 

Open innovation practices have been promoted through the National Roadmap for Research, Development and Innovation (2020–2023), which included actions to open public-sector data resources for research and innovation. Innokylä, a national open innovation network in health and social services coordinated by the Finnish Institute for Health and Welfare (THL) and the Finnish Federation for Social Affairs and Health (SOSTE), has over 700 members and more than 4,000 openly available operating models.

Finland promotes international scientific collaboration on open science through participation in the European Open Science Cloud (EOSC), the Research Data Alliance (RDA), the International Science Council Committee on Data (CODATA) and the European Strategy Forum on Research Infrastructures (ESFRI). Finland is connecting its Fairdata services to the EOSC and preparing a national EOSC node. The country also participates in several European Digital Infrastructure Consortia (EDICs), including those for genomics, mobility and logistics data, and connected public administration.

 

Cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration is framed by the Declaration of Open Science and Research, whose vision positions the Finnish research community as an international forerunner. Supporting policies operationalise this goal: the Policy for Open Scholarship calls on organisations to utilise international cooperation, while the Policy on Research Data and Methods directs the community to participate actively in relevant European and international forums. The national open science monitoring model provides data for international comparisons, particularly within Europe.

 

Finland participates in discussions on regional and international funding mechanisms through the Finnish Research Infrastructure Committee, which published the Strategy for National Research Infrastructures in Finland 2020–2030. The strategy promotes the development of national nodes for international research infrastructures and provides the basis for related roadmaps and funding calls.

The report states that the 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has not yet been promoted to or shared with relevant ministries and institutions. The Danish Agency for Higher Education and Science plans to promote the Recommendation via its website on open access and Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable (FAIR) data in 2025.


 Awareness-raising activities on open science have taken place. In November 2023, the Ministry of Higher Education and Science held a conference connected to the evaluation of Denmark's National Strategy for Open Access, with participation from senior management of universities, university colleges, higher education institutions within the fine arts, other research organisations and public and private foundations. The European Commission delivered a presentation on open science, including the value of FAIR data. The Danish e-Infrastructure Concortium (DeiC) participates in "Knowledge Exchange", a collaboration among six European organisations established in 2005 to develop infrastructures and services for open science; outputs from this partnership have been disseminated to Danish universities and other stakeholders.

Denmark does not report having a national science, technology and innovation policy with open science elements, nor a national legal framework addressing open science. However, the Ministry of Higher Education and Science has established specific policy instruments to promote open science at the national level. These include Denmark's National Strategy for Open Access (2014/2018), the National Strategy for Data Management Based on the FAIR Principles (2021) and the Danish Code of Conduct for Research Integrity (2014). The Open Access Strategy aims to achieve 100% open access to Danish university publications by 2025, but  2024 monitoring found that 75 per cent of scientific articles from Danish universities were open access and that the full target unlikely to be reached owing to embargo periods and journal restrictions. A Ministry committee is updating the Code of Conduct to address FAIR data management, open access and citizen science.

 Eight Danish universities have institutional open access to publications and (FAIR) data management policies; some universities have guidelines regarding citizen science.
