A few specific policy instruments promote open science, including the CONARE's Open Science Declaration, which promotes policies and collaborative actions for implementing open science and non-commercial open access approaches in public universities.
  
At the institutional level, the report cites the Open Science Strategy of the National University  of Costa Rica (UNA)  as an exemplary policy, which addresses seven open science pillars aligned with the 2021 UNESCO Recommendation.   
 
As a specific national funding mechanism for open science, CONARE allocates USD 80,000 annually through dedicated calls for projects related to open science. The same fund is cited as the means of integrating open science practices into existing research funding. 
 
The report does not describe efforts toward equitable public-private partnerships on open science, noting that the forthcoming National Open Science Strategy is expected to address this area. 
 
The report indicates the absence of a national monitoring framework for open science. However, it references UNA's Open Science Strategy, noting that UNA is expected to develop evaluation mechanisms for CONARE's scientific journals.

Two national open science infrastructures are reported: MICITT’s National Information System on Science and Technology (SINCYT) and CONARE’s Kímuk National RepositoryKímuk National Repository. The former centralises information on government-funded research projects. The latter preserves and provides open access to scientific publications from public universities by aggregating information from institutional repositories. In addition to institutional repositories, the report cites UNA’s Academic Network, a portal serving as a search tool for academic staff, research programmes, projects and publications.  
 
Costa Rica participates in regional and international collaborations related to shared open science infrastructures, including through regional initiatives such as the Latin American Forum on Science Evaluation (FOLEC) and the Panama Declaration on Open Science (2018). Costa Rica also participates in RedCLARA, which aims to interconnect Latin America's Research and Education Networks (NRENs). In addition, the Kímuk National Repository is reported to facilitate participation in international projects through interoperability agreements.

Systematic capacity building on open science has taken place in higher education institutions, with the support of CONARE. For example, it established the Open Knowledge Subcommission, a working group under the Vice-Chancellors' Commission, to strengthen member institutions' journals and repositories and promote open science. The report cites the CONARE’s Open Science Declaration as providing a basis for both the incorporation of open science competencies in higher education and capacity building for policymakers. 

 

CONARE's annual USD 80,000 allocation for open science-related projects and activities is cited as supporting the use of open educational resources for open science capacity building.

CONARE’s Open Science Declaration calls for the development of research evaluation models in public universities that incorporate open science indicators, including aspects related to career evaluation and progression. 

 

As an initiative to recognise and reward open science practices, the report cites CONARE’s annual allocation of USD 80,000 to support projects and activities related to open science. 

 

No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.

CONARE’s Open Science Declaration is reported to promote innovative approaches at different stages of the research cycle. In particular, it encourages citizen participation in the management of research generated in public universities. The Declaration also promotes the opening up of codes and systems so that they can be reproduced by others and knowledge can circulate without physical, digital or language barriers. In addition, CONARE has been promoted activities supporting the inclusion of preprints in scientific journals.

Costa Rica promotes international collaborations in different areas of open science through regional initiatives such as FOLEC, which also serves as a platform for discussions on funding mechanisms for open science. See above for the reported collaborations on open science infrastructures.  

 

The report does not describe a specific strategy or plan for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted to government bodies including the Ministry of Technical and Higher Education, the Ministry of Communication, Technology and Innovation and the National Science, Technology and Innovation Council. Other recipients include the Sierra Leone Research and Education Network (SLREN), higher education and research institutions and civil society organisations.

Several awareness-raising activities on open science have taken place, including the National Open Science Symposium (August 2023), National Open Science Policy validation workshops (February 2024) as well as activities on science, technology and innovation (STI) and open science in universities, colleges and research institutions (December 2024).

Sierra Leone has a national STI policy (2023) that incorporates open science elements, covering open access to scientific knowledge, open science infrastructure, open engagement of societal actors and open dialogue with other knowledge systems. The country also reports having a dedicated National Open Science Policy of Sierra Leone (2024), developed through a participatory, multi-stakeholder consultative process and aimed at guiding the implementation of open science nationwide.

 The report indicates the availability of institutional open science policies; however, no specific policies are cited.

 No specific national funding mechanisms for open science are reported, and the report indicates that open science practices have not been integrated into existing research funding mechanisms. Efforts towards equitable public-private partnerships on open science are also not reported.

 A national monitoring framework for open science is reported to be under development, though no further details are provided.

Summary:
Open science infrastructures are reported to be under development, with a focus on platforms for exchanges and co-creation of knowledge between scientists and society. In particular, DSpace is being deployed for institutional repositories.

 Sierra Leone does not report hosting or funding any federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science. As a member of the West and Central African Research and Education Network (WACREN) community, the country participates in regional collaboration to optimise infrastructure use.

Regarding capacity building on open science in higher education and research performing institutions, technical training to develop open science infrastructures and network infrastructure has been conducted, alongside the awareness-raising activities reported above.

 The report indicates that open science competencies have not been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula.

Open educational resources are reported to be used as an instrument for open science capacity building, though no further details are given.

Initiatives to review research assessment and evaluation processes in line with open science principles are reported, linked to the 2024 National Open Science Policy, with the country also confirming the incorporation of specific clauses recognising open science practices in career evaluation and progression systems. The report does not identify, however, any initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices.

 No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.

The report indicates that initiatives promoting innovative, participatory methodological or procedural changes at different stages of the research cycle operate at both the national and institutional levels, linked to the National Open Science Policy. These initiatives address the promotion of preprints, the exploration of open peer review practices and the valuing and sharing of negative scientific results, while also extending to participatory engagement, encompassing citizen science, participatory strategies to identify the needs of marginalised communities and open innovation practices.

Sierra Leone promotes international scientific collaboration on open science through partnerships with WACREN, universities and the Science Granting Councils Initiative (SGCI) in Africa.

 The report indicates that a strategy for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science exists, though no further details are provided.

 Sierra Leone reports involvement in discussions on the establishment of regional and international funding mechanisms for open science, but no additional information is given.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted to the Ministry of Higher Education and Scientific Research and the Ministry of Digital Transition.


 Awareness-raising activities on open science are planned for incorporation into upcoming capacity-building training at higher education and research institutions.

Mauritania adopted a national research and innovation strategy in 2022, which references open science infrastructures and open dialogue with other knowledge systems as elements of open science. In addition, Mauritania’s National Digital Transformation Agenda 2022–2025, overseen by the Ministry of Digital Transition and developed with support from the United Nations Economic and Social Commission for Western Asia (ESCWA) following broad consultation, is reported as partly addressing open science, although no further details are provided. 

Regarding specific policy instruments to promote open science, the report states that Mauritania has no dedicated structure for this purpose, though the National Agency for Scientific Research and Innovation (Agence Nationale de la Recherche Scientifique et de l'Innovation, ANRSI) and higher education institutions are identified as bodies that can play an important role. No national implementation plan or roadmap for open science is reported, nor any institutional-level policies.

No specific national funding mechanisms for open science are reported. Open science practices have not been integrated into existing research funding mechanisms, which the report attributes to a lack of adequate regulatory framework.

No efforts toward equitable public-private partnerships on open science are reported; no is a national monitoring framework for open science in place.

National and institutional open science infrastructures, including a network of higher education and research institutions, are reported as under development.

 Mauritania does not report hosting or funding any federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science.

 The report affirms involvement in North-South, North-South-South or South-South collaborations related to open science platforms, though no further details are provided.

The report affirms that systematic capacity building on open science has taken place in higher education and research institutions but provides no details on the nature or scope of these activities.

 No capacity building programmes for policymakers on integrating open science values and principles into science, technology and innovation policies are reported.

 No framework of open science competencies has been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula, according to the report.

 Open educational resources are reported as partly used for open science capacity building, though no details are provided.

 No science communication initiatives in line with open science values and principles are reported. The report cites a lack of trainers as the barrier.

No initiatives to review research assessment and evaluation processes in order to align them with open science principles are reported.

No specific clauses recognising open science practices have been incorporated into career evaluation and progression systems, according to the report. Barriers cited include an inadequate regulatory framework and the need to mobilise resources for international consultation.

 No initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices are reported; the report attributes this to financial constraints.

 No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.

No initiatives promoting innovative, participatory methodological or procedural changes at different stages of the research cycle are reported. The report attributes this to limited awareness and financial constraints.

Mauritania indicates the absence of a specific strategy or plan for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science, and the report does not describe any initiatives promoting international cooperation on open science.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted through the Instituto Tecnológico de Santo Domingo (INTEC), a private university.
 
The report affirms that awareness-raising activities on open science has been concluded, primarily in the form of publications, such as "Open Science: Challenges for Building Scientific Culture in the Dominican Republic". In addition, conferences and innovation fairs organised by the Ministry of Higher Education, Science and Technology (MESCyT) during the Dominican Science Week were mentioned as promoting open science.

A partial national legal and policy framework addressing certain open science elements exists, including the Law on Open Access to Public Information. However, no dedicated national implementation plan or roadmap for open science is reported.

 

The Dominican Republic has also endorsed a regional declaration adopted at the Meeting of Ministers and High-Level Authorities of Higher Education of Ibero-American countries (Valencia, Spain, October 2024), which includes commitments to promote open science.

 At the institutional level, the report indicates the existence of open science policies. However, while several activities involving universities are reported, including an event launched by the Pontifical Catholic University Madre y Maestra (PUCMM) to promote open science policies in the country, no formal policy documents are cited.

The report cites the 5th EU-Latin America and Caribbean Joint Call in Science, Technology and Innovation (2025), in which the Dominican Republic participated and invested, as a specific funding mechanism for open science. According to the report, open science practices have also been integrated into existing research funding through the General Regulations of the National Fund for Innovation and Scientific and Technological Development (FONDOCYT), although no further details are provided.  

 The report cites “science and technology indicators” as part of a national monitoring framework for open science, though no further details are provided.

The report indicates the presence of national and institutional open science infrastructures, citing as an example a national portal of scientific and technological information resources launched by MESCyT for the higher education community.

 

In addition, the report describes the Network of Researchers from Higher Education Institutions in the Southern Region of the Dominican Republic (REDISUR), established in 2025 through an agreement between MESCyT and several universities to promote collaborative research and shared infrastructure use.

Capacity building on open science in higher education is reported to have taken place. However, the report states that no framework of open science competencies has been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula, although the country has been promoting open science mainly through panels, conferences and other events since its commitment at the 41st UNESCO General Conference in November 2021.

 The report indicates that open educational resources are used for open science capacity building, though no details are provided.

According to the report, science communication initiatives in line with open science principles include congresses, seminars and workshops for the dissemination of science, such as the Student Congress of Scientific and Technological Research (CEICyT) and the Scientific and Technological Research Congress.

The report indicates the existence of initiatives related to research assessment and evaluation processes in the context of open science. For example, Article 56 (b) of the FONDOCYT regulations, which establish criteria for the evaluation and selections of research projects, is cited as highlighting the role of training and human capacity development in research.

 No specific rewards for open science practices are reported. The report notes that the Dominican Republic has endorsed a regional declaration for Ibero-American countries (see above), which includes open science commitments, and indicates that work is under way to develop recognition mechanisms.

 No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.

Some initiatives at both national and institutional levels promote innovative approaches to increase the openness of the scientific process. For example, MESCyT and the Open University for Adults (UAPA) held a seminar on open science that included citizen science.

International scientific collaboration on open science is promoted, for example, through CEICyT.

 The Dominican Republic reports involvement in discussions on regional funding mechanisms for open science through the Regional Open Science Framework, although no further details are provided.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted and shared with several national ministries and authorities, including the Ministry of Higher Education and Scientific Research, the Libyan Authority for Scientific Research, the Ministry of Education and the Ministry of Culture and Knowledge Development. However, the report does not describe any awareness-raising activities on open science.

The report does not describe the existence of a national policy or legal framework addressing open science or its elements, nor of a dedicated implementation plan or roadmap, specific policy instruments or institutional open science policies. However, the report notes the existence of a National Committee for Open Science, comprising senior officials from sectors involved in science, technology and innovation, which intends to advocate for the development of a national framework in these areas.

Libya identifies the availability of two types of open science infrastructure: federated information technology infrastructure, including high-performance computing, cloud computing and data storage; and community-managed infrastructures, protocols and standards supporting bibliodiversity and engagement with society. As an example, the Ministry of Education has integrated all scientific journals affiliated with universities and research centres into a single open access platform, requiring all journals to be openly accessible.

 

The report does not indicate that Libya hosts or funds any federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science.

The report states that systematic capacity building on open science has not yet taken place in Libyan higher education or research performing institutions. Although these institutions engage in open science activities such as knowledge sharing and maintaining open access journals, they currently lack formal, structured programmes dedicated to building capacity in open science. The report also does not describe the integration of open science competencies into higher education research skills curricula in Libya.

The report indicates that initiatives to review research assessment and evaluation processes in line with open science principles are foreseen through the newly established National Committee for Open Science. The committee aims to modernise existing research evaluation frameworks to include open science indicators. It plans to develop national policies and guidelines that incentivise researchers to adopt open science practices and to encourage universities and research centres to integrate open science into institutional assessment criteria. The report describes these initiatives as being in their early stages.

The report states that Libya has not yet established formal international collaborations specifically focused on open science. The National Committee for Open Science is reported to be working towards developing open science projects intended to encourage international partnerships and knowledge exchange.
The report does not describe initiatives promoting innovative, participatory methodological or procedural changes to increase openness of the scientific process.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted by the Ministry of University and Research (MUR), which integrated its principles into national research policies. The Recommendation has also been promoted through events organised by the Open Science Working Group of the Italian Council of Presidents of Public Research Institutions (CoPER), the Italian Association for the Promotion of Open Science (AISA) and the Galileo Museum.

 Awareness-raising activities have taken place at national and institutional levels. Most awareness-raising events have addressed changes to the scholarly communication model, copyright reform and data management practices. Open-science.it serves as a platform for disseminating relevant information on open science and related activities within the country. The Open Science Café, hosted by the Italian Computing and Data Infrastructure (ICDI) Competence Centre, is another important initiative contributing to raise awareness, engaging the research community through informal sessions on data sharing, research transparency and open access publishing.

Italy's science, technology and innovation policy, the National Research Programme (Piano Nazionale della Ricerca, PNR) 2021–2027, coordinated by MUR, includes open science as a strategic objective, promoting open access to scientific publications and research data, supporting infrastructure development for open repositories and encouraging the use of open-source software.  


The national legal framework partly addresses open science. Law 112/2013 (Article 4) regulates open access to publications. For research data, Italy implemented the 2019 EU Open Data Directive through Legislative Decree 36/2006, with the Agency for Digital Italy (AgID) publishing technical guidelines.

 The National Plan for Open Science (Piano Nazionale per la Scienza Aperta, PNSA), published by MUR in 2022 and forming part of the overarching PNR agenda, is the principal strategy and policy instrument for open science, establishing guidelines across multiple dimensions, including open access publishing, data sharing and FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable) principles. In March 2023, MUR appointed an expert group to prepare an operational implementation plan for priority actions through 2027.
 

At the institutional level, at least 75 academic and research institutions, including the National Research Council (CNR) and the National Institute of Geophysics and Volcanology (INGV), have adopted open science policies, most focusing on publications.
 

MUR has allocated specific resources to support PNSA implementation as a national funding mechanism for open science. Open science practices have been integrated into existing research funding: an increasing number of programmes, including projects funded under the National Recovery and Resilience Plan (Piano Nazionale di Ripresa e Resilienza, PNRR), explicitly require FAIR data management and other open science practices and treat associated costs as eligible expenses.

PNRR investments also support public–private partnerships on open science by requiring compliance with open science principles from both public and private entities and fostering collaboration between them.

 A national open science monitoring framework is reported as under development. No centralised system is reported, but several initiatives track adoption using shared core indicators and non-proprietary monitoring infrastructures, supplemented by data from OpenAIRE (Open Access Infrastructure for Research in Europe).

National open science infrastructure is reported as under development. The PNSA mandates creation of a national infrastructure, led by the Italian Research Centre on High Performance Computing, Big Data and Quantum Computing (ICSC) and ICDI, leveraging existing institutional resources. From 2025, this infrastructure aims to be federated with other European nodes as part of the European Open Science Cloud (EOSC)’s build-up phase.

 Several institutions have developed their own open science infrastructures. For example, CNR operates D4Science, a platform providing virtual research environments supporting open science workflows across the entire research lifecycle. The National Institute of Nuclear Physics (INFN) runs its cloud infrastructure and an open access repository for research outputs. INGV maintains a data portal.

Italy reports hosting and funding federated international infrastructure for open science. It hosts services and supports a large number of European Research Infrastructures, which, through the Science-Clusters initiative, collaborate on common open science strategies. CNR hosts a set of IT services of the OpenAIRE infrastructure. Italy also contributes to GÉANT through the national research and education network GARR and to the European Grid Infrastructure (EGI) through INFN.

Regarding international collaborations on open science infrastructures, including North–South and North-South-South, the report highlights D4Science, managed by CNR, as well as participation in the European Life-Science Infrastructure for Biological Information (ELIXIR) Italy node for biological data sharing.

Systematic capacity building on open science has taken place in higher education and research institutions, although these efforts are not yet fully coordinated. Many universities and research performing organisations have established specialised open science units, including dedicated task forces for training (e.g., the University of Milan, the University of Bologna and CNR). A principal collective initiative is the ICDI Competence Centre, a network of twenty-one universities, research centres and infrastructures that supports adoption of open science practices and FAIR principles. It has established the Italian Data Stewards network, with over 100 participants, and participates in the Skills4EOSC EU project, which aims to create a training ecosystem for open and FAIR science across Europe. However, no systematic capacity building programme for policymakers is reported, though the topic has been addressed at individual events.

 

The report indicates that a standardised national framework of open science competencies has not yet been adopted across higher education curricula; however, individual institutions are introducing relevant courses. For example, the Scuola Normale Superiore has made a course on open science and research data management mandatory for second-year doctoral students since 2022/23.

 The report states that open educational resources are not used systematically for open science capacity building, despite Italian participation in Skills4EOSC, which promotes their use.

 Regarding science communication initiatives aligned with open science principles, the report refers to AISA and Citizen Science Italia ETS. It also cites the G7 Science and Technology Ministers' Meeting communiqué, adopted in Bologna, as supporting the role of science communication in building public trust and promoting evidence-based policymaking.

Initiatives to review research assessment and evaluation processes are under way at both national and institutional levels. The PNSA includes research assessment reform among its action areas. Fifty-three Italian academic and research performing institutions participate in CoARA and have established the CoARA Italian Chapter, which supports assessment reform through mutual learning and dialogue on criteria and processes. The National Agency for the Evaluation of Universities and Research Institutes (ANVUR) has signed the CoARA Agreement and taken preliminary steps in its guidelines for the evaluation period 2020–2024. At the institutional level, the University of Bologna's action plan promotes open science and qualitative evaluation, and CNR's Roadmap for Open Science outlines a plan to define quality and impact indicators for evaluating researchers and projects. .

 The report states that specific clauses recognising open science practices have not been incorporated in career evaluation and progression systems. Only preliminary steps have been taken in an uncoordinated and experimental manner by a few institutions, such as CNR.
 Regarding recognition and rewarding open science practices, the report does not cite specific initiatives but notes that the membership of Italian institutions in CoARA demonstrates a commitment to reforms that recognise open science.

 Regarding unintended negative consequences of open science practices, the report highlights an ongoing debate on balancing open science with security and research integrity. A dedicated workshop was organised in 2024 in preparation for the G7 Science and Technology Ministers' Meeting.

Several initiatives at national and institutional levels promote innovative approaches to increase the openness at different stages of the research cycle. The Italian Reproducibility Network (ITRN), the Italian branch of the international reproducibility network, works to improve research quality and conducts surveys and empirical studies on open science implementation. Its activities address the valuing and sharing of negative scientific results and open innovation practices.

 Citizen Science Italia ETS advances participatory methods that value input from societal actors. This national network of experts, universities, research centres, scientific museums, associations and public bodies develops inclusive and collaborative tools within an open science framework.

International collaboration is a priority action area in the PNSA. The plan serves as a strategic framework for cross-border, multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science, encouraging participation in international initiatives, contributions to EU and global research infrastructures and engagement in the EOSC.

 

Specifically, the report highlights Italy’s contribution to the build-up phase of the EOSC Federation. MUR has also announced its intention to join a coalition of European research organisations supporting Open Research Europe (ORE), a collective not-for-profit open access publishing platform, for an initial five-year period beginning in 2026.   

The report indicates that Italy participates in discussions on international funding mechanisms for open science, citing INFN’s membership in Plan S and the country’s participation in the European Cooperation in Science and Technology (COST) programme.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted at the federal level to the Federal Ministry for Education and Research (BMBF) and the Federal Ministry for Digital and Transport (BMDV), and at the state level to the relevant science ministries of the 16 German Länder (states) through the Standing Conference of the Ministers of Education and Cultural Affairs (Kultusministerkonferenz, KMK). All members of the Alliance of Science Organisations and the 50 most prominent learned societies have also received it.


 Germany's engagement in promoting open science dates to 2003, when the Berlin Declaration on Open Access was launched; it has since been signed by some 800 institutions worldwide. Building on this, the German Commission for UNESCO has organised approximately fifteen conferences and workshops on the UNESCO Recommendation since 2020, targeting various audiences domestically and internationally, and its representatives have contributed as invited speakers to at least fifteen further open science events. In March 2022, the international Open Science Conference hosted by the Leibniz Association was co-organised with the German Commission for UNESCO; its main panel discussed the Recommendation with participants from UNESCO and the Chair of the UNESCO Executive Board. In 2024, the Commission published a bilingual brochure containing the German translation of the Recommendation and hosted a global online seminar for National Commissions to prepare the periodic reporting process. The European Open Science Cloud (EOSC) Symposium held in Berlin in October 2024 attracted more than 600 participants. Several science organisations incorporated the Recommendation into their presentations and websites, and major funders referenced it in their open science policies.

Germany's federal science, technology and innovation policy, the Future Research and Innovation Strategy (2023), addresses open science elements, such as open access to scientific knowledge, open science infrastructure, open engagement of societal actors and open dialogue with other knowledge systems. Other key policies and legal frameworks include the Secondary Publication Right in copyright law applying to publicly funded scholarly publications (2014), the Federal Government’s Open Data Strategy (2023), the Joint Federal-Länder Open Access Guidelines (2023), the BMBF Strategy on Open Educational Resources (2023) and the Citizen Science Strategy 2030 for Germany (2022). A Research Data Law is under discussion. The report does not describe a stand-alone national implementation plan for open science, noting that above-mentioned policies reduced this need; however, discussion are ongoing.

 

At the institutional level, many, if not most, higher education institutions have adopted open science policies, including the Berlin University Alliance (HU Berlin, FU Berlin, TU Berlin and Charité Berlin), the University of Freiburg, the University of Tübingen, Leibniz University Hannover, TH Köln, universities in Hamburg and the University of Applied Sciences Jena. In addition, the report cites open science policies of major research institutions, such as the Helmholtz Association and the Leibniz Association, which both explicitly refer to the 2021 Recommendation as a basis.

 

Among specific funding mechanisms for open science, the National Research Data Infrastructure (NFDI), established in 2019, coordinates 27 disciplinary consortia for research data management with up to 90 million EUR per year. The DEAL Consortium negotiates nationwide transformative open access agreements with major commercial publishers. The German Research Foundation (DFG)'s  Open Access Publication Funding programme grants a fixed allowance to research institutions (not individuals) for the publication of research results via open access, with over 38 million EUR awarded in 2024. The DFG also supports a National Service Centre for Diamond Open Access. At the Länder level, dedicated funding programmes also support open science practices, including the NiedersachsenOPEN initiative in Lower Saxony, which supports diamond open access publications and the development of a diamond open access publishing landscape, alongside similar schemes in Schleswig-Holstein and Thuringia. However, open science practices have not been made legally binding for recipients of research grants in general.

 

Regarding equitable public-private partnerships on open science, the Stifterverband, a joint initiative of companies and foundations devoted to higher education, science and innovation, has supported the development of the Road to Openness self-assessment tool for universities, while the NFDI's industry engagement section facilitates data exchange with industry.

 

The report does not describe a national monitoring framework for open science, though Germany participates in the Open Science Monitoring Initiative (OSMI), with the Charité Berlin as a founding member.

Various open science infrastructures are available non-commercially to public-sector researchers across Germany. Many comply with FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable) principles. According to the UNESCO Open Science Outlook 2023, Germany hosts 498 open data repositories, ranking second globally, and 299 open access repositories, ranking fourth, alongside approximately 345 diamond open access journals. The DFG maintains a curated, non-exhaustive register of recognised research infrastructures and scientific facilities across the country, which provide access either openly or through transparent selection procedures. A few examples of open science infrastructures cited in the report are highlighted below.

 

Germany has several high-performance computing facilities, including JUWELS at Jülich Research Centre (85 PetaFLOPS) and JUPITER (reported to be launched in 2025 as Europe’s first ExaFLOPS computer). The National High Performance Computing (NHR) Alliance combines high-performance computing resources and make them available to scientists at German universities free of charge. In addition, the report cites a national citizen science platform, funded by the BMBF since 2014.


Germany hosts and funds federated international open science infrastructures. It is a primary supporter of the EOSC, with 19 German institutional members in the EOSC Association, including the NFDI. Germany also supports the Open Research Europe publishing platform and funds international infrastructures including zbMATH Open, the Sponsoring Consortium for Open Access Publishing in Particle Physics (SCOAP3), the Directory of Open Access Journals (DOAJ) and the Open Access Publishing in European Networks (OAPEN).

 

Germany also funds North-South collaborations that promote open science infrastructure. The Southern African Science Service Centre for Climate Change and Adaptive Land Management (SASSCAL, established 2012 with BMBF funding) includes the transnational Open Access Data Centre (OADC). The West African Science Service Centre on Climate Change and Adapted Land Use (WASCAL, also established 2012 with BMBF funding) is developing open data infrastructure.

Systematic capacity building on open science is delivered through multiple channels. The open-access.network, funded by the BMBF since 2019, is a nationwide platform offering trainings, workshops, webinars and open educational resources (OER) on open access. Parallel platforms serve research data management and OER capacity building. The Max Planck Society holds periodic Open Science Days and maintains a network of Open Science Ambassadors composed primarily of early-career researchers. NFDI consortia provide discipline-specific training on research data management, and in 2021–2022 the BMBF launched calls for proposals that resulted in 11 data competence centres at higher education and research institutions. The NHR Alliance offers training for university researchers on high-performance computing. In addition, some institutions have established dedicated units, such as the Open Science Centre at Ludwig-Maximilians-Universität Munich.

 

A national framework of open science competencies in higher education curricula is not reported. Due to university autonomy, centralised curricular requirements are limited. Open science is part of many individual curricula on a voluntary basis, and no obligatory courses for students are reported. The University of Konstanz, for example, offers basic and advanced open science courses as OER, awarding academic credits.  

 

OER are widely used for open science capacity building. The Cooperation Network OER Supporting Infrastructures and Services (KNOER) supports cross-Länder cooperation on digital teaching infrastructure in higher education.

 

Science communication initiatives aligned with open science principles include Science in Dialogue (Wissenschaft im Dialog, WiD), funded by the BMBF since 2000, which hosts the annual national forum on science communication. The National Institute for Science Communication (NaWiK) and the National Association of Higher Education Communication jointly operate the Wissenschaftskommunikation.de platform.

German science organisations participate in international initiatives to review research assessment and evaluation. The Coalition for Advancing Research Assessment (CoARA) has more than 30 members from Germany, including the DFG, the Leibniz Association, the Alexander von Humboldt Foundation and several large universities. A German CoARA National Chapter was established in 2024. Ongoing discussions within the Alliance of Science Organisations and the German CoARA National Chapter address whether open science practices should feature in research assessment; the current position is that open science activities should be recognised in the assessment of individual researchers but should not serve as a criterion for evaluating research proposals. The report states that specific clauses recognising open science practices in career evaluation and progression systems are not yet in place and that debates on this issue are ongoing, as reflected in the DFG’s analysis of obstacles in research assessment related to academic publishing and in discussions during a 2024 workshop held by Alliance of Science Organisations.

 

Several initiatives recognise and reward open science practices through awards. These include the Enter Award for open access, funded by the BMBF and implemented by iRights.Lab in 2024, and the Stifterverband's Open Data Impact Award. The Berlin Institute of Health at Charité offers awards spanning diverse dimensions of open science. Additional awards have been established by the Hamburg Open Science Programme, the Humboldt University of Berlin and the University of Oldenburg, among others.

 

Germany highlighted several unintended negative consequences of open science as follows. There is debate about promoting openness in international cooperation with countries that have different practices and understandings of openness. Costs associated with commercial open access publishing are a concern, and open science has become a topic in relation to research security and digital sovereignty.

The report identifies initiatives at both national and institutional levels that promote innovative approaches to increase openness at different stages of the research cycle.

 

For example, preprints, open peer review and the sharing of negative results are addressed by the Code of Conduct: Guidelines for Safeguarding Good Research Practice, administered by the DFG and adopted by almost all universities and research organisations. Germany also supported the 2024 G20 consensus on an Open Innovation Strategy. The Stifterverband's innOsci project, which promoted the strategic opening of innovation processes, produced an Open Transfer Index to measure open innovation. In addition, two participatory formats for incorporating societal perspectives into research are in regular use: citizens' councils (Bürgerräte), including the Citizens' Council on Research (Bürgerrat Forschung), which convene randomly selected citizens; and living labs (Reallabore), structured cooperation formats between science and civil society. The Leibniz Association and the Helmholtz Association each maintain internal working groups on citizen science and run projects in this area. In 2022, the Alliance of Science Organisations issued a statement committing to enable participation in research where it offers added value for science and society. Other citizen science initiatives include the above-mentioned Citizen Science Strategy 2030 for Germany, the national citizen science platform and an annual forum on citizen science.

The 2024 Strategy for Promoting the International Orientation of German Universities, adopted by the KMK (The Standing Conference of the Ministers of Education, Science and Cultural Affairs), contains implicit and explicit references to open science principles in international scientific collaboration.

 

The report does not describe, however, a dedicated strategy for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science, noting that promoting open science principles as part of all international activities is a priority and a stand-alone plan is not seen as necessary.

 

Germany is an active member of the Council for National Open Science Coordination (CoNOSC), which brings together national open science leaders for international dialogue and the sharing of good practice.

 

Germany participates in discussions on regional and international funding mechanisms for open science, including through its involvement in the EOSC and through its support for Open Research Europe as a publicly funded publication infrastructure.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted to the Ministry of Higher Education and Science and Technology and the National Research and Innovation Agency (BRIN), both identified in the report as playing a significant role in advancing the Recommendation.

According to the report, awareness-raising on open science has been promoted through an open science meeting and a webinar (no further details provided), as well as through publications and blogposts.

Indonesia reports having a national science, technology and innovation policy, including BRIN Regulation Number 23 of 2022, which incorporates certain open science-related elements. Specific policy instruments to promote open science are reported to exist but remain largely focused on open access publications, particularly the management and development of open access journals; the report notes these do not fully reflect the broader spectrum of open science practices and do not comprehensively lead to the implementation of the UNESCO Recommendation. The report also indicates the availability of open science policies at the institutional level, but it does not describe any formal policy documents.
 
A specific national funding mechanism for open science is under development. However, open science practices  are reported to be integrated into existing research funding mechanisms, although no concrete examples are provided.

 
 The report states that no equitable public-private partnerships on open science have been established, noting that the implementing regulation is under discussion. A national monitoring framework for open science is reported to be under development, with the corresponding regulation also under discussion.

Indonesia reports the presence of several categories of national open science infrastructures, as illustrated through the following examples. BRIN has launched a national research data repository “RIN” and provides high-performance computing, cloud computing and data storage services through the Electronic Science Service (E-Layanan Sains, ELSA) programme. In addition, the report highlights the existence of a preprint server (RINarxiv).


Regarding North-South, North-South-South or South-South collaborations on open science infrastructures, the country reports its participation in CERN’s ALICE (A Large Ion Collider Experiment).

The report states that no systematic capacity building on open science has taken place in higher education or research performing institutions, and no capacity building programmes for policy makers on integrating open science values into science, technology and innovation policies have been conducted. Both gaps are attributed to limited understanding among policy makers of the concept and benefits of open science and insufficient awareness of the importance of its integration into national policies.

 A framework of open science competencies has not been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula, according to the report. The report noted, however, that the 2019 Indonesian Guided Insight on Open Science (Wawasan Terbimbing Sains Terbuka Indonesia, WIBISANA) initiative of the Indonesian Open Science Community provided a learning platform for open science, but no systematic institutional or national effort has been made to integrate open science into the higher education curriculum.

 Open educational resources (OER) are reported to be used for open science capacity building. The report cited the Creative Commons Indonesia's Open Learning Resources project, funded by the Wikimedia Foundation, which provided training for educators on creating OER in 2024–2025.

 Regarding science communication initiatives aligned with open science values, the report cites ScienceWatchdog.id platform.

The report states that no initiatives have been undertaken to review research assessment and evaluation processes to align them with open science principles. Existing systems rely on mainstream metrics such as citation counts and journal impact factors and tend to ignore open science practices. No specific clauses recognising open science practices are reported to have been incorporated into career evaluation and progression systems.

No initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices are reported. The report states that awards remain centred on citation-based measurements rather than the quality of content or substantive contribution of research.

 The report identifies several concerns raised as unintended negative consequences of open science, including fears of plagiarism, commercial exploitation of disclosed research by non-contributing parties, misappropriation without proper attribution, and difficulties in formal publication of previously shared results. It notes, however, that these concerns have not been supported by strong arguments or empirical evidence and tend to overlook the broader benefits of open science.

At the national level, initiatives promoting innovative, participatory approaches at different stages of the research cycle are reported. These address the promotion of preprints, exploration of open peer review practices, valuing and sharing of negative scientific results, participatory methods including citizen science and crowdsourcing, development of strategies for identifying the needs of marginalised communities and open innovation. The RINarxiv preprint server is cited as specific example.

International collaboration on open science is described in the report as not yet systematic or structured at the institutional or national level. Efforts remain limited to initiatives by small groups, including university research teams, BRIN research units, non-governmental organisations and media platforms such as The Conversation Indonesia. These are characterised as sporadic and not coordinated.

 The report identifies the Indonesian Collaborative Research and International Collaborative Research programmes as major mechanisms encouraging cross-institutional and cross-country research collaboration. However, the academic guidelines these programmes follow still rely on mainstream metrics such as citation counts and journal impact factors, an approach in contradiction with the 2021 Recommendation.

 Indonesia's involvement in discussions on regional and international funding mechanisms for open science is confirmed in the report. Several meetings and discussions have taken place, but there has been no consistent follow-up. The report states that without clear regulatory and funding mechanisms, open science practices will remain difficult to develop.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted through the Ministry of Education, Research, Development and Youth of the Slovak Republic, the Slovak Academy of Sciences and the Slovak Rectors' Conference.

The Slovak Centre of Scientific and Technical Information (SCSTI), managed by the Ministry of Education, Research, Development and Youth,  conducts awareness-raising activities on open science through in-person events (e.g., annual Slovak Open Science Forum), webinars and courses.

Slovakia reports having a national science, technology and innovation policy  that incorporates open science elements: open access to scientific knowledge, open science infrastructure and open engagement of societal actors; however, no further details are provided.

The National Strategy for Open Science 2021–2028 together with its associated action plan constitute the dedicated national framework for open science. The action plan is updated every two years to support the implementation of the Strategy.

 

A specific policy instrument to promote open science is the Contact Office of Open Access, established at SCSTI in 2016. It acts as the national coordinator and methodological body for open science, supporting the implementation of the National Strategy for Open Science 2021–2028 and its associated action plan.

 

Institutional-level open science policies are reported to be under development.

 
 Specific national funding mechanisms for open science consist of three long-term transformative agreements with Elsevier, the Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE) and Springer Nature, covering gold open access publishing. Open science practices have not been integrated into existing research funding mechanisms; the report attributes this to the fragmented organisational structure of grant agencies, each with different systems and priorities.

 Efforts towards equitable public-private partnerships are foreseen through the Action Plan for Open Science 2025–2028.

 A national monitoring framework for open science is under development, focusing initially on monitoring open access publications.

Open science infrastructures are available in the country. The reported infrastructure types include federated information technology infrastructure for open science, covering high-performance computing, cloud computing and data storage, and community-based monitoring and information systems. SCSTI serves as the national information centre, which coordinates and ensures the operation of interdisciplinary research and development centres and national open science infrastructures. Among the infrastructures it operates is the Central Information Portal for Research, Development and Innovation. SCSTI is a member of the European Open Science Cloud (EOSC) Association. In addition, the Slovak Academic Network (SANET), the national research and education network, provides high-speed internet connectivity to universities and research institutions.

 

Slovakia does not report hosting or funding federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science. Existing open science infrastructures at regional and international levels are, however, reported to be accessible to relevant open science actors in the country.

 

Regarding international collaborations to optimise infrastructure use, Slovakia has joined the pilot phase of establishing the EOSC federation and serves as an EOSC node; however, no North–South or North–South–South collaborations related to shared open science infrastructures are reported.

The report states that systematic capacity building on open science has not taken place in higher education or research performing institutions, attributing this to the fragmentation of the system. Open science education is instead addressed through train-the-trainer courses at the level of further education, such as introductory courses on open science provided by SCSTI, as mentioned above. The report notes that, for the same reason, a framework of open science competencies has not been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula.

 

Capacity building programmes for policymakers on integrating open science values and principles into science, technology and innovation policies are not reported. The report states that this is not considered relevant at present, as knowledge building occurs through the Working Group for Open Science at the Ministry of Education, Research, Development, and Youth, which includes representatives of government authorities, the academic community and learned societies.

 

The report does not indicate that open educational resources are used as an instrument for open science capacity building.

 

In the area of science communication in line with open science values, the report cites the Slovak Reproducibility Network.

The report indicates a lack of initiatives to review existing research assessment and evaluation processes to align them with open science principles and values. It also indicates that specific clauses recognising open science practices have not been incorporated into career evaluation and progression systems, nor does it describe initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices.

 

Among unintended negative consequences of open science practices, the report identifies high article processing charges associated with gold open access business models in scientific publishing.

Slovakia does not report any initiatives at the national or institutional level promoting innovative, participatory methodological or procedural changes at different stages of the research cycle to increase the openness of the scientific process.

Slovakia promotes international scientific collaborations on open science through participation in the EOSC and partnership with OpenAIRE (Open Access Infrastructure for Research in Europe). Slovak research institutions also engage in cross-border projects, such as CAPSID, a biomedical research collaboration under the Interreg V-A Slovakia-Austria programme.

 

The report does not indicate that Slovakia has a specific strategy or plan for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science. Involvement in discussions on the establishment of regional and international funding mechanisms for open science is also not reported.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been shared with the Ministry of Higher Education, Science and Innovation, which promoted it alongside compatible EU strategic documents. In 2020, the Ministry established a national working group of stakeholders to prepare a national research and innovation strategy involving open science and an Action Plan for Open Science, intended to enable implementation of international open science policies at the national level.

Awareness-raising activities on open science have been organised through several channels. Since 2022, the Academic and Research Network of Slovenia (ARNES), the Slovenian Open Science Community (SSOZ) and the Ministry of Higher Education, Science and Innovation have jointly organised annual Open Science Days and European Open Science Cloud (EOSC) National Tripartite Events, with plans to continue in the coming years.

Slovenia's key national policies explicitly addressing open science, including those in the field of science, technology and innovation, are as follows: the Scientific Research and Innovation Activities Act, ZZrID (in particular, Articles 40-42), the Resolution on the Slovenian Scientific Research and Innovation Strategy 2030, ReZrIS30 (in particular, Measure 6.2), the Decree on the Implementation of Scientific Research Work in Accordance with the Principles of Open Science (detailing certain provisions of the ZZrID) and the Action Plan for Open Science (detailing certain provisions of the ReZrIS30). The Action Plan, adopted by the Government of the Republic of Slovenia in 2023, is the principal policy instrument for implementation of open science.

At institutional level, the SPOZNAJ project (2023–2026), co-financed by the EU Recovery and Resilience Facility and the national budget, supports twenty public research organisations and the Central Technical Library at the University of Ljubljana in incorporating open science into their policies and operations. The University of Ljubljana has already incorporated open science into its statute (Article 6 and 133), doctoral studies rules (Article 50) and digital strategy (Research Objective 2).

 Slovenia reports specific national funding mechanisms in place. The Ministry of Higher Education, Science and Innovation funds open science through the Action Plan and co-finances SPOZNAJ project, while the Slovenian Research and Innovation Agency (ARIS)'s Sector of Open Science manages tenders for (co)financing open access scientific publications, including monographs, citizen science and science popularisation activities. Open science practices have been integrated into research funding: all peer-reviewed publications and research data from ARIS-funded research must be in open access, and from 2025 project leaders must submit a Data Management Plan.

 The report does not describe efforts to foster equitable public-private partnerships on open science.

 A national monitoring framework is under development through two mechanisms: narrative reporting by publicly funded research organisations on their open science progress, and a national open science monitor currently being piloted. The monitor draws data from the Slovenian Current Research Information System (SICRIS), the national open data portal and the Co-operative Online Bibliographic System and Services (COBISS), which serves several Southeast European countries, and is expected to expand its data sources in the future.

The following two public institutions are responsible for the development and management of key national research infrastructure: ARNES, which provides network infrastructure, data centres and supercomputing services and coordinates the Slovenian National Supercomputing Network (SLING); and the Institute of Information Science (IZUM), which manages the Vega supercomputer and develops the COBISS.SI library information system. Community-managed research infrastructures include CLARIN.SI, the Slovenian research infrastructure for language resources and technologies; the Social Science Data Archives (ADP); ELIXIR Slovenia, the national node of the European life sciences research infrastructure; and the Digital Research Infrastructure for the Arts and Humanities – Slovenia (DARIAH-SI).

 Slovenia reports hosting and funding several federated regional and international infrastructures. For example, ARNES participates in the pan-European research and education network GÉANT. The Vega supercomputer is part of the European High-Performance Computing (EuroHPC) Joint Undertaking network. ARNES contributes to the EOSC and is set to become the national EOSC EU Node. The ADP represents Slovenia in the Consortium of European Social Science Data Archives European Research Infrastructure Consortium (CESSDA ERIC), while CLARIN.SI and DARIAH-SI participate in their respective European federations.

 The report does not describe involvement in North-South or North-South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use.

Systematic capacity building on open science takes place through universities, faculties and research performing organisations. The SPOZNAJ project and the Action Plan for Open Science support the provision of courses covering open publishing, research data management and authors' rights. In 2024, the first handbook on open science in Slovenian, Spoznaj FAIR, was published under the SPOZNAJ project. The University of Maribor has organised international Open Science Summer Schools and staff training programmes called IzpopolniUM. Since 2024, the University of Primorska has offered monthly open science seminars. Under the Action Plan, three designated university libraries are tasked with coordinating open science training of researchers.

The report does not describe capacity building programmes specifically designed for policymakers, noting instead that policymakers are regularly invited to open science events, participate in working groups and engage in European Research Area (ERA)’s open science activities.

The report indicates that a framework of open science competencies has not yet been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula and is not foreseen by end of 2025, though some training is provided for doctoral students. For example, at the University of Ljubljana, some faculties have integrated open science into compulsory doctoral lectures.
 
Open educational resources are reported as only partly used for open science capacity building, without further detail.

Slovenia reports the existence of initiatives  to support science communication in line with open science values. In 2024, a national strategic document on Communicating Science in Slovenia was adopted, which highlights the need for activities to strengthen science communication through alignment with open science policies.

Nine Slovenian organisations have joined the Coalition for Advancing Research Assessment (CoARA), committing to reform research evaluation. The Slovenian Quality Assurance Agency for Higher Education (NAKVIS) and ARIS have aligned the minimal standards for appointment to the titles of higher education teachers and researchers with open science principles. The Decree on the Implementation of Scientific Research Work in Accordance with the Principles of Open Science obliges public universities and research institutes to align assessment with open science, supported by the SPOZNAJ project. ARIS, a signatory to the San Francisco Declaration on Research Assessment (DORA) and CoARA, has announced a complete overhaul of its evaluation system beginning in May 2026. The University of Ljubljana and the University of Maribor have begun modifying their assessment procedures in 2024.

 The report does not describe existing initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices. The Action Plan for Open Science does, however, include plans to introduce incentives by amending internal regulations of public research organisations and establishing a scheme to recognise early-career researchers with results in open science.

 Regarding unintended negative consequences, the report notes that the transition to open science is technically and financially demanding, requiring additional resources and cultural change, and that these factors slow the pace of reform.

Initiatives promoting innovative, participatory approaches at different stages of the research cycle operate at both national and institutional levels and cover areas including preprints, open peer review, the sharing of negative scientific results and participatory methods involving societal actors. For example, the Citizen Science Network has 55 members drawn from universities, research institutions, non-governmental organisations and individuals, and its catalogue currently lists 72 citizen science projects.

Slovenia's framework for cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science is grounded in the ReZRIS30 (see above), whose Measure 6.2.1 requires the national open science ecosystem to be coherent with international practices and integrated into international networks. This is complemented by the Strategy for the Internationalisation of Higher Education and Science until 2030. As specific examples, Slovenia participates in the Open Science Monitoring Initiative (OSMI), the Research Data Alliance (RDA) with a nationally financed Slovenian RDA node, the EOSC Association and the Council for National Open Science Coordination (CoNOSC). National delegates have been nominated for ERA Policy Agenda open science actions. Slovenian institutions also participate in CoARA, Knowledge Rights 21, OPERAS, the PALOMERA project (Policy Alignment of Open Access Monographs in the European Research Area) and the European Citizen Science Association.

 Slovenia is involved in discussions on regional and international funding mechanisms for open science through its active participation in the operation and development of the EOSC and in the formation of open science policies within the ERA.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted through the Ministry of Science, Research and Technology and the National Commission for UNESCO in Iran.

Awareness-raising activities include an event organised by the Iranian Association for United Nations Studies (no further details are provided) and a series of LinkedIn posts by Dr. Tahereh Miremadi (Professor Emerita of innovation policy and diplomacy at the Iranian Research Organization for Science and Technology, IROST) that generated debate on the importance of open science and UNESCO initiatives. A workshop on open science and open innovation was also planned for spring 2025 at IROST.

At the national level, Iran’s Five-Year Plans for Science, Technology and Development, which constitute the main science, technology and innovation policy framework, are not reported to incorporate open science elements; instead, open science is only partially addressed through sector-specific policy instruments. In particular, the report cites the official policy governing Iranian scientific journals, issued by the Ministry of Science, Research and Technology and the Ministry of Health and Medical Education, which includes provisions on open access publishing; however, no further details, including the title of the policy, are provided. An administrative circular signed by the Minister of Science, Research and Technology permits editors-in-chief to require editorial fees from authors, yet the majority of journals decline to collect such fees, in practice operating under a diamond open access model in which neither authors nor readers pay. According to the Directory of Open Access Journals (DOAJ), 973 Iranian scientific journals are available free of charge.

 

A national implementation plan for open science is reported as under development, with the Ministry of Science, Research and Technology in charge.

 At the institutional level, IROST and the National Research Institute for Science Policy (NRISP) are reported as having open science policies, though no further details are provided.

 Regarding specific national funding mechanisms for open science, the report states that the government and private publishers contribute to making scientific publications accessible free of charge to all audiences; however, no further details are provided. Open science practices are described as integrated into existing research funding, the report notes that scientific publications are generally made openly accessible to readers by default.

 The report indicates that no efforts have been made to foster equitable public-private partnerships on open science, though the issue is being reviewed and initiatives are under consideration.

 No national monitoring framework for open science is reported.

The report identifies a few examples of national open science infrastructures in place: Elmnet (a nationwide search engine for scientific publications, providing open metadata free of charge and offering access to full texts upon request, typically for a small fee) and the Scientific Information Database, SID (an open access repository).

Iran does not report hosting or funding any federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science. Existing open science infrastructures at regional and international levels are described as accessible to relevant actors in the country, though the report notes limited possibilities regarding physical infrastructure for researchers due to political constraints.

No involvement in North-South, North-South-South or South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use is reported.

The report characterises both systematic capacity building on open science in higher education and capacity building for policymakers as partly addressed.

The report indicates that no framework of open science competencies has been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula, though this is under consideration with plans to develop such a framework.

 The report indicates that open educational resources are not used as an instrument for open science capacity building.

Initiatives to review existing research assessment and evaluation processes to align them with open science principles are reported as being discussed. The incorporation of specific clauses recognising open science practices into career evaluation and progression systems is similarly reported as under consideration. Initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices are also foreseen for implementation.

 The report does not indicate any unintended negative consequences of open science practices.

Initiatives promoting innovative, participatory approaches at different stages of the scientific process are reported at both the national and institutional levels. These focus on the development of participatory strategies for identifying the needs of marginalised communities and incorporating socially relevant issues into research agendas.

Iran reports plans to promote international scientific collaboration on open science by inviting Islamic countries to build a region of "science roads without borders" through a regional open science policy. The Organisation of Islamic Cooperation (OIC) is identified as the most appropriate intergovernmental body to build a regional open science community.

Regarding discussions on regional and international funding mechanisms for open science, the report states that the allocation of resources is under consideration by the relevant authorities.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted through several public events. In February 2022, an online event titled "UNESCO: Open Science – Artificial Intelligence. New Horizons, Visions, and Boundaries" presented the Recommendation to the Greek scientific community. It was also communicated at the 1st Greek European Open Science Cloud (EOSC) Tripartite Event in October 2022 and the 3rd Open Science Symposium and EOSC Tripartite Event in December 2024. The latter was held under the joint auspices of the Ministries of Development, Digital Governance, and Education, Religious Affairs and Sports.

Awareness-raising activities on open science are carried out by several actors. The Hellenic Open Science Initiative (HOSI), established in February 2022 by 13 research, technology and innovation organisations, has a Training Working Group that prepares and disseminates training material. OpenAIRE's National Open Access Desk (NOAD), hosted by Athena Research Center, has been promoting open science and running awareness activities since 2009, including joint webinars with the Cypriot NOAD. Athena Research Center and HOSI also convened national Open Science Symposia.

Greece's Law 4310/2014 on Research, Technological Development and Innovation (an explanatory memorandum in English), which forms the national science, technology and innovation policy, reflects open access to scientific knowledge as an element of open science.

The report states that no national-level policy or policy instruments specifically addressing open science are in place. Responsibilities related to open science governance are distributed across three ministries, which the report identifies as a challenge requiring clearer guidelines and stronger coordination. However, a National Plan for Open Science is reported to be under development. At the time of reporting (2025), a draft version, under review by the Ministry of Digital Governance, the Ministry of Education, Religious Affairs and Sports (MERAS) and the General Secretariat for Research and Innovation (GSRI), outlined 62 actions covering open access, research data, software development and e-infrastructures. The GSRI is working to enable HOSI to implement these actions, although these efforts are described as being at a preliminary stage.

 At the institutional level, five universities have adopted open access policies for their affiliated authors, and all universities maintain policies linked to their institutional repositories, ensuring the preservation of open access publications. HOSI coordinates the harmonisation of institutional policy adoption, with the 71st Rectors' Conference having formally recognised open science.

 No dedicated national funding mechanism for open science is reported. The Hellenic Academic Libraries Link (HEAL-Link) negotiates open access agreements with publishers that waive publication fees, funded primarily through national resources. Open science requirements have recently been introduced in funding calls by the Hellenic Foundation for Research and Innovation (HFRI) and the Research-Innovate (Ερευνώ – Καινοτομώ) programme of GSRI, focusing on open access to publications.

 The report indicates that no efforts on equitable public-private partnerships for open science have been undertaken, with such actions expected once open science is established in the public sector.

 A national monitoring framework for open science is under development. Greece contributes to the EOSC Observatory, while HEAL-Link monitors open access through surveys and reports and is developing an OpenAIRE Monitor for its academic community.

National open science infrastructures are reported as under development, covering federated information technology infrastructure and community-based monitoring and information systems. Notably, the Greek Research and Technology Network (GRNET) provides network, cloud computing and high-performance computing services to Greece's educational, academic and research communities. Athena Research Center and GRNET have developed the Hellenic Data Service (HELIX), a national data preservation infrastructure that follows open science and open-source design practices, supports Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable (FAIR) data principles and complies with OpenAIRE interoperability guidelines. The Hellenic Academic Research Data Management Initiative (HARDMIN), a twin infrastructure to HELIX developed by Athena Research Center and managed by HEAL-Link, provides interconnected research data services for Greek universities. ARGOS, OpenAIRE's machine-actionable data management planning service, has been deployed in HEAL-Link's infrastructure and integrated with HARDMIN to automate the data management planning process.

 

Greek nodes of European research infrastructures, such as SoDaNet (Social Data Network, part of CESSDA), ELIXIR-GR (European Life Sciences Infrastructure for Biological Information), DARIAH-GR (Digital Research Infrastructure for the Arts and Humanities), CLARIN:EL  (Common Language Resources and Technology Infrastructure) and EPOS-GR (European Plate Observing System), also contribute domain-specific services for research data management.

 Greece hosts OpenAIRE (Open Access Infrastructure for Research in Europe), a federated international information technology infrastructure for open science established in 2009. Governed as a non-profit organisation with over 50 European partners, it maintains the OpenAIRE Graph and provides services and policy support across the European research ecosystem.

 Both GRNET and OpenAIRE support international collaborations with South-East and North-West regions through their networks.

Capacity building on open science has taken the form of multiple bottom-up initiatives aimed at upskilling researchers, academic librarians and research support staff. Both HEAL-Link and Athena Research Center provide training through webinars and events on research data management, FAIR principles, repository use and compliance with Horizon Europe mandates.
