In 2022, the OpenAIRE NOAD offered a research data management training programme for academic librarians, focused on FAIR data principles and data curation. The HOSI Training Working Group has since coordinated training activities across member institutions.

 The report states that no capacity building programmes for policymakers on integrating open science values into science, technology and innovation policies have taken place, though such activities are being planned.

 A framework of open science competencies has not been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula according to the report. Scattered efforts exist through HOSI members active in European projects; the RAISE Suite, an EOSC project, is expected to develop a course and deliver it at the Aristotle University of Thessaloniki.

 Open educational resources are reported as being used for open science capacity building, though no further details are provided.

 Initiatives to support science communication in line with open science values include an annual "researchers' night" event held across Greek regions, where citizens and students learn about the science behind everyday phenomena and technology.

Initiatives to review research assessment and evaluation processes have been undertaken by individual organisations through the Coalition for Advancing Research Assessment (CoARA). Athena Research Center is the only Greek organisation to have joined CoARA and is working to update its policies to adopt responsible research assessment practices. The National and Kapodistrian University of Athens (NKUA) closely monitors CoARA developments as a signatory. A national CoARA Chapter is being developed, led by Athena Research Center and supported by HOSI, to coordinate institutional engagement in research assessment reform. The Hellenic Authority for Higher Education (HAHE) is reported as aware of open science principles but has not yet integrated them into its monitoring and evaluation processes.

 The report does not indicate that specific clauses recognising open science practices have been incorporated in career evaluation and progression systems, nor that initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices have been undertaken.

 No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.

There are certain initiatives promoting innovative, participatory approaches at different stages of the scientific process. For example, HEAL-Link operates a journal platform based on Open Research Europe that embeds open peer review processes and encourages the early sharing of research outputs through preprints. OpenAIRE's citizen science activities support engagement between researchers and the public; greek research institutions and non-governmental organisations run citizen-driven projects in environmental, health and cultural heritage domains that engage non-academic contributors in data collection, analysis and co-creation of knowledge. At NKUA, preregistration teams encourage students and early-career researchers to preregister study designs and hypotheses before data collection. Open innovation is promoted through public-private partnerships and national initiatives aligned with Horizon Europe that leverage open data and open-source software.

Greece promotes international scientific collaborations on open science primarily through the Research Data Alliance (RDA) and the European Strategy Forum on Research Infrastructures (ESFRI). Members of HOSI actively contribute to RDA working groups, with the Centre for Research and Technology Hellas (CERTH) and Athena Research Center leading at least five groups that shape global practices in FAIR data, reproducibility and research data management. These collaborations feed into the HOSI General Assembly to maintain alignment with international strategies. Greece's participation in ESFRI research infrastructures is described above.

The report does not indicate that Greece has a specific strategy for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science, nor that the country is involved in discussions on regional or international funding mechanisms for open science.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted and shared with the Ministry of Education, Sport and Youth.

 Awareness-raising activities on open science are led primarily by libraries, to which the National Policy for Open Science Practices delegates this responsibility. The University of Cyprus Library, which has been the OpenAIRE’s National Open Access Desk (NOAD) in the country since 2009, in collaboration with the Greek OpenAIRE NOAD, organises regular webinar series covering legal topics, services, infrastructure, policies, citizen science and data management plans. These sessions target researchers, policymakers, funders, librarians and the general public. Beyond webinars, the Library organises annual conferences and face-to-face events.

The national science, technology and innovation policy, including the National Strategy for Research and Innovation 2024-2026, contains elements related to open science, such as open access to scientific knowledge and open science infrastructure.

 As a dedicated national framework for open science implementation is provided by the National Policy for Open Science Practices, adopted in May 2022, which focuses on open access to publications, research data management, open science infrastructure and tools, among other open science aspects. An accompanying Action Plan supports the country's gradual transition to open science, while a National Open Science Initiative has been implemented since 2021 by the Deputy Ministry of Research, Innovation and Digital Policy (DMRID) in collaboration with the Ad Hoc National Open Science Committee.

At the institutional level, several organisations have adopted open science policies. Mandatory policies are reported at institutions including the CYENS Centre of Excellence, the Cyprus Marine and Maritime Institute, the Open University of Cyprus and the ERATOSTHENES Centre of Excellence. Others, such as the University of Cyprus and Neapolis University Pafos, have voluntary policies, while institutions including Frederick University and the Cyprus University of Technology report policies under development.

 Regarding specific national funding mechanisms, costs for open access publications are eligible under national research funding schemes. The report states that open science practices have not been more broadly integrated into existing research funding mechanisms.

 No efforts to foster equitable public-private partnerships on open science are reported.

 A national monitoring framework for open science operates through an annual mapping exercise conducted by the DMRID to track progress of the National Policy for Open Science Practices.

Summary:
National open science infrastructures are under development, including federated information technology infrastructure encompassing high-performance computing, cloud computing and data storage. Several institutional repositories exist (e.g., at the University of Cyprus) but mainly host publications, with few providing services for other open access outputs such as datasets or software. The DMRID has initiated actions for the creation of a national-scale repository, a project expected to be completed within two years.  

Cyprus does not report hosting or funding any federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science, though existing international infrastructures are reported as accessible to the country's open science actors.

 No involvement in North-South or North-South-South collaborations on infrastructure optimisation is reported.

According to the report, systematic capacity building on open science has not taken place in higher education or research performing institutions, as such a systemic approach has not yet been established at most of these institutions. Some partial measures exist, in addition to the awareness- raising activities described above. For example, Cypriot libraries participate in the Consortium and Cyprus Association of Librarians – Information Scientists (CALIS) for Open Science, supervised by the DMRID. Similarly, no framework of open science competencies has been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula, according to the report.

 The report does not indicate that capacity building programmes for policymakers on integrating open science values into science, technology and innovation policies have taken place.

Open educational resources are reported as partly used for open science capacity building.

 Science communication initiatives in line with open science values exist, mainly led by academic institutions. For example, the University of Cyprus participates in the Horizon Europe-funded project Citizen-Enhanced Open Science in Southeastern Europe Higher Education Knowledge Hubs.

Regarding research assessment and evaluation, national academic institutions are making efforts in the context of the Coalition for Advancing Research Assessment (CoARA) initiative.

 The report indicates that specific clauses recognising open science practices have not been incorporated into career evaluation and progression systems, though they are under development in several institutional policies. No initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices are reported, with the report citing a lack of funding.

 No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.

The report states that no initiatives at the national or institutional level specifically promote innovative, participatory methodological or procedural changes to increase openness in the scientific process. At the same time, citizen science is addressed as part of awareness-raising activities, as described above.

Cyprus promotes international scientific collaboration on open science through its participation, as an EU member state, in the European Open Science Cloud (EOSC) and the European Research Area (ERA) Open Science Action.

 The report states that Cyprus does not have a specific strategy or plan for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science, noting that such engagement occurs only through EU-level actions and initiatives.

 Regarding discussions on regional and international funding mechanisms for open science, Cyprus reports involvement through the EOSC.

The report states the 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has not been promoted with relevant ministries and institutions. Planned open science awareness-raising includes academic meetings in schools and communities.

The Education Sector Plan (2017-2025) is cited as the main national policy framework addressing open science; however, no further details are provided. No dedicated national implementation plan for open science is reported. At institutional level, open science policies are reported to be under development.   

 No specific national funding mechanism for open science is reported; nor are equitable public-private partnerships on open science described as being in place.

 Regarding a national monitoring framework for open science, the report indicates that the General Directorate of Planning, Evaluation and the Education System (DGEPASE) handles monitoring, although insufficient funding for data collection is noted; however, it does not clarify whether and how open science is integrated into this monitoring framework.

No national or institutional open science infrastructures are reported (financial constraints are cited as the main barrier). Nor does the country host or fund federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science.

As part of North-South or South-South collaborations for open science infrastructures, the country highlighted two initiatives: the National Education and Research Network of Guinea-Bissau (RNEP-GW) signed a collaboration agreement with Portugal's Foundation for Science and Technology (FCT) in May 2025 to promote technology in higher education and research; and, in the field of education, it collaborates with a UNESCO statistician in Dakar on data collection and the development of indicators that could be relevant to open science.
No systematic capacity building on open science is reported, whether in higher education, for policymakers or through open educational resources, attributed to insufficient funding.

Regarding initiatives to review research assessment, the report states that the DGEPASE's information collection programme aims to identify statistical indicators within the education system and deliver them to the National Institute of Studies and Research (INEP), with a view to exploring how these may support or align with open science.
No any initiatives reported that promote innovative, participatory methodological or procedural changes at different stages of the research cycle to increase openness in the scientific process.

Regarding international cooperation on open science, no further initiatives are reported, beyond those described above in relation to an agreement between RNEP-GW and Portugal’s FCT, and cooperation with a UNESCO statistician in Dakar on data collection and indicator development.   

 

No participation in discussions on regional or international open science funding mechanisms is reported.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted through the Ministry of Science and Technology, the Ministry of Education, the China Association for Science and Technology (CAST), the Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC). Non-governmental bodies involved include the Open Science Promotion Consortium (OSPC), the Alliance of National and International Science Organizations for the Belt and Road Regions (ANSO) and the CAST UN Consultative Committee on Open Science and Global Partnership (CCOS).

 

Awareness-raising activities span conferences, multi-stakeholder networks and annual events. In August 2022, CAST initiated the OSPC, a body comprising academic institutions, scientific societies, international publishers, Chinese publishing houses and information service institutions. Since its establishment, the OSPC has held council meetings and seminars on topics such as open access ecosystem development and green open access. Other relevant activities include the Hangzhou Declaration, released at the Sixteenth General Conference of The World Academy of Sciences (TWAS), and the 2024 World Science and Technology Development Forum's thematic session on open science infrastructures. The 758th Session of the Xiangshan Science Conference in November 2024, jointly organised by CCOS and the National Science Library of CAS, produced the Xiangshan Initiative on Open Science. That same month, the National Science Library of CAS hosted the China Open Access Week 2024.

China's national science, technology and innovation policy framework is reported to include elements related to open science, such as open access to scientific knowledge and open science infrastructure. As of 2023, this is reflected, in particular, in the Notice on Guiding Opinions on Accelerating the Establishment of a National Science and Technology Reporting System (2014), issued by the State Council; the Measures for the Management of Scientific Data (2018), issued by the State Council to standardise scientific data management and balance data security with open sharing; the Administrative Measures for the National Scientific and Technological Resource Sharing Service Platform (2019), issued by the Ministry of Science and Technology; and the Administrative Measures for the Open Sharing of National Major Scientific Research Infrastructure and Large Scientific Research Facilities, which establish requirements for the shared use of publicly funded research infrastructure and equipment.

 

China reported the existence of institutional open science policies; however, the report did not cite specific policy documents.  

 

The report indicates that open science is integrated into existing research funding schemes. The Regulations on the National Natural Science Fund, issued by the State Council, include provisions requiring an institution responsible for managing fund grants to support open access to research results from funded projects. Building on this framework, the NSFC has adopted a policy mandating open access to publications arising from its funded projects.

 

Equitable public-private partnerships on open science are pursued through the OSPC, which brings together domestic and international research, publishing and information service organisations.

 

A national monitoring framework for open science takes the form of the Open Science Readiness Digital Twin Dashboard, developed by CCOS and the National Science Library of CAS.

China hosts a range of national and institutional open science infrastructures, all reported as non-commercial. Among them, for example, the National Advanced Platform for S&T information and Communication (NAPSTIC), which is established by the Institute of Scientific and Technical Information of China (ISTIC) and serves as an information portal on researchers, research outputs and other resources within the national science, technology and innovation system; national thematic data centres, such as the National Tibetan Plateau Data Center; shared large-scale research infrastructures, such as the Five-hundred-meter Aperture Spherical Radio Telescope (FAST); and open science services developed by CAS, including its institutional repository, the ChinaXiv preprint server and the GoOA platform for discovering high-quality open access journals and papers.

 
China also reports hosting federated international open science infrastructure: for example, the Institute of Microbiology of CAS hosts the World Data Centre for Microorganisms (WDCM). It operates under the World Federation for Culture Collections of the International Union of Biological Sciences and the Microbial Resources Centers (MIRCEN) of UNESCO, and is open to all users regardless of region, serving a global community of microbiologists and data scientists.
 
As an international collaboration to optimise infrastructure use, the report highlights a recent joint initiative involving ANSO, CAS, UNESCO and the African Academy of Sciences to establish the African Open Science and Innovation Hub, aimed at facilitating collaboration between ANSO members and Global South countries, with a budget of $1 million allocated for 2025.

Efforts to support systematic capacity building on open science within research institutions have focused in particular on open access publishing standards. For example, the China Association of University Science and Technology Periodicals has contributed to the development of national open access guidelines for domestic scientific journals. The National Science Library of CAS complements these efforts through the GoOA platform, which applies evaluation standards for open access journals and supports their dissemination and discoverability.
 
The report identifies the National Science and Technology Infrastructure Center and the National Data Administration as key actors supporting open science capacity building for policymakers, through their roles in managing national resource-sharing services and national data systems.
 
According to the report, open science competencies have been incorporated in higher education research curricula, with reference to data science programmes at Fudan University and a multidisciplinary big data master's programme at Tsinghua University. However, the report does not clarify whether open science is explicitly integrated into these programmes.
 
China uses open educational resources to support open science capacity building, as illustrated by the Open Science Microvideo Series launched by the National Science Library of CAS.
 
Regarding science communication aligned with open science values, the report refers to the revised Law on Science and Technology Popularisation (2024), which mandates an open and shared national repository of science popularisation resources and supports volunteer science communication services. The China Science Communication platform is cited as a specific example of such a national resource.

With regard to the review and reform of research assessment and evaluation, the General Office of the State Council issued the Guiding Opinions on Improving the Evaluation Mechanism for Scientific and Technological Achievements (2021), which aim to enhance the openness and transparency of evaluation activities and call for the disclosure of information on non-confidential, publicly funded scientific and technological achievements.
 
Regarding specific clauses recognising open science practices in career evaluation and progression systems, the report confirms their availability, citing the Law on Scientific and Technological Progress, which encourages open sharing of research data and societal application of research outcomes, and the Several Opinions on Further Strengthening the Construction of Scientific Research Integrity, which requires researchers to publicly disclose research data and methodologies.
 
Initiatives to reward open science practices include the Regulations on Post-Subsidy Management for National Science and Technology Programs and Special Funds, jointly issued by the Ministry of Finance and the Ministry of Science and Technology, which encourage stakeholders to establish reward policies for open access to scientific knowledge.
 
The report does not indicate any unintended negative consequences of open science practices.

China reports multiple innovative, participatory initiatives at different stages of the research cycle to increase the openness of the entire scientific process. For example, preprint dissemination is supported by three platforms: ChinaXiv, developed by CAS; the national preprint server (as part of the National Advanced Platform for S&T information and Communication, NAPSTIC); and biomedRxiv, managed by the Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences. Open peer review practices are explored through China Scientific Data, a bilingual journal that publishes data papers and promotes open data sharing in accordance with FAIR principles.

 

Among participatory initiatives engaging societal actors, the report cites two citizen science projects: the Public Supernova Search Project of the National Astronomical Observatories, CAS, which invites public participation in the search for and study of supernovae; and the Bird Monitoring Project of the China Birdwatching Record Centre, which encourages public participation in bird monitoring and the collection of data to support conservation efforts. To address the needs of under-represented groups, CAST, the Ministry of Science and Technology and eleven other government departments jointly issued the Measures to Support Women in Science and Technology to Play a Greater Role in Innovation (2021).

China promotes international scientific collaboration on open science through multiple channels. At the 2024 G20 Leaders' Summit, the African Union, Brazil, China and South Africa put forward the Initiative on International Cooperation in Open Science. In addition, through ANSO, China supports several initiatives, including the African Open Science and Innovation Hub (see above), as well as joint research and training activities with institutions in countries along the Belt and Road, such as the Open Science for Sustainable Development project and annual open science training courses. The OSPC also facilitates international collaboration through its network of members, which includes international publishers and the STM association.

 

Regarding discussions on international funding mechanisms for open science, the report cites China's role in establishing the Consortium for Reliability and Reproducibility (CoRR), an international initiative to strengthen the reliability and reproducibility of neuroimaging research.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has not been formally promoted to ministries and institutions, according to the report. The Research, Development and Innovation (R&D&I) Council, a government advisory body, did however approve in 2024 a document titled "Recommendations for R&D&I Financial Support Providers on the application of open science practices in the context of national programmes and public competitions in research, development and innovation", which cites the 2021 Recommendation as a foundational document.

 Awareness-raising activities on open science take several forms. Research institutions regularly participate in Open Science Week, organised by the Association of Libraries of Czech Universities. These efforts are further supported by organisations such as the European Open Science Cloud Czech Republic (EOSC CZ) Training Centre, as well as open science centres at universities including Masaryk University and Charles University, and the National Library of Technology.

Czechia's national science, technology and innovation policy, the National Research, Development and Innovation Policy 2021+, includes open science elements such as open access to scientific knowledge and open science infrastructure.

National-level policies partly address open science. Act No. 130/2002 Coll. on the support of research and development, amended in 2022, requires funder-beneficiary contracts to include data management plans and information on the availability of research results and data (§ 9(1), points l) and m)). The R&D&I Council's 2024 Recommendations for Financial Support Providers further set out open science practices for national programmes.

Specific policy instruments to promote open science are under development. The former National Strategy for Open Access to Scientific Information (2017–2020), which covered publications and data (on a non-mandatory basis), has expired without a successor action plan. A new national open science strategy was reported to be under preparation in 2025.

Institutional open science policies exist at multiple research performing institutions, including the Charles University Research Data Policy, the Masaryk University Open Science Strategy 2022–2028 and the Open Science Principles of the Czech Academy of Sciences.

A specific national funding mechanism was implemented through the Operational Programme Johannes Amos Comenius: Open Science I and II calls, which support development of the National Repository Platform as part of the National Data Infrastructure for managing and sharing research data in line with Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable (FAIR) principles. Research funders also enable coverage of open access publishing costs and data steward positions from project funds. All major grant providers have integrated open science principles into their conditions, including the Czech Science Foundation, the Technology Agency of the Czech Republic (KAPPA Programme) and the Agency for Health Research of the Czech Republic.

The report does not describe efforts towards equitable public-private partnerships on open science, though the topic is expected to be addressed in the forthcoming national open science strategy.

A national monitoring framework for open science operates through the Information System of Research, Experimental Development and Innovation (IS VaVaI), where the access status (open, embargoed or restricted) is required to be recorded for all journal articles.

National open science infrastructure in Czechia is centred on e-INFRA CZ, a non-commercial e-infrastructure for research and development that provides high-performance computing capacities for the transfer, storage and processing of scientific data across all domains; its core activities are shifting towards adherence to FAIR principles. Under the national EOSC development plan, a large number of research institutions are currently developing the National Data Infrastructure, with the National Repository Platform at its centre. This infrastructure will encompass services for persistent identifiers, authentication and authorisation, data management planning and scientific data collaboration, and will form part of the e-INFRA CZ environment.

 With regard to hosting or funding federated international open science infrastructure, the report cites several resources. The Central European Institute of Technology (CEITEC) Nano Research Infrastructure in Brno provides open access to the facilities and equipment in nanotechnology and materials science for domestic researchers and from abroad. The Data Stewardship Wizard, a data management planning tool developed in Czechia, has grown in international use and is recommended by the Horizon Europe Programme Guide.

 Czechia does not report involvement in North-South or North-South-South collaborations on open science infrastructure.

Systematic capacity building on open science has taken place in higher education and research performing institutions. For example, since 2023, the EOSC CZ Training Centre has cooperated with research institutions to deliver tailor-made courses on data management and open science. In addition, dedicated open science centres exist at universities such as Masaryk University, Charles University and Silesian University in Opava. An expanding data steward community also supports research data management throughout the research cycle.

 Regarding capacity building for policymakers, the report notes that the open science environment is fragmented and that such activity is carried out by a limited number of institutions.

 Open science competencies have been incorporated into higher education curricula at individual institutions. Palacký University Olomouc offers ten modules for staff and doctoral students on research data management, open science and related topics. Charles University is incorporating these competencies through the Marie Skłodowska-Curie Actions Co-funding (MSCA COFUND) project Charleston for postdoctoral researchers. The University of Chemistry and Technology Prague includes open science elements in its mandatory Chemical Informatics course for bachelor's students.

 Open educational resources are reported as being used partly for open science capacity building. The Technology Agency of the Czech Republic funded the project Quality Assurance of Open Educational Resources (2019–2021), which produced a certified methodology and a freely available online course for creating such resources.

 Science communication initiatives aligned with open science values include the National Library of Technology's Doctoral Summer School and the annual Researchers' Night event, during which institutions open their doors to the public.

The report states that no information is available about initiatives at the national or institutional level promoting innovative, participatory methodological or procedural changes to increase the openness of the scientific process.

Regarding the review of research assessment and evaluation, the national Methodology 25+ for evaluating research organisations in the higher education segment includes open science as an indicator of their sustainability and resilience. As noted above, major research funders have embedded open science practices in their requirements, either on a recommended or a mandatory basis. Several institutions have signed the Agreement on Reforming Research Assessment and joined the Coalition for Advancing Research Assessment (CoARA), including Brno University of Technology, Charles University and Palacký University Olomouc.

 The report states that no widely applicable framework exists for incorporating open science practices into career evaluation and progression systems, though such practices are expected to be integrated into these systems over time.

 Unintended negative consequences of open science reported include rising publication costs and high article processing charges that disproportionately affect less-funded researchers. Predatory journals persist, and the report considers questionable practices by established publishers a larger concern. Researchers also often perceive open science as additional work for limited benefit, as evaluation schemes do not yet sufficiently recognise these efforts. Data migration risks and the potential privatisation of publicly funded research data are also cited.

In international collaboration on open science, the Open Access Initiative of the Czech Republic was established as early as 2010 within the Association of Libraries of Czech Universities as an informal group of academic librarians working on open access to scientific information and open repositories. The Association signed the Berlin Declaration in 2012 and has been a member of Scholarly Publishing and Academic Resources Coalition Europe (SPARC Europe) since 2015. In 2021, the Open Science Working Group was established to continue this work and monitor global trends in the field.

 Czechia does not report having a specific strategy for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science, noting that a new national open science strategy under preparation may address this.

 The report states that Czechia is not involved in discussions on regional or international funding mechanisms for open science, noting that the open science environment is fragmented and requires unification at the national level.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted through the Ministry of Education and Higher Education and higher education institutions.

The report confirms the availability of awareness-raising activities on open science; however, only two infrastructures were cited in this regard: the Palestinian Research Gate/Portal and the Palestinian National Research and Education Network (PALNREN) (see details below in the section on open science infrastructures).

Palestine's national science, technology and innovation policy was reported to contain certain elements related to open science, such as open engagement of societal actors and open dialogue with other knowledge systems.

The report states there is no national policy or policy instruments specifically addressing open science, citing lack of awareness among policymakers, insufficient funding and the absence of a supporting legal framework as obstacles. However, a national implementation plan for open science is reported as under development, coordinated by the Palestinian National Commission for Education, Culture and Science through multi-stakeholder consultations, with a recommendation to establish a national open science working group and mechanisms for international collaboration.
 
 Palestine reports the absence of institutional-level open science policies.

 No specific national funding mechanisms for open science are reported, with limited budget allocations and lack of awareness cited as barriers.

 No efforts to foster equitable public-private partnerships are reported, with limited open science awareness in both sectors and the absence of regulatory frameworks cited as obstacles. Nor is a national monitoring framework for open science reported.

The report indicates the availability of several national and institutional open science infrastructures in Palestine, as illustrated below. The Palestinian National Research and Education Network (PALNREN) provides local researchers with a high-speed communication network, as well as high-performance computing and cloud services. The Palestinian Research Gate/Portal is a national platform, providing an interactive digital environment that supports researchers in managing, sharing and collaborating on their research, and facilitates access to research resources and advanced technologies. For example, this platform offers a single interface for accessing institutional open access repositories, including those at the Islamic University of Gaza, An-Najah National University, Birzeit University, Palestine Polytechnic University and Al-Quds Open University, among others.

 Palestine does not report hosting or funding any federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science. However, existing open science infrastructures at regional and international levels are reported as accessible to relevant actors in the country.

 No involvement in North-South, North-South-South or South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use is reported.

Systematic capacity building on open science in higher education and research institutions has not been properly established, according to the report. Reported obstacles include the absence of a national policy mandating open science practices, funding constraints for training and infrastructure, and the need for cultural shifts from traditional publishing models. Nor have capacity-building programmes for policymakers  been established; however, efforts are under way to engage with UNESCO and the Arab League Educational, Cultural and Scientific Organization (ALECSO) to explore training programmes aimed at equipping policymakers with the knowledge and skills needed to integrate open science into national science, technology and innovation strategies.

 Open educational resources as defined in the 2019 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Educational Resources are not reported as being used for open science capacity building.

 The report confirms the availability of initiatives supporting science communication in line with open science values, referring in particular to the Palestinian Research Gate/Portal as a platform facilitating the dissemination of scientific knowledge.

No initiatives to review research assessment and evaluation processes to align them with open science principles are reported at either national or institutional level. The report cites the prioritisation of traditional metrics such as journal impact factors and citation counts, the absence of institutional policies integrating open science into assessment frameworks, and resource and awareness constraints as obstacles.

 No specific clauses recognising open science practices have been incorporated into career evaluation and progression systems, according to the report. No initiatives to recognise or reward open science practices are reported.

 No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.

No initiatives promoting innovative, participatory methodological or procedural changes at different stages of the research cycle to increase the openness of the scientific process are reported at either national or institutional level.

Palestine promotes international scientific collaboration on open science through several channels. For example, it engages with UNESCO and other international organisations to exchange knowledge on open science developments. Palestine is a member of the Arab States Research and Education Network (ASREN), which provides digital infrastructure and collaborative opportunities with regional and international partners.

No specific strategy or plan for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science is reported.

 Regarding regional and international funding mechanisms for open science, the report describes Palestine's involvement in the EU co-funded EUMEDplus project, coordinated by the Gigabit European Academic Network (GÉANT) and launched in 2024. With a four-year timeline and a budget of approximately €13.3 million, the project aims to strengthen the capabilities of national research and education networks in the EU's Southern Neighbourhood and promote the adoption of open science practices.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been shared with universities, which the report identifies as the country's major research performing organisations. Awareness-raising activities on open science have been conducted by the National Open Science Cloud Initiative (NOSCI.mk) and the Center for Open Science at Ss. Cyril and Methodius University in Skopje (UKIM).

North Macedonia does not report having a national-level policy or legal framework addressing open science. This is expected to be developed after the introduction of new laws on higher education and scientific work. A national implementation plan or roadmap for open science is reported as under development, contingent on the same forthcoming legislation.

 At the institutional level, open science policies are in place at UKIM, both at the university level and across its faculties (e.g., the Institute of Economics has adopted a Policy for Open Access to Research Infrastructures).

 The report indicates that open science is integrated in existing research funding mechanisms, noting that the Ministry of Education and Science requires that excellent research publications it funds to be made openly available through institutional repositories; however, no additional details are reported.

No efforts to foster equitable public-private partnerships on open science are reported. Nor is national monitoring framework for open science reported.

Among available open science infrastructures, the report highlights the Open Cloud Research Laboratory (OSCLab), a national open research e-infrastructure based at the UKIM Faculty of Computer Science and Engineering, providing high-performance computing and data storage for researchers across the country. In addition, it refers to a single point of access to all university repositories.

The country does not report hosting or funding any federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science. Existing regional and international open science infrastructures are, however, reported as accessible to relevant actors in the country.

As international collaborations on open science infrastructure, the report cites participation in GÉANT (the pan-European research and education network), EOSC (the European Open Science Cloud) Association and OpenAIRE (the Open Access Infrastructure for Research in Europe).

The report does not describe systematic capacity building on open science in higher education or research performing institutions, nor capacity building programmes for policymakers on integrating open science values into science, technology and innovation policies and strategies. Not has a framework of open science competencies been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula.

 The report nevertheless notes that open educational resources are used for open science capacity building.

 Regarding science communication in line with open science values, the report cites the activities of NOSCI.mk.

The report does not describe any initiatives at the national or institutional level to review research assessment and evaluation processes in line with open science principles. No specific clauses recognising open science practices have been incorporated in career evaluation and progression systems.

 No initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices are reported.

 No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.

The reports indicates the existence of institutional initiatives promoting innovative, participatory methodological or procedural changes at different stages of the research cycle to increase the openness of the scientific process; however, no further details are provided.

The report does not describe the country’s involvement in international cooperation on open science beyond that related to open science infrastructures mentioned above. No specific strategy or plan for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science is reported. Nor does the report indicate the involvement in discussions on the establishment of regional or international funding mechanisms for open science.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted among relevant ministries and institutions. The Ministry of Science and Technology (MoST) and the Higher Education Commission (HEC) are identified as the main entities promoting open science, while the Pakistan Science Foundation (PSF) and the Pakistan Academy of Sciences (PAS) are reported as aligning with global open science standards. 

 

Efforts to raise awareness on open science have taken place, mainly coordinated by HEC, PSF and MoST. In 2022, the Organisation of Islamic Cooperation's Standing Committee on Scientific and Technological Cooperation (COMSTECH), headquartered in Pakistan, co-organised a webinar on open science with UNESCO, contributing to national awareness-raising efforts and including a speaker from the Pakistan Academy of Sciences.

The report does not describe any national policy or legal framework clearly addressing open science. It also states that no national implementation plan or roadmap for open science currently exists; its development through multi-stakeholder consultations is anticipated. No specific policy instrument exclusively promoting open science is reported either, nor are institutional open science policy documents indicated.

However, national research funding programmes managed by HEC and PSF are reported to support open science elements, such as open access publishing, open data sharing and collaborative research. These include, for example, the following HEC-managed programmes: the National Research Program for Universities, the Competitive Research Program and the Grand Challenge Fund. PSF has also implemented awards for female, young and senior scientists and science communicators, thereby supporting equity and inclusivity in science. 

 

Efforts to foster equitable public-private partnerships (PPP) in support of open science are reported to be underway, including the strengthening of the legal and institutional framework enabling PPPs, multi-stakeholder initiatives such as the National Open Data Portal as well as funding mechanisms supporting collaborative research and open innovation, such as Ignite's Challenge-driven Innovation Fund.  

 

The report states that no formal national monitoring framework for open science exists, nor are specific open science indicators included in the science, technology and innovation policy monitoring framework.

As an example of national open science infrastructure, the report refers to the National Open Data Portal, launched in 2025 by the National Center for Big Data and Cloud Computing (NCBC) at the private Lahore University of Management Sciences (LUMS) in collaboration with HEC, which provides open access to over 1,100 datasets across sectors such as health, education and environment. In addition, the Pakistan Research Repository, implemented by HEC to provide open access to PhD theses, is cited, along with institutional repositories in selected universities. 

 

The report indicates that Pakistan does not host or fund any federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science. 

 

Pakistan reports involvement in North-South, North-South-South and South-South collaborations with relevance to open science infrastructure, notably through participation, via PSF, in a 2025 multilateral joint call for research and development projects under the Shanghai Cooperation Organization, which is intended to foster shared infrastructure use and collaborative open science strategies among member countries, focusing on priority areas such as climate change and nanotechnology.

Systematic capacity building on open science in higher education is supported through the National Academy of Higher Education (NAHE), designated by HEC as the national hub for professional development in higher education. NAHE provides training programmes, workshops and certifications for faculty and administrators, increasingly incorporating components relevant to open science such as research transparency, data sharing and open access publishing. 

 

The report states that no capacity building programmes for policymakers on integrating open science values into science, technology and innovation policies have been undertaken. No formal framework of open science competencies has been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula, though efforts by HEC and PSF are reported to be underway.  

 

Open educational resources are gradually being integrated into open science capacity building activities, particularly those led by higher education institutions. 

 

Pakistan reports the existence of initiatives supporting science communication in line with open science values, including the Journal of Science Communication and Diplomacy, launched by COMSTECH and the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

The report states that no formal review of research assessment and evaluation processes for alignment with open science principles has been undertaken at the national or institutional level. HEC’s  Journal Recognition System and Research Excellence Framework are driven by citations and impact indicators and are not designed to promote open science practices. 

 

No specific clauses recognising open science practices are reported to have been incorporated into career evaluation and progression systems. Similarly, no widely established initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices are reported. 

 

Regarding unintended negative consequences of open science practices, concerns have emerged around intellectual property and data security, quality assurance and the digital divide, although no supporting evidence has been documented at the national level.

Pakistan reports the existence of initiatives promoting innovative, participatory methodological or procedural changes at different stages of the research cycle to increase the openness of the scientific process, such as HEC’s efforts to promote academia-industry-government linkages and encourage collaborative research and innovation.

Pakistan promotes international collaboration on open science through several initiatives. For example, HEC facilitates partnerships between Pakistani institutions and international universities, industries and research organisations for joint research and data sharing. As noted above, collaboration also takes place through the Shanghai Cooperation Organization and COMSTECH.

 

However, the report states that Pakistan does not yet have a formal strategy for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science. It also does not describe the country’s involvement in discussions on the establishment of regional or international funding mechanisms for open science.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science was disseminated to relevant national stakeholders, with a request to implement its provisions and report on progress at stakeholder meetings. Recipients include the Ministry of Tertiary Education, Science and Research, higher education institutions such as the University of Mauritius and the Open University of Mauritius, and bodies including the Higher Education Commission (HEC), the Mauritius Research and Innovation Council (MRIC), the Mauritius Institute of Education and the Rajiv Gandhi Science Centre. 

 

Awareness-raising activities on open science are reported to have taken place, including the annual National Science Week and community engagement initiatives; however, no further details are provided.

The National Roadmap for Research and Innovation of MRIC provides strategic direction for science, technology and innovation and incorporates certain elements related to open science, such as open access to scientific knowledge and open dialogue with other knowledge systems. 

 

The report states that no national policy, legal framework, specific policy instruments or national implementation plan for open science currently exist. Coordination across higher education institutions and the need for prior discussion of intellectual property rights implications are identified as obstacles. However, Mauritius has adopted a National Open Educational Resources (OER) Policy, which provides direction for using OER in the education and training system and is monitored by HEC.  

 

Reported dedicated funding mechanisms relevant to open science include measures under the OER Policy that guide publicly funded educational institutions in allocating resources to open educational initiatives. Open science practices are also integrated into established research funding schemes: for example, the MRIC publishes all funded project reports on the publicly accessible Mauritius Research Repository, and the University of Mauritius requires commitments to open access publishing and data sharing for internal funding applications. 

 

Efforts to promote equitable public-private partnerships on open science are noted, though the report describes them as ad hoc and not yet structured. A national monitoring framework for open science is reported to be under development.

The report states that no comprehensive national open science infrastructure exists in Mauritius, citing insufficient financial investment, regulatory barriers to public-private collaboration and lack of coordination as contributing factors. Some digital resources are nonetheless in place, including the Mauritius Research Repository established by MRIC, which provides open access to research outputs related to the Republic of Mauritius or produced by authors of Mauritian origin, as well as the Research Management Information System of the University of Mauritius. 

 

Regarding federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science, the report notes that Mauritius does not currently host or fund such infrastructure, though some international databases are accessible to researchers in Mauritius. 

 

Mauritius participates in several North-South and South-South collaborations related to shared open science infrastructures through regional and international networks, including H3BIONET (Pan African Bioinformatics Network for H3Africa), RUFORUM (Regional Universities Forum for Capacity Building in Agriculture) and PreRAD (Partnerships for Research and Development).

The report states that no systematic capacity building on open science has taken place in higher education or research performing institutions, citing inadequate infrastructure and strategic support as obstacles, and that capacity-building programmes for policymakers are also not established.

 

Open science competencies have not been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula according to the report.

 

Despite the availability of the National OER Policy, open educational resources are not reported to be used for open science capacity building. 

 

The report does not describe any initiatives to support science communication in line with open science values.

Mauritius reports that initiatives to review research assessment and evaluation processes are in place, including the assessment of research project sustainability, and that the integration of open science principles into these processes is under consideration. In particular, he HEC has reviewed its Research Publication Incentive Scheme to recognise publications in open access journals. 

 

The report does not describe specific clauses recognising open science practices in career evaluation and progression systems. 

 

The report indicates that no unintended negative consequences of open science practices have been identified.

Initiatives promoting innovative, participatory approaches to increase openness at different stages of the scientific process are reported at both national and institutional levels. Specifically, the report identifies the promotion of preprints, the exploration of open peer review practices and participatory methods engaging societal actors as areas of action. For example, in some research projects, various stakeholders are actively involved, contributing to the research process and ensuring that diverse perspectives are considered.

Mauritius recognises the importance of fostering international collaboration in open science, particularly within regional thematic research networks such as H3BIONET (see above), which may facilitate knowledge exchange, capacity building and the shared use of open science infrastructures. The report describes ongoing discussions on the potential development of a strategy to stimulate cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration.  

 

The report further indicates that Mauritius is not involved in discussions on the establishment of regional or international funding mechanisms for open science, citing limited awareness of relevant initiatives as a contributing factor.

The Ministry of Education, Science and Technology (MoEST) has promoted the 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science among relevant ministries and institutions through workshops and seminars.  

 

Awareness-raising activities on open science vary across institutions. In 2023, the Tanzania Commission for Science and Technology (COSTECH) co-organised a National Open Science Dialogue to explore the challenges and opportunities of establishing an open science ecosystem in Tanzania, identifying the need for a national open science policy to guide research dissemination, data sharing and the incentivisation of open science practices. In December 2024, MoEST conducted an awareness-raising seminar for staff of universities and Technical and Vocational Education and Training (TVET) institutions. The Nelson Mandela African Institution of Science and Technology (NM-AIST), the Mining Commission, the Tanzania Fisheries Research Institute (TAFIRI), the Tanzania Gemological Centre (TGC) and the Tanzania Meteorological Authority (TMA) conducted internal workshops and training sessions on open science.

The report states that there is no national-level policy or legal framework addressing open science, no specific policy instruments to promote open science, and no national implementation plan or roadmap for open science. Nevertheless, it points to the National Data Sharing Framework for Science, Technology and Innovation, developed by COSTECH, which provides guidance on data sharing within the science, technology and innovation ecosystem. 

 

At the institutional level, some institutions have policies that generally align with open science principles, though standalone open science policies remain limited. For example, Kampala International University in Tanzania (KIUT) has a research policy centred on community involvement and participatory research.  

 

No dedicated national funding mechanism for open science is reported. Some institutions have nonetheless integrated open science practices into existing research funding mechanisms. For example, COSTECH's funding guidelines require researchers to disseminate findings through open access platforms, as well as research funded by the European Union at KIUT is subject to open science requirements as a condition of funding. 

 

Regarding public-private partnerships on open science, TGC and COSTECH report memoranda of understanding and collaboration agreements with private-sector organisations. COSTECH's strategic plan calls for increased public-private partnership engagement in sceince, technology and innovation. 

 

No national monitoring framework for open science is reported.

Certain open science infrastructures have been developed, some of which are of national relevance. COSTECH and TAFIRI have established such infrastructures, including high-performance computing facilities. COSTECH's Higher Education and Research Infrastructure Network (HERIN) provides high-speed internet connectivity for higher education and research institutions. TAFIRI has developed the Electronic Catch Assessment System (eCAS) to support fisheries data collection and sharing. 

 

Regarding federated regional or international information technology infrastructure, the Tanzania Atomic Energy Commission (TAEC) contributes to the International Nuclear Information System (INIS) under the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA). 

 

Some institutions such as COSTECH and TMA participate in North-South and South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use. For example, TMA collaborates with the World Meteorological Organization (WMO) and the Coordinated Regional Climate Downscaling Experiment (CORDEX) Africa initiative in this context.

Systematic capacity building on open science in higher education and research institutions has been implemented, for example by  COSTECH and KIUT, including workshops and training sessions.  However, the report indicates the absence of  dedicated capacity-building programmes for policymakers. 

 

A few institutions have incorporated open science competencies into higher education curricula, including KIUT, while the University of Dar es Salaam (UDSM) plans to do so through the Erasmus+ Capacity Building in Higher Education (CBHE) project. 

 

The use of open educational resources for open science capacity building is reported as limited, with emerging recognition of their potential at institutions such as Mwalimu Nyerere University of Agriculture and Technology (MNUAT) and the University of Iringa. 

 

The report indicates that science communication initiatives in line with open science values have taken place but provides no further details.

At the institutional level, KIUT has reviewed its research assessment and evaluation processes to align with open science principles.

  

The report also indicates that specific clauses recognising open science practices have been incorporated into career evaluation and progression systems in Tanzania, although no further details are provided. 

 

Reported reward mechanisms for open science appear limited to a small number of institutions (such as KIUT and TAFIRI) rather than reflecting a system-wide approach. 

 

No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.

The report indicates that initiatives promoting innovative, participatory methodological or procedural changes at different stages of the research cycle have been undertaken, although no further details are provided.

Tanzania is promoting international collaborations on open science. For example, COSTECH is leading discussions and preparatory efforts towards the development of a national open science policy, working with the Consortium of Tanzania Universities and Research Libraries (COTUL) and Electronic Information for Libraries (EIFL). Tanzania has also participated in East African Community (EAC) initiatives on open science. 

 

The report states, however, that Tanzania does not have a specific strategy or plan for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science.  

 

Tanzania is engaged in discussions on the establishment of regional and international funding mechanisms for open science. The above-mentioned 2023 National Open Science Dialogue, co-organised by COSTECH together with the East African Science and Technology Commission (EASTECO), the Public Library of Science (PLOS) and the Training Centre in Communication Africa, explored avenues for securing funding from international partners. Tanzania also participates in the African Union (AU), the EAC and the Southern African Development Community (SADC) in this context.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted as part of the development of the National Open Research Landscape Report (2021) and the National Action Plan for Open Research 2022-2030, both led by the National Open Research Forum (NORF). NORF is a Government of Ireland initiative funded by the Department of Further and Higher Education, Research, Innovation and Science (DFHERIS) through the Higher Education Authority (HEA) and supported by the Royal Irish Academy and the Digital Repository of Ireland (DRI). It brings together representatives from policy, research funding, research performing organisations, the library sector, research infrastructures and enterprise.

Awareness-raising activities address all elements of open science, including initiatives such as the annual National Open Research Festival, organised by NORF at the Royal Irish Academy, and the National Forum on Research Integrity, established in 2015 and coordinated by the Irish Universities Association (IUA) and the Technological Higher Education Association (THEA).

Ireland's national science, technology and innovation policy, Impact 2030: Ireland's Research and Innovation Strategy (2022) incorporates open science elements, such as open access to scientific knowledge, open engagement of societal actors and open dialogue with other knowledge systems. It devolves the open science agenda to NORF.

 The National Action Plan for Open Research 2022-2030, developed by NORF through public consultation and endorsed by 57 entities, is Ireland's dedicated national open science policy and implementation plan. Its three strategic objectives are to establish a culture of open research, achieve 100% open access to research publications and enable FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable) research data and other outputs.

 

In addition, several complementary national-level policy instruments further support the implementation of open science, including the HEA Principles of Good Practice in Research within Higher Education Institutions, the National Policy Statement on Ensuring Research Integrity in Ireland and the National Persistent Identifier Strategy and Roadmap.

 At institutional level, all publicly funded universities have endorsed the National Action Plan for Open Research. However, none have adopted a standalone open science (open research) policy, with the Technological University Network (TU-Net) Joint Statement on Open Research reflecting coordination among five technological universities rather than institutional policy. Instead, universities primarily operate with open access and research data management policies, both of which are widely present, with research data management more consistently formalised as standalone policies, while open access is more often embedded within broader strategies or shaped by funder requirements.


The National Open Research Fund, totalling €4.33 million since 2022 and managed by NORF through DRI, constitutes a dedicated national funding mechanism and serves as an instrument for implementing specific goals outlined in the National Action Plan for Open Research. In addition, the Irish Research eLibrary (IReL), funded by HEA and the Department of Further and Higher Education, Research, Innovation and Science, has supported the publication of almost 10,000 open access research articles from member institutions between 2021 and 2023. Open science has also been integrated into existing research funding schemes: agencies including the Health Research Board, Research Ireland and the Marine Institute include open science in their policies, assessment and reporting.

 The report describes efforts toward equitable public-private partnerships on open science as partial. The NORF-funded Review of Open Research in Connected Health Ecosystems in Ireland is examining open research in public-private partnerships, due to conclude in the first quarter of 2026.

 A national monitoring framework is under development. NORF and IReL launched the National Open Access Monitor  in March 2024 in a pilot phase, with plans to expand its scope during 2025-2027.

The National Open Research Landscape Report (2021), prepared by NORF, maps national open science infrastructure under four categories – networking and computing, meta-infrastructures, data infrastructures and thematic infrastructures – and provides examples of infrastructures within each category. An inventory of Irish open access repositories is available via the University of Galway. Several infrastructures have been developed through projects funded by the National Open Research Fund; for example, the Open Access Transition Programme is conducting a feasibility study and pilot to establish a publicly owned national platform for diamond open access publication of journals and books.
 
Ireland reports as hosting or funding federated international open science infrastructure. The National Open Research Landscape Report details national nodes for several European Research Infrastructure Consortiums (ERICs) and European Strategy Forum on Research Infrastructures (ESFRI) bodies. In addition, Ireland is represented in the European Open Science Cloud (EOSC) Association, with the EOSC-Ireland structure established to connect national digital services to, and enable their contribution to, the EOSC ecosystem.

The report states that Ireland is not involved in North-South or North-South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use or develop joint strategies for shared open science platforms.

Systematic capacity building on open science has taken place in higher education and research performing institutions through activities funded by NORF. For example, the National Data Stewardship Network aims to support the development of data stewardship skills across the national research landscape, contributing to Ireland’s transition to FAIR research data management.

 Capacity building for policymakers operates through NORF, whose membership includes policy representatives, and through the National Forum on Research Integrity.

 The report indicates that a framework of open science competencies is being incorporated into higher education research skills curricula through the NORF-funded Open Research Training Programme (TROPIC), led by Maynooth University. The programme is expected to develop and share training resources as open educational resources.

 Science communication initiatives in line with open science values include Campus Engage, a national initiative working with 20 higher education institutions to embed civic and community engagement including community-based research. The NORF-funded Public Engagement and Open Research project is co-creating a road map for public engagement with research through collective intelligence workshops with academic, public and NGO stakeholders.

The National Action Plan for Open Research encourages the review of research assessment and evaluation. The NORF-funded Incentivising Open Research Practices in Ireland (ABOARD) project brings together research performing and funding organisations to reach agreement on incentivising open research, develop a statement on responsible research metrics and support responsible research assessment. Under ABOARD, the Irish national chapter of the Coalition for Advancing Research Assessment (CoARA) was established; thirteen national organisations are now members. Technological University Dublin became the first Irish institution to launch a CoARA Action Plan, in September 2024. Another NORF-funded project, Responsible Use of Research Metrics, has piloted an online training module for those involved in recruitment, assessment and promotion decisions. The report also states that through these activities Ireland is addressing the recognition of open science practices within career evaluation and progression systems.

 

Initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices include Lero (the Research Ireland Centre for Software)'s Annual Director's Awards, which features an Open Science Champion category. NORF's 2023 Open Research Stimulus Call also sought to incentivise researchers wishing to advance open research.

 

The report does not indicate any unintended negative consequences of open science practices.

Several initiatives promote innovative, participatory approaches at different stages of the scientific process. For example, the Health Research Board Open Research Platform enables funded researchers to rapidly publish outputs through preprints and open peer review. In citizen science, the NORF-funded Star Guide project engages international networks in exoplanet observation through accessible planning systems. The Managing Open Source Software in Irish Universities project has developed a policy framework for open source software at Irish universities.

The National Open Research Coordinator represents NORF nationally and internationally, including through participation in the Council for National Open Science Coordination (CoNOSC) and the European Commission's Informal Expert Group on Scientific Information. Irish institutions actively participate in the Research Data Alliance (RDA), with DRI serving as the RDA-Ireland node.

 

Regarding a specific strategy stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science, the report cites the National Persistent Identifier Strategy and Roadmap, which presents 15 recommendations and actions spanning the period to 2030. Its objective is to enable seamless information flow between national and international systems, reduce bureaucratic burdens and improve research transparency and integrity.

 

The report states that Ireland is not involved in discussions on the establishment of regional or international funding mechanisms for open science.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science was disseminated through a series of six Open Science Awareness Days webinars hosted on the official YouTube channel of the Secretariat of Science, Humanities, Technology and Innovation (Secihti), where members of the humanities, science, technology and innovation community shared knowledge and experiences on the following topics: open scientific knowledge, scientific publications and open educational resources, research management systems (CRIS), citizen science and gender equality, copyright in the framework of open science and the current state of open science.

Mexico's national science, technology and innovation policy is anchored in the General Law on Humanities, Sciences, Technologies and Innovation, which explicitly addresses open access to scientific knowledge, defining it as an element of the human right to science. For example, Article 9 requires all levels of government to support open access to research-derived information.

 The Specific Guidelines for the Integration and Operation of Repositories are reported as a national policy instrument addressing the registration and deposit of information products derived from activities in humanities, sciences, technologies and innovation in repositories, ensuring open access to these materials, as well as acknowledging Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable (FAIR) principles and datasets within these information products.

At institutional level, the report confirms the presence of open science policies, citing the General Guidelines for the Open Access Policy of the National Autonomous University of Mexico as an example.  

 No specific national funding mechanism for open science is reported. Open science practices have, however, been integrated into existing research funding mechanisms: Article 3, Section IV of the General Law on Humanities, Sciences, Technologies and Innovation provides for the establishment of public instruments to promote open access, and, in practice, through the National System of Researchers (SNI), scholarships and calls for proposals, beneficiaries have been asked to make research outputs openly accessible through repositories.

 Regarding equitable public-private partnerships on open science, the report notes that Secihti is considering an agreement with LA Referencia, a Latin American network of open access repositories, to enhance the recognition and dissemination of publicly funded research outputs.

 Monitoring of open science is documented through the Special Programme on Humanities, Sciences, Technologies and Innovation 2021–2024, whose progress report tracks indicators related to open access under Priority Strategy 6.4 (p. 101-102; 141-144).

Among national open science infrastructures, the National Repository, developed by the National Council of Humanities, Sciences and Technologies (Conahcyt, now replaced by Secihti), aggregates open-access, full-text publicly funded scientific outputs from institutional repositories. The report also identifies the National Laboratories (Laboratorios Nacionales), which are inter-institutional, non-profit infrastructures distributed throughout the country that generate scientific, technological and innovation capacities, while promoting public access to and use of their resources.

The report states that no systematic capacity building on open science has taken place in higher education or research performing institutions; Secihti is considering including this in the institutional agenda. Similarly, no capacity-building programmes for policymakers on integrating open science values into science, technology and innovation policies are reported.

Open science competencies have not been reported as incorporated into higher education curricula; the report attributes this primarily to autonomy of each educational institution.
 
Open educational resources are reported as being used partly as an instrument for open science capacity building, though no further details are provided.

Regarding science communication in line with open science values, in 2024 Conahcyt (now Secihti) organised a Conference on Scientific Communication and Universal Access to Knowledge (Jornadas de Comunicación Científica y Acceso Universal al Conocimiento), aimed at bringing knowledge in the humanities, sciences, technologies and innovation closer to society to guarantee the human right to science.

The report does not describe specific initiatives at the national or institutional level to review existing research assessment and evaluation processes in alignment with open science principles.

On specific clauses recognising open science practices in career evaluation and progression systems, the report cites Article 11, Section XVIII of the General Law on Humanities, Sciences, Technologies and Innovation, which provides a basis for this.

No initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices are reported; Secihti is evaluating the feasibility of such measures.

 

No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.

The report identifies the existence of initiatives promoting innovative, participatory approaches at different stages of the research cycle, including the use of preprints. The Common Space Network for Education, Science, Humanities, Technology and Innovation (Red de Espacio Común de Educación, Ciencia, Humanidades, Tecnología e Innovación, Red ECOS Nacional), led by Secihti, is an example of an initiative promoting a collaborative research model that fosters inter-institutional, multidisciplinary and multisectoral collaboration, including the private sector and civil society, to develop solutions to national and societal challenges.

Mexico reports that its legal and regulatory framework in relation to humanities, sciences, technologies and innovation provides partial support for cross-border collaboration in open science; however, no specific initiatives or partnerships illustrating such collaboration are identified.

The report states that Mexico is not currently involved in discussions on the establishment of regional or international funding mechanisms for open science.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been disseminated across multiple national stakeholders. The Ministry of Foreign Affairs, through the Portuguese National Commission for UNESCO and the Permanent Delegation of Portugal, and the Ministry of Education, Science and Innovation have been involved in its promotion.
