The Foundation for Science and Technology (FCT), the national funding body supporting research, and its digital services unit FCCN (Foundation for National Scientific Computing) have actively shared the Recommendation. Universities including the University of Coimbra, the NOVA School of Social Sciences and Humanities and the University of Minho have disseminated it through their open science portals.


 Awareness-raising activities span several formats. The annual Lusophone Open Science Conference (ConfOA), in collaboration with Brazil, brings together communities from Portuguese-speaking countries. FCCN organises the annual Jornadas FCCN, which contributes to raising awareness on open science infrastructures and digital services. A dedicated citizen science session was held for the first time at the national CIÊNCIA2024 summit promoted by FCT.

At the national level, the political commitment to open science is reflected in the legal and regulatory framework, in instruments such as the Resolution of the Council of Ministers No. 21/2016, which defines the guiding principles for the implementation of a national open science policy and mandates the establishment of an interministerial working group for the elaboration of a strategic implementation plan, and Decree-Law No. 63/2019, which determines that research and development institutions must contribute to open science, ensuring free and open access to scientific knowledge and promoting engagement with society. However, according to the report, no dedicated national implementation plan or roadmap for open science has yet been developed. Nevertheless, the operationalisation of open science is largely driven by the main national research funding body, FCT.

 

Open science is addressed in the FCT’s Activity Plan (e.g., section 2.6.4), encompassing measures to promote open access to scientific publications and open research data through the implementation of policies and the provision of supporting infrastructure and services. FCT has adopted specific open science policies addressing open access to publications and the management and sharing of research data within the framework of scientific activities it funds, with open access requirements set out as mandatory. FCT also allocates dedicated funding to support the implementation of open science, including calls for the creation of a national network of research data management centres and funding of national open science services and infrastructures such as the Open Access Scientific Repositories of Portugal (RCAAP).
 
 At institutional level, an increasing number of institutions have adopted open science policies, reflecting the delegation of responsibilities under the Resolution of the Council of Ministers No. 21/2016; by end of 2022, 28 open access policies from research organisations were registered in the Registry of Open Access Repository Mandates and Policies (ROARMAP).

 Regarding equitable public-private partnerships on open science, the report indicates that such efforts have been undertaken by FCT, for instance through European Partnerships under the Horizon Europe Framework Programme.

 

A national monitoring framework for open science is under development by FCT: an Open Science Indicators Service will consolidate data from multiple information systems to generate metrics on research outputs and open access adoption, while a trackerFCT system will monitor compliance with the funder’s open science policies.

Portugal maintains several national open science infrastructures (most of them supported by FCT). The National Advanced Computing Network at FCT provides computing services to research communities. RCAAP aggregates open access research outputs from Portuguese institutional repositories. Pub In initiative provides infrastructure and services for the publication of open access scientific journals, including SARC (Scientific Journal Hosting Service), based on Open Journal Systems, and SciELO Portugal journal collection. The Digital Resources of Knowledge portal (Indexar) serves as a national directory of digital repositories and journals, including information on open science policy compliance. POLEN provides research data management services, including data preservation and sharing. PTCRIS, the Portuguese Current Research Information System, promotes the integration of research information management systems across institutions, enabling access to interconnected information on national scientific activities. The National Citizen Science Network serves as a national platform for citizen science communities.


Portugal reports hosting or funding federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science. For example, FCT funds the Sponsoring Consortium for Open Access Publishing in Particle Physics (SCOAP3) and contributes to the European Diamond Capacity Hub.

 Portugal is involved in international collaborations, including North-South, to optimise infrastructure use. FCT is a mandated organisation of the European Open Science Cloud (EOSC) Association and supports national activities aligned with EOSC's objectives. FCT and La Referencia, the federated network of institutional repositories in Latin America, signed a Memorandum of Collaboration enabling mutual content aggregation between RCAAP and the La Referencia network. In addition, FCT and the University of Minho signed an agreement with the Redalyc Scientific Information System to promote diamond open access journals.

Systematic capacity building on open science in higher education and research institutions is reported to be present through both national and institutional initiatives. At national level, FCT provides training sessions through FCCN on its digital services and infrastructures, customised to universities' needs (e.g., scientific journal management and publishing through Pub In, research data management through POLEN). In addition, the annual Research Data Management Forum, promoted by FCT, brings together communities of practice and professionals involved in research data management to coordinate national capacity-building initiatives, including through dedicated working groups. At institutional level, the University of Coimbra leads the European Research Area (ERA) Chair project EXCELScIOR, funded by Horizon Europe (2024–2029), which builds a dedicated meta-research team to support reproducibility, interdisciplinarity and open science within the university.

 

The report does not describe capacity-building programmes specifically for policymakers on integrating open science into science, technology and innovation policies.

 

Open science competencies have been incorporated into higher education curricula in some forms. For example, the NAU platform, a national MOOC platform managed by FCCN, offers the course "The Essentials of Research Data Management" (with a certificate upon completion), equipping researchers and doctoral students with practical skills across the data lifecycle.

 

Open educational resources are reported to be used as an instrument for open science capacity building.

 

Portugal confirms the presence of initiatives promoting science communication in line with open science values, referring to Ciência Viva, a national entity responsible for promoting scientific culture under the framework of Law No. 63/2019. The Portuguese Science Summit (CIÊNCIA) is identified as the largest annual national event in this regard, organised by FCT in collaboration with Ciência Viva.

Several initiatives to review research assessment and evaluation processes are reported. FCT, nine Portuguese universities and eight research performing institutions participate in the Coalition for Advancing Research Assessment (CoARA). The Portuguese National Chapter of CoARA raises awareness of CoARA principles, enables mutual learning and advances the development of action plans for research assessment reform. The co-chair of this National Chapter advises the FCT Board of Directors, leading reform efforts at the national funding level. At institutional level, the University of Coimbra participates in the TREASURE project, an institutional pilot funded under the Horizon Europe CoARA Boost, which aims to reward master's and PhD candidates for integrating reproducible, reusable and open research practices into their thesis research.

 

Regarding specific clauses recognising open science in career evaluation and progression, the report indicates that FCT has adopted a Narrative CV model across all its calls for researchers, teams and institutions, broadening recognition beyond traditional metrics to include datasets, software, methods and societal impact. FCT also advises evaluation panels not to rely on metrics as a proxy for assessing research quality. The ongoing revision of the Scientific Research Career Statute (Estatuto da Carreira de Investigação Científica, ECIC), under discussion in Parliament at the time of reporting, aims to provide broader recognition of researchers' diverse profiles and contributions. Several institutions have also introduced measures to recognise citizen science experience in research recruitment evaluations, as reflected in recruitment notices issued by institutions such as the University of Aveiro and the University of Coimbra.

 

No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.

Portugal reports initiatives at both national and institutional levels that promote innovative, participatory approaches at different stages of the research cycle. These address the promotion of preprints, open peer review practices, participatory methods such as citizen science and crowdsourcing, participatory strategies for identifying the needs of marginalised communities and open innovation. For example, citizen science is promoted through various initiatives mentioned above, including the National Citizen Science Network platform and the recognition of citizen science in research recruitment processes at certain universities. The Pub In services modernise editorial management platforms for open access journal publishing and support the adoption of open peer review and open annotation practices.

Portugal promotes international collaboration on open science through participation in several networks. As noted above, Portugal participates in infrastructure networks, including EOSC, SciELO, La Referencia and Redalyc. FCT is a signatory of the Open Access 2020 initiative and one of 20 funding agencies in cOAlition S, implementing Plan S principles. FCT also a member of Science Europe and the FAIRsharing Programme, fostering alignment of national research data management policies and practices with their recommendations.

 

Bilateral collaborations on open science include a long-standing partnership with Brazil formalised since 2009, which led to the establishment of the annual joint Lusophone Open Science Conference (ConfOA). In addition, FCT, together with the University of Minho and the Brazilian Institute of Information in Science and Technology (IBICT), is implementing a project to create the CPLP Scientific Repository Portuguese-speaking countries, which aggregates national scientific repositories from member states and includes a capacity-building component.

 

Portugal is involved in discussions on international funding mechanisms for open science. For example, FCT participates in the OECD Global Science Forum Expert Group on Citizen Science, where discussions are underway on new funding mechanisms for citizen science activities.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has not been promoted or disseminated to ministries and institutions in the Philippines, according to the report. The report suggests that future engagement with international frameworks such as the Recommendation could guide the country in formalising its commitment to open science through national policies and implementation plans. 

While the report explicitly indicates that no awareness-raising activities on open science have been organised in the Philippines, some isolated initiatives at the institutional level are nevertheless identified in other sections of the report. These include the University of the Philippines Open University's Research Conversations: Open Science Series and the University of Santo Tomas's Science Week, which included discussions on open science.

At the national level, elements related to open science are incorporated in key science, technology and innovation policies and legal frameworks, including the Harmonized National Research and Development Agenda (HNRDA) 2022-2028 coordinated by the Department of Science and Technology (DOST), the Technology Transfer Act (Republic Act No. 10055) and the Philippine Innovation Act (Republic Act No. 11293). In particular, the Technology Transfer Act assigns the ownership of intellectual property from government-funded research to research institutions, facilitating the  dissemination and use of publicly funded research outputs, while the Innovation Act promotes collaborative research and knowledge sharing across sectors. However, the report indicates that no dedicated national open science implementation plan, strategy or roadmap has yet been developed.

 At the institutional level, the report confirms the existence of open science policies; however, no formal policy documents are cited.

According to the report, open science practices have been integrated into existing research funding mechanisms as follows. In particular, the DOST Grants-in-Aid (GIA) Programme requires funded projects to share findings through open access repositories and platforms, encourages publication in open access journals and mandates the submission of knowledge products such as technical reports, policy briefs and datasets for public access.  


The Philippines reports ongoing efforts to build equitable public-private partnerships on open science and refers, for example, to collaborative research supported under the GIA Programme and the Renewable Energy Science, Technology and Innovation (RESTI) Programme.

 A national monitoring framework for open science is reported as present, citing a DOST monitoring and evaluation protocol for research and development activities; however, the report does not clarify whether this framework explicitly includes open science-specific indicators.

The Philippines reports the existence of several open science infrastructures. For example, the Computing and Archiving Research Environment (COARE), a supercomputing facility operated by the DOST’s Advanced Science and Technology Institute, provides free high-performance computing, science cloud and research data repository services to students and researchers. The eLibrary of the DOST’s Philippine Council for Agriculture, Aquatic and Natural Resources Research and Development (DOST-PCAARRD) provides open access to research publications in agriculture, aquatic and natural resources, with full-text access available upon user registration. DOST-PCAARRD also operates the Advanced Learning Management System (PALMS), an e-learning platform offering training courses for researchers in agriculture, aquatic and natural resources. Additional reported resources operated by DOST include OneLab, which serves as a single point of access to testing laboratories nationwide, and One Expert, a national network of science and technology experts that fosters collaboration.    

 

The report states that the Philippines does not host or fund federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science. Whether existing regional and international infrastructures are accessible to the country's relevant actors is described as requiring further review.

 

The country confirms participation in international collaborations for shared open science infrastructures, including North-South, North-South-South or South-South partnerships. The report cites a joint initiative with the European Union, the National Copernicus Capacity Support Action Programme for the Philippines (CopPhil), which aims to deploy local infrastructure to promote the use of free and open Copernicus Earth Observation data in the Philippines. Regional cooperation includes the Association of Southeast Asian Nations Science and Technology Network (ASTNET), the ASEAN Network for Drugs, Diagnostics, Vaccines and Traditional Medicines Innovation (ASEAN-NDI) and the East Asia Science and Innovation Area Joint Research Programme (e-ASIA JRP); however, the report does not provide specific examples of open science initiatives within these frameworks.

The report indicates that systematic capacity building on open science is taking place in higher education and research institutions, referring to initiatives such as the Institution Development Programme (IDP), which aims to strengthen the research capabilities of academic institutions by supporting the development and upgrading of research laboratories and facilities; however, it does not clarify whether open science is explicitly addressed within these initiatives.

 

Capacity-building programmes for policymakers on integrating open science values into science, technology and innovation policies are not reported.

 

Open science competencies have not been incorporated in higher education research skills curricula. Open educational resources are not reported as being used for open science capacity building.

 

Several initiatives to support science communication in line with open science values are reported, including the Science and Technology Academic and Research-Based Openly Operated KioskS (STARBOOKS) programme and the DOST Bantog Awards for Media and Science Communication.

The report indicates that no initiatives have been undertaken to review research assessment and evaluation processes to align them with open science, and that no specific clauses recognising open science practices have been incorporated into career evaluation and progression systems.

 

Regarding initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices, the Philippines reports several science awards administered by the National Academy of Science and Technology (e.g., the Outstanding Young Scientist and the NAST Talent Search for Young Scientists), while noting that these are not specifically designed for open science.

 

No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.

The report indicates the existence of national initiatives supporting participatory approaches to increase the openness of the scientific process, as reflected in the development and updating of the Harmonized National Research and Development Agenda (HNRDA). This process includes strategies to identify the needs of marginalised communities and to incorporate socially relevant issues into research agendas. DOST agencies lead the process in consultation with public and private research institutions, academia, industry and other stakeholders.

According to the report, DOST promotes international scientific collaborations across different fields of science, technology and innovation. However, no specific partnerships relevant to open science are cited.

 

The report does not describe a strategy or plan for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science, identifying this as an area for future progress.

 

Involvement in discussions on the establishment of regional and international funding mechanisms for open science is not reported.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been disseminated to a range of ministries, government agencies and universities. The entities involved include Makerere University, Kyambogo University, the International Business, Science and Technology (ISBAT) University and Mbarara University of Science and Technology, as well as government bodies such as the Uganda National Council for Science and Technology (UNCST), the National Environment Management Authority (NEMA), the Ministry of Education and Sports, the Office of the President's Science, Technology and Innovation Secretariat and the Uganda National Academy of Sciences (UNAS), among others. A dissemination meeting convened by the Uganda National Commission for UNESCO on 17 December 2024, attended by 24 officials, introduced participants from ministries, departments and agencies to the Recommendation and the UNESCO Open Science Toolkit.

 Reported open science awareness-raising activities include multi-stakeholder discussions on the benefits and challenges of open science, addressing topics such as competition, data exploitation, intellectual property rights, privacy and inequalities between publicly and privately funded research.

Uganda has a National Science, Technology and Innovation Policy, adopted in 2009 and developed by UNCST. The report notes that the policy contains open science elements related to open access to scientific knowledge, open science infrastructure, open engagement of societal actors and open dialogue with other knowledge systems. These are not framed as open science, however, as the concept had not been conceived when the policy was adopted.

 No national-level policy or legal framework specifically addressing open science is reported. No national implementation plan or roadmap for open science and no institutional-level policies promoting open science are reported. The report states that the 2021 Recommendation has not yet been domesticated in the country and identifies the need for specialised expertise to develop relevant policy instruments.

No specific national funding mechanism for open science is reported, nor have open science practices been integrated into existing research funding mechanisms according to the report.

No efforts to develop equitable public-private partnerships on open science are reported, and no national monitoring framework for open science has been developed.

Open science infrastructures are reported as being under development, without further clarification. Elsewhere, the report refers to two related national infrastructures: the Research and Education Network for Uganda (RENU), which provides connectivity services to research and education institutions, and the National Research Information Management System (NRIMS), an integrated online platform operated by the UNCST and designed to support researchers in the submission and clearance of research projects and to facilitate regulatory oversight by national authorities.

 The report states that Uganda does not host or fund any federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science.

 

No involvement in North-South, North-South-South or South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use or develop joint strategies for shared open science platforms is reported.

The report indicates that no systematic capacity building on open science has taken place in higher education or research performing institutions, and no capacity-building programmes for policymakers are reported.

 Open science competencies have not been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula, according to the report.

The report does not describe the use of open educational resources as an instrument for open science capacity building, nor does it report initiatives to support science communication in line with open science values.

Regarding initiatives to review research assessment and evaluation processes, the report states that all Vice Chancellors of Universities adopted a resolution at their 2024 meeting, though no further details are provided.

 No specific clauses recognising open science practices have yet been incorporated into career evaluation and progression systems according to the report. No initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices are reported, and the report states there are no plans for such measures.

 No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.

The report indicates that initiatives promoting innovative, participatory approaches at different stages of the scientific process exist at both the national and institutional levels, particularly in relation to open innovation practices; however, no specific details or examples are provided.

Regarding international scientific collaborations on open science, the report states that wider dissemination of the UNESCO Recommendation is being conducted. This is to be followed by efforts to encourage international collaborations and to support the creation and maintenance of collaborative networks for exchanging open science practices and lessons learned.

 No strategy or plan for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science is reported. The report also does not indicate the country’s involvement in discussions on the establishment of regional or international funding mechanisms for open science.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science was disseminated by the National Council of Science, Technology and Innovation (CONCYTEC) to universities and public research institutes in February 2025.

 

Various awareness-raising activities were organised, including three macro-regional meetings held by CONCYTEC in 2024 in Chiclayo, Arequipa and Lima, bringing together member institutions of the National Network of Open Access Digital Repositories (RENARE) and the PerúCRIS (Current Research Information System), a national system for collecting and disseminating data on science, technology and innovation (STI) activities. These meetings facilitated awareness of open science in the context of the 2024 regulations of the Law of the National System of Science, Technology and Innovation (Law No. 31250), which explicitly introduced provisions promoting open science (see the following section). In addition, the College of Librarians of Peru and the Open Access Peru Collective held Open Access Week Peru 2024 in October 2024, promoting open science values and principles to the broader community.

Peru's National Policy for the Development of Science, Technology and Technological Innovation, approved in 2016, does not incorporate aspects of open science; a revised version is under development. However, open science is integrated into the STI legal framework. The legal framework for open science rests on two national laws. Law No. 30035, enacted in 2013, establishes ALICIA as the National Digital Repository for Open Access, with the aim of centralising and preserving the results of government-funded STI research, while requiring institutions to integrate metadata on the research outputs available in their repositories into the ALICIA platform. Law No. 31250, enacted in 2021, is implemented by regulations adopted in June 2024, which institutionalise the promotion of open science under four fundamental pillars: open scientific knowledge, open science infrastructures, open participation of social actors and open dialogue with other knowledge systems (Article 38); the regulations further require that data management plans be submitted during the public funding application process and that all research outputs, including research data, be deposited in open access repositories (Article 39).
 
At the institutional level, Peru reports the existence of open science policies; however, no examples of formal policy documents are provided.  
 
The report does not identify a specific national funding mechanism for open science. Both laws nonetheless integrate open science practices into research funding by requiring data management plans for publicly funded research projects and the availability of research outputs through open repositories.
 
The report states that public-private partnerships for open science have not been pursued, as their feasibility has not yet been assessed.
 
A national monitoring framework for open science is reported as under development, with strategic indicators covering the number of institutions with STI systems integrated into governing body platforms, the number of open access articles and the number of publications for which research data are available in public repositories.

Open science infrastructures are reported as under development, with several examples identified. The country maintains more than 200 institutional open access repositories from public and private institutions (members of the RENARE network managed by CONCYTEC), whose metadata are integrated into and made searchable through the centralised ALICIA platform. The Scientific Electronic Library Online Peru (SciELO Peru) provides full-text access to Peruvian scientific journals as part of a broader regional initiative. The PerúCRIS platform aggregates and provides access to metadata on STI activities from public and private entities across the country. A centralised repository for publicly funded research data is planned.

 

The report states that Peru does not host any federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science.

 

North-South and South-South collaborations on open science infrastructure include participation in regional networks such as SciELO and La Referencia, a platform that aggregates open access repositories across Latin America and Spain. CONCYTEC is also involved in the Horizon Europe ResInfra and ResInfra Plus projects, which are working to establish the Latin American and Caribbean Research Infrastructure Forum (LACFRI) to coordinate open and large-scale research infrastructures across Latin America and the Caribbean.

The report states that systematic capacity building on open science has not yet been implemented in higher education or research performing institutions. However, several isolated initiatives are identified, such as research data management workshops conducted in 2024 in Lima, Chiclayo, Iquitos and Arequipa for researchers, covering the incorporation of data management plans in project proposals and the deposit of research data from publicly funded projects. No specific capacity-building programmes for policymakers are reported.

 

The report indicates that open science competencies have not been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula.

 

Open educational resources are not reported as being used for open science capacity building.

The report states that no initiatives have been undertaken to review research assessment and evaluation processes in line with open science principles. No specific clauses recognising open science practices are reported in career evaluation and progression systems. The report also does not identify any initiatives to recognise or reward open science practices.

 

No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.
At the national level, efforts are under way to promote preprints on the SciELO Peru platform for domestic open access journals.

Peru confirms the existence of international collaborations on open science. Apart from initiatives related to open science infrastructure (see above), no additional examples are reported.

 

The report does not describe a specific strategy for cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science. Peru is also not reported as being involved in discussions on the establishment of regional or international funding mechanisms for open science.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted and shared with relevant ministries and institutions, though the report does not specify the entities involved.


 Awareness-raising activities on open science predate the 2021 Recommendation. The Consortium of Higher Education and Research Libraries of Senegal (COBESS) launched the open access movement in Senegal in February 2010. In 2013, during International Open Access Week, COBESS began an advocacy campaign for adopting an open access policy at Cheikh Anta Diop University of Dakar (UCAD), linked to a project establishing an institutional repository. Subsequent activities include a 2019 international colloquium on open science in francophone sub-Saharan Africa organised with the French Research Institute for Development (IRD), and workshops on the Right to Research co-organised by COBESS and Electronic Information for Libraries (EIFL) in December 2022 and August 2023. In October 2022, the Regional Centre for Research and Training (CRCF), IRD, the School of Librarians, Archivists and Documentalists (EBAD) and the university library of UCAD jointly held a seminar on publishing in the era of open science. The Cheikh Hamidou Kane Digital University (UNCHK) also hosted a webinar on open science in Senegal as part of its "Research Rendezvous" series.

Senegal's science, technology and innovation policy addresses elements related to open science through several strategic frameworks. The New Deal Technologique promotes open digital infrastructure, access to scientific content and digital sovereignty. The Sector Development Policy Letter 2025–2029 (LPSD) targets the modernisation of higher education and research through digital integration and infrastructure strengthening, and the National Agenda for the Transformation of Higher Education, Research and Innovation (ANTESRI) identifies the need to develop a national open science strategy.

 The report states that no national policy or legal framework specifically addressing open science has been formally adopted, and no national implementation plan or roadmap exists. Governance of open science is described as being structured, with the Ministry of Higher Education, Research and Innovation (MESRI) coordinating alongside universities, research centres and university libraries.

At the institutional level, some institutions have introduced governance arrangements for open science through ad hoc committees overseeing institutional repository projects and open science training. UNCHK is developing an open science roadmap, and institutions including IRD, the Senegalese Institute for Agricultural Research (ISRA), the Pasteur Institute of Dakar, COBESS and the National Centre for Scientific and Technical Documentation (CNDST) are developing open access strategies.

 No specific national funding mechanism for open science is reported. Nevertheless, the New Deal Technologique, which foresees increased mobilisation of resources for digital infrastructure and open innovation, could support indirectly open science. Regarding the integration of open science into established research funding schemes, the report identifies that national calls for projects encourage the use of open-source software, publication in open access journals and archiving in institutional repositories.

 A national monitoring framework for open science is reported as under development. A national focal point has been designated by MESRI, indicators are being defined for a future national roadmap, and the Integrated Governance System for Higher Education and Research (SIGESR) is expected to integrate open science-related data. Performance indicators related to scientific outputs and open access are already integrated in governance instruments and performance contracts with public higher education institutions.

Senegal has several national and institutional open science infrastructures. The National Cyberinfrastructure for Higher Education, Research and Innovation (CINERI) manages shared digital infrastructure including the TAQUEY supercomputer, a data centre, the Senegalese National Research and Education Network (SnRER) and the Integrated Governance System for Higher Education and Research (SIGESR), used for monitoring performance indicators. The national data archive (ANADS) provides archiving services for population survey data. Several institutions operate institutional repositories for archiving scientific publications, including Assane Seck University of Ziguinchor and UCAD. Amadou Mahtar Mbow University and Gaston Berger University have launched projects for establishing their own repositories. The report also refers to a few open access journals that operate under the diamond open access model, including Global Africa.

 

Senegal participates in several North-South and South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use. These include collaboration with IRD (France) on the OpenOPSE project to facilitate data sharing within the consortium of the Population, Health and Environmental Observatory (OPSE); engagement with the Agence Universitaire de la Francophonie (AUF); engagement with the African and Malagasy Council for Higher Education (CAMES) on its regional repository for scientific outputs; and participation in the EU co-funded LIBSENSE programme (Library Support for Embedded NREN Services and E-infrastructure) led by the West and Central African Research and Education Network (WACREN).

Systematic capacity building on open science has taken place in higher education and research institutions. EBAD at UCAD offers a specialised training in research data management. MESRI, through CNDST and in partnership with organisations including UNESCO, IRD, AUF and COBESS, organises capacity-building workshops for researchers, librarians and information technology professionals. Targeted trainings on persistent identifiers, open licences and FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable) practices have also been delivered. Regarding capacity building for policymakers, the report confirms the existence of such efforts, referring to a national open science workshop organised by Amadou Mahtar Mbow University in partnership with UNESCO in October 2023, as well as the Right to Research workshops co-organised by COBESS and EIFL in 2022 and 2023, which were attended by policymakers and parliamentarians.


Senegalese universities are progressively integrating open science modules into master's and doctoral programmes, and COBESS has developed a syllabus addressing open science for these levels.

The report indicates that open educational resources are used for open science capacity building but does not provide further detail.

The report does not address the question whether initiatives to review research assessment processes are in place. Regarding specific clauses recognising open science practices in career evaluation and progression systems, the report refers to CAMES directives concerning scientific publications but does not elaborate on how these directives incorporate open science.

 In response to the question on unintended negative consequences of open science practices, the report describes several challenges encountered in implementing open science: a lack of dedicated funding, the absence of a specific legal framework, low initial awareness and fragmentation of initiatives across institutions. These have been partially addressed through international partnerships and collaborative projects, ongoing regulatory development, and regular training and communication campaigns.

The report indicates the existence of national and institutional initiatives promoting participatory approaches and openness across different stages of the research cycle, including the encouragement of interdisciplinarity and the involvement of non-academic actors such as NGOs, citizens and local authorities.
Senegal reports that collaborations with organisations such as UNESCO, IRD, AUF, EIFL support the development of open science in the country.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted across government and research entities, including the Department of Science, Technology and Innovation (DSTI), the Department of Higher Education and Training (DHET), the National Research Foundation (NRF) and Universities South Africa, a network of all universities in the country. All 26 public universities received the Recommendation, along with the Committee of Higher Education Libraries of South Africa, the South African National Library and Information Consortium (SANLiC) and the Academy of Science of South Africa (ASSAf).

 Awareness-raising activities on open science take several forms. Universities organise events during the International Open Access Week. The South African Science Forum, a national policy dialogue, has featured sessions on open science. Other entities engaged in advocacy include ASSAf, the NRF, the Africa Open Science Platform (AOSP), SANLiC and the Library and Information Association of South Africa (LIASA).

South Africa's national science, technology and innovation policy incorporates elements related to open science covering open access to scientific knowledge, open science infrastructure and open engagement of societal actors. National-level policies are reported as partly addressing open science in line with the 2021 Recommendation. At the time of reporting, a draft National Open Science Policy was under ministerial cluster review following extensive consultation (it has since been adopted).
 
At institutional level, several universities and institutions have adopted open science policies, including ASSAf. The University of Pretoria, Stellenbosch University, Sefako Makgatho Health Sciences University and the Durban University of Technology collaborate with the Public Library of Science (PLOS) and the Training Centre in Communication (TCC Africa) to promote open science policies and practices.
 
As a national funding mechanism for open science, the report refers to SANLiC facilitating Transformative Agreements across university and science council consortia. The report further notes efforts to integrate open science practices into existing research funding schemes, including NRF collaborative grants, where open science expectations form part of the proposal assessment and review process.
 
Efforts toward equitable public-private partnerships on open science are reported, notably through university partnerships with external stakeholders; however, no further details are provided.
 
A national monitoring framework for open science is reported as partly in place, noting that the DSTI has developed the Science Engagement Monitoring and Evaluation Impact Indicator Framework; however, the report does not clarify whether open science is explicitly incorporated into this framework.

Summary:
Reported open science infrastructures include the DSTI-supported National Integrated Cyber Infrastructure System (NICIS), providing high-performance computing, high-speed network and a national research data infrastructure, and NRF’s national research infrastructure platforms, such as the South African Environmental Observation network (SAEON).

 

South Africa hosts the Africa Open Science Platform and its Regional Nodes as a federated regional information technology infrastructure for open science.

 

The country reports involvement in North-South and South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use, including the Square Kilometre Array (SKAO) and the Southern African Large Telescope (SALT).

Systematic capacity building on open science has taken place in higher education and research institutions through both formal and informal channels, including conferences, webinars and Continuing Professional Development and Workplace Learning (CPDWL) activities. The report states that open science competencies have been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula.

 

Open educational resources are reported as used for open science capacity building.

 

Science communication initiatives in line with open science values are supported by DHET, the Council for Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR) and the NRF, with universities playing an important role in disseminating knowledge to the broader public.

The report indicates the existence of initiatives to review research assessment and evaluation processes to align them with open science principles, such as the DSTI's Science Engagement Monitoring and Evaluation Impact Indicator Framework. It also states that specific clauses recognising open science practices have been incorporated in career evaluation and progression systems; however, no further details are provided.

 

The NRF has for the first time introduced a Societal Impact Award, which the report states was influenced by the shift within both government and the agency towards openness.

 

The report does not indicate any unintended negative consequences of open science practices.

South Africa reports the existence of national and institutional initiatives promoting innovative, participatory approaches at different stages of the research cycle. These include the promotion of preprints, exploration of open peer review practices, valuing and sharing negative scientific results, citizen science, participatory strategies to identify the needs of marginalised communities and incorporate socially relevant issues into research agendas, and the promotion of open innovation practices. However, the report does not provide specific examples of these initiatives.

South Africa has a strategy for cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science through the Africa Open Science Platform. The report also highlights NRF collaborative grants with other countries as a mechanism supporting international collaboration, with open science forming part of the proposal assessment and review process.

 

The report identifies the NRF, the MRC and the DSTI and its nodal agencies as participants in discussions on the establishment of regional and international funding mechanisms for open science.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has not been promoted or shared with appropriate ministries and institutions. However, a small number of organisations were informed through participation in an online training session organised by the national focal point and the UNESCO Dakar office.

 No awareness-raising activities on open science are reported as having been organised or foreseen by the end of 2025.

Niger’s National Policy on Science, Technology and Innovation (POSTINI), adopted in 2020 and developed by the Ministry of Higher Education, Research and Technological Innovation, incorporates open access to scientific knowledge as an open science element.

 However, the report does not identify a national-level policy or legal framework specifically addressing open science, policy instruments to promote open science, a dedicated national implementation plan or roadmap, or institutional-level open science policies. These absences are attributed to the Ministry of Higher Education, Research and Technological Innovation having been recently informed about the open science recommendations and having only recently designated a national focal point for open science.

 The report states that no specific national funding mechanisms for open science exist and that open science practices have not been integrated into existing research funding mechanisms, for the reasons noted above.

 The report indicates that efforts to develop equitable public-private partnerships on open science have been made or are foreseen, though no details are provided. No national monitoring framework for open science is reported.

The report states that no national or institutional open science infrastructures exist in Niger.

 Niger does not report hosting or funding any federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science. The report also notes that existing open science infrastructures at regional and international levels are not accessible to relevant actors in the country.

 No involvement in North-South, North-South-South or South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use or develop joint strategies for shared open science platforms is reported.

No systematic capacity building on open science in higher education or research performing institutions is reported. The report attributes this to the recent designation of the national open science focal point and notes that training, particularly for the focal point, is awaited. Capacity building programmes for policymakers on integrating open science values and principles into science, technology and innovation policies have not yet started.

 The report states that open science competencies have not been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula, noting that the open science is not yet operational.

 

Open educational resources are likewise not reported as being used for open science capacity building.

 The report indicates that initiatives supporting science communication in line with open science values have been undertaken, though no details are provided.

The report states that no initiatives have been undertaken to review existing research assessment and evaluation processes to align them with open science principles, nor have specific clauses recognising open science practices been incorporated into career evaluation and progression systems. These absences are attributed to the recent designation of the national open science focal point and the fact that open science has not yet been operationalised.

 No initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices are reported, for the same reason state above.

 

No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.

The report indicates the existence of initiatives at both national and institutional levels promote innovative, participatory changes at different stages of the research cycle. These are reported to address the promotion of preprints and the exploration of open peer review, alongside participatory methods such as citizen science. Strategies for identifying the needs of marginalised communities to orient research towards addressing societal challenges and the promotion of open innovation practices are also covered, although no concrete examples are provided.

The report refers more broadly to international scientific collaboration through research networks at universities and research institutions; however, no specific open science initiatives are identified.

 

No strategy or plan for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science is reported, though the report notes that one is in progress.

 Niger does not report involvement in discussions on the establishment of regional and international funding mechanisms for open science.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted through the Ministry of Higher Education and Scientific Research (MESRS), primarily via its National University Centre for Scientific and Technical Documentation (CNUDST). Some higher education and research institutions have also promoted it independently, including the Higher School of Design Sciences and Technologies (ESSTED).

 Awareness-raising activities on open science have been conducted for over a decade, initially centred on open access through CNUDST-led workshops, seminars and information campaigns at research institutions. On 30 November 2022, the CNUDST organised a Library Support for Embedded NREN Services and E-infrastructure (LIBSENSE) workshop at the City of Sciences of Tunis. The workshop mobilised universities, libraries and research centres around priorities such as repository interoperability, institutional policy alignment and skills development. The Study Day on Open Science (Journée d'Étude sur la Science Ouverte, JESO), an annual event whose first edition took place on 10 April 2025 at the Pasteur Institute of Tunis (IPT), addresses open access, open data, open infrastructures and citizen participation.

Tunisia’s national science, technology and innovation policies, including governmental strategic plans, integrate elements related to open science, such as open access to scientific knowledge, open science infrastructure and open engagement of societal actors. For example, the 2023–2025 sectoral document on higher education and research contains orientations on open access, and the next five-year sectoral plan (2026–2030) is expected explicitly incorporate open science principles.


 The report states, however, that Tunisia does not yet have a national policy specifically dedicated to open science. Nevertheless, several legal and regulatory frameworks include provisions broadly relevant to open science principles, including provisions on e-governance, access to information, open government data, personal data protection, scientific integrity and ethics.

A national open science implementation plan is under development. The General Directorate of Scientific Research (DGRS) has established a steering group and a working group for open science implementation, in coordination with the CNUDST, El-Khawarizmi Computing Centre (CCK), General Directorate for Research Valorisation (DGVR) and the Management Unit for the EU Research and Innovation Framework Programmes (UGPO) for Horizon Europe. Specific policy instruments are also reported as under development, with the report citing initiatives led by the CNUDST supporting the implementation of open science, such as the establishment of an open access portal for theses and articles (PIST.tn) and transformative publishing agreements.

 

At the institutional level, several institutions are engaged in the implementation of open science, particularly the IPT, though the report notes these initiatives require consolidation through formalised policies.

 Specific funding mechanisms for open science are reported as under development. Open science practices are beginning to be integrated into established research funding programmes, though not yet systematically. Existing programmes such as the Valorisation of Research Results Programme (VRR) and Collabora-Technopôle support research valorisation and collaboration with businesses and civil society organisations but do not include open science as an explicit funding criterion.

A national monitoring framework is under development. Efforts are under way to define open science indicators for the MESRS "Scientific Research" budget programme under the Organic Budget Law (Loi Organique du Budget, LOB). In addition, the CNUDST maintains its own indicators on open science.

Tunisia has a range of national open science infrastructures supported by the MESRS. For example, the CCK hosts cloud infrastructure and provides high-performance computing and data storage for the higher education and scientific research sector. The CNUDST operates the Scientific and Technical Information Portal (PIST.tn), offering open access to theses and scientific articles, and coordinates the Tunisian Digital Object Identifier Consortium in partnership with DataCite to assign persistent identifiers to Tunisian research outputs. The National Platform of Scientific Equipment (PNES.tn), a registry of shared research instruments, further support open access to resources. Among institutional platforms, the report cites the VISIRECH web platform of the University of Tunis El Manar (UTM), which enables research units to create their own websites and rapidly publish their research results, with long-term archiving.
 
The report states that Tunisia does not host or fund federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science, though existing international infrastructures are accessible to Tunisian open science actors.

The IPT and the Centre of Biotechnology of Sfax (CBS) participate in North-South and South-South collaborations on open science infrastructure. Both are involved in the Data Science for Health Discovery and Innovation in Africa (DS-I Africa) project, an National Institutes of Health (NIH)-funded pan-African open data science platform for biomedical research, and in the Human Heredity and Health in Africa (H3Africa) project, which promotes equitable genomic research through open data sharing, biobanks and bioinformatics capacity building across over 30 African countries.

Capacity building on open science is mainly led by the CNUDST and primarily targeted at higher education and research institutions. CNUDST  provides technical assistance and regularly organizes seminars and training sessions on open access, copyright, research data, data management plans and persistent identifiers. It also participates in the Skills for the European Open Science Cloud (Skills4EOSC) project, an EU-funded initiative for open science training, through which the CNUDST strengthened its capacities to deliver national training sessions on Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable (FAIR) principles.


Open science competencies have been progressively integrated into higher education curriculum. The Professional Master in Software Engineering – Open Source (MP2L), coordinated by the Higher Institute of Informatics (ISI) and the Virtual University of Tunis (UVT), trains students in free and open-source software. The Higher Institute of Documentation (ISD) at the University of Manouba offers a second-year undergraduate course entitled “Open Access to Information”, while its research master's program in Information and Documentation Science includes a course entitled “Scientific and Technical Information" covering open science aspects. Workshops for doctoral students on archiving publications in open repositories also take place at the ISD.

The report indicates that open educational resources are partly used for open science capacity building, without providing further detail.

The report indicates the existence of several initiatives supporting science communication in line with open science values, including the Tunis Science City , the Monastir Sciences Palace and t he EU-supported Science With and For Youth (SWAFY) project, which disseminate scientific culture to the public.

As part of the review of research assessment and evaluation processes, the Tunisian Agency for Evaluation and Accreditation (ATEA) plans to update current evaluation frameworks for research structures and projects to incorporate open science criteria. Current research evaluation frameworks include criteria related to openness to the socio-economic environment and the social and economic impact of research, though open science is not yet mentioned explicitly. Regarding career evaluation and progression, in some disciplines, open science practices are already recognised in recruitment and promotion: in molecular and cellular biology, up to 10 points out of 100 may be awarded for open science practices in candidate applications.

 

Reported unintended negative consequences of open science include predatory publishing. Doctoral schools address this by providing lists of acceptable journals for doctoral publications, and both the CNUDST and doctoral schools deliver training to prevent publication in predatory journals.

Initiatives promoting participatory approaches at different stages of the research cycle operate at both national and institutional levels. For example, the IPT created Tunisia’s first Science Shop (Boutique des Sciences) called “Science Together”, within the Horizon 2020 Ingenious Science Shops to Promote Participatory Innovation, Research and Equity in Science (InSPIRES) project. This mechanism enables co-creation of research projects and knowledge exchange between scientists and the public. It also serves to identify societal needs, including those of marginalised communities, for incorporation into research agendas. In addition, practices associated with open innovation are promoted, notably through policies supporting startups, such as the Startup Act.

Tunisia promotes international scientific collaborations on open science primarily through its association with the EU funding programme Horizon Europe, which incorporates open science requirements, including open access to publications, responsible data management through data management plans, deposit in open archives and the use of open licences. The MESRS supports cross-border cooperation through the General Directorate for International Cooperation and the UGPO for Horizon Europe.

 

Regarding regional and international funding mechanisms for open science, the report states that Tunisia has not engaged in discussions on establishing such mechanisms. Tunisian researchers are nonetheless involved in international fundraising for capacity building in Africa, particularly in bioinformatics and artificial intelligence, through projects such as DS-I Africa and H3Africa.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted through the Ministry of Industry and Technology and the Scientific and Technological Research Council of Türkiye (TÜBİTAK).

 Awareness-raising activities have been carried out by multiple actors. The Izmir Institute of Technology (IZTECH) contributed through its participation in OpenAIRE (Open Access Infrastructure for Research in Europe) projects from 2011 to 2021, during which it took part in 123 events (26 international), delivered 100 presentations and organised 15 webinars. The Turkish Open Science Summit 2018 brought together decision-makers and stakeholders, described as the first gathering of all open science stakeholders in Türkiye. In July 2023, IZTECH hosted the European Open Science Cloud (EOSC) Trainer Training under the EOSC Future Project and, in autumn 2025, it hosted the euroCRIS conference on research information management. Civil society organisations, including Creative Commons Türkiye, have organised events, and annual Open Access Week activities are coordinated nationally through the acikerisim.org website.

Türkiye's national science, technology and innovation policy includes certain open science elements, such as open access to scientific knowledge and open science infrastructure; however, no further details are provided. The report states, however, that there is no comprehensive national-level policy or legal framework specifically addressing open science. Nor is there a formal national implementation plan or roadmap in place, although TÜBİTAK has been developing a draft National Open Science Strategy. Reported obstacles include insufficient dissemination of open science among academics, infrastructure that is still under development, the lack of prioritisation of investments and resource allocation for open science and weak inter-institutional coordination. Several policy instruments nevertheless promote open science, including the Higher Education Open Access and Institutional Repository Policy established by the Council of Higher Education (YÖK), which requires universities to collect and preserve academic outputs through institutional repositories.

At the institutional level, IZTECH established Türkiye's first mandatory open access policy in 2013, an open science policy in 2019 and the country's first research data management directive in 2022. Many other universities have also adopted open science policies.

As a dedicated national funding mechanisms for open science, the report refers to financial support provided by TÜBİTAK for the sustainability of domestic open access journals, including DergiPark, a TÜBİTAK-funded platform hosting open access journals. In addition, some universities provide article processing charge (APC) support for publications in Q1 and Q2-ranked journals. The TÜBİTAK-led National Academic License for Electronic Resources (EKUAL) consortium has also concluded open access publishing agreements with several publishers. Open science practices have been integrated into research funding mechanisms: for example, TÜBİTAK adopted its open science policy in March 2019, based on the OpenAIRE model for research funding organisations, covering the management, storage and digital preservation of publications and research data from its supported projects, including requirements for data management plans and, in many cases, open access to publications.

Regarding equitable public-private partnerships on open science, the report indicates that these are encouraged by TÜBİTAK, particularly through EKUAL consortium’s negotiated open access publishing agreements. In addition, the National Open Science Strategy being developed by TÜBİTAK is expected to include guidelines for fostering public-private partnerships and to emphasise the need for incentives for private entities participating in open science practices.

No national monitoring framework for open science is reported.

Several national open science infrastructures are managed by TÜBİTAK’s National Academic Network and Information Centre (ULAKBİM). Aperta is a national multi-disciplinary repository that hosts and provides access to scientific outputs, including academic publications, research data and other materials. Harman is a national umbrella search engine that aggregates metadata on research outputs from institutional open access repositories across the country, providing a single access point to these resources. According to the report, over 160 higher education institutions have established institutional repositories compliant with international standards. DergiPark provides electronic hosting and editorial process management for open access journals (approximately 2,904 at the time of reporting), most of which are no-fee (diamond) journals.

 Türkiye reports hosting or funding several federated regional and international infrastructures. TÜBİTAK ULAKBİM contributes to EOSC, promoting access to research data and services across Europe. Türkiye also contributes to the Sponsoring Consortium for Open Access Publishing in Particle Physics (SCOAP³) through TÜBİTAK.

The report does not describe the country’s involvement in North-South, North-South-South or South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use.

Systematic capacity building on open science has been carried out in higher education and research institutions by YÖK and TÜBİTAK; however, no further details are provided.

 

The report does not address whether capacity-building programmes specifically for policymakers have been implemented, whether open science competencies have been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula, whether open educational resources have been used as instruments for open science capacity building, or whether science communication initiatives aligned with open science values have been undertaken.

The report indicates that initiatives to review research assessment and evaluation processes to align them with open science principles exist but provides no further details.

 No incorporation of specific clauses recognising open science practices in career evaluation and progression systems is reported.

 Regarding initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices, the report confirms the existence of such initiatives, pointing in particular to TÜBİTAK’s expectations and requirements for supported research projects related to open science practices.
The report does not address the questions covered by this section.

Türkiye promotes international collaboration on open science by aligning with the European Union's open science policy and the UNESCO Recommendation. The country participates, through IZTECH, in OpenAIRE and the Confederation of Open Access Repositories (COAR), and its involvement in Horizon 2020 and Horizon Europe has driven the adoption of open science practices, as these programmes require adherence to open access publishing and data sharing. Through its participation in these initiatives, the country has also been involved in discussions on the establishment of regional and international funding mechanisms for open science.

No specific formal strategy or plan for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science is reported.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science was disseminated by the Ministry for Innovation and Technology and by the Secretariat of the Hungarian National Commission for UNESCO to appropriate government agencies and other relevant institutions. Additional bodies involved in the promotion include the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade, the Hungarian Academy of Sciences and the Hungarian Research Network (HUN-REN).

Awareness-raising activities on open science span various conferences, lectures and publications. For example, in 2021, the National Research, Development and Innovation (NRDI) Office, a funding agency for scientific research, published a national Position Paper on Open Science clearly defining the concept and its key pillars; and in March 2023, it hosted a “Tripartite Event” about Open Science, supported by the European Commission and the European Open Science Cloud (EOSC) Association, to discuss its status in Hungary. In 2021, the Governmental Agency for IT Development (KIFÜ) and the National Library of the University of Debrecen initiated the first Hungarian Open Science Forum, which has since served as a regular platform for raising awareness among national stakeholders. In 2022, KIFÜ delivered an introductory Open Science Lecture Series on 22 occasions at eight higher education venues, reaching more than 370 participants.

Hungary's Research, Development and Innovation Strategy (2021–2030) broadly addresses the open engagement of societal actors and cooperation across the research, development and innovation ecosystem, as an element of open science.

 

The national legal framework supporting open science includes Act LXXVI of 1999 on Copyright, which was amended to address issues related to open access to research publications, particularly by allowing authors to retain certain rights over publicly funded research work, thereby enabling its deposit in open access repositories, and Act CXII of 2011 on the Right to Informational Self-Determination, which establishes requirements for transparency and access to publicly funded data held by public institutions, including research data.

The report states that no formal national open science implementation plan or strategy has been adopted, though the above-mentioned Position Paper on Open Science, initiated by the NRDI Office, represents a national declaration jointly developed and signed by government bodies, including the Ministry for Innovation and Technology, and umbrella organisations from the research and higher education sector, calling on all stakeholders in the scientific community to support the promotion of open science practices based on the directions articulated in the document. Specifically, it outlines the main pillars of the open science ecosystem in Hungary, including open access to research outputs, Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable (FAIR) and Collective Benefit, Authority to Control, Responsibility and Ethics (CARE) data management, new research evaluation systems, new types of incentives, training and citizen science, among others.

 

Regarding institutional-level open science policies, the report indicates their existence but does not refer to formal policy documents adopted by individual research institutions or universities.

 Dedicated national funding mechanisms for open science are described as being under development, with a reference to current initiatives such as investments in transformative agreements with publishers enabling open access publishing. Open science practices have been integrated into existing research funding schemes, such as the Research Grant Hungary (RGH_24), managed by the NRDI Office, while the NRDI Office plans to further encourage the adoption of open science practices by incorporating open science criteria into funding calls.


The report states that Hungary does not have a national monitoring framework for open science. However, the country contributes to and

benefits from EOSC monitoring frameworks and indicators assessing the implementation and impact of open science policies.

Hungary operates several national open science infrastructures. The Hungarian Open Access Repositories (HUNOR) consortium, established in 2008 by academic libraries and the Library of the Hungarian Academy of Sciences, operates a national network of institutional open access repositories, including repositories maintained by universities such as Corvinus University of Budapest and Eötvös Loránd University (ELTE). The Hungarian Scientific Publication Database (MTMT) is national bibliographic database of scientific publications and citations that provides a single access point for searching publication records and links to available full texts. The HUN-REN Cloud delivers European-standard cloud computing and data storage to the research community and contributes to EOSC. The HUN-REN Data Repository Platform (HUN-REN ARP), available to users from March 2024, is a national repository infrastructure for long-term research data management, preservation, sharing and reuse in accordance with FAIR principles. The Hungarian Archives is a free curated digital archive established by the American Hungarian Library and Historical Society that provides access to historically significant pre-digital documents and photographs.
 
Hungary also contributes to federated European and international open science infrastructures through its membership in the EOSC Association and participation in related projects, including the NI4OS-Europe, EGI-ACE and SLICES-SC.
 
Hungary does not report participation in North-South or North-South-South collaborations related to shared open science infrastructures.

Systematic capacity building on open science has taken place in higher education and research institutions. In this regard, the report refers to support structures for open access publishing and research data management, including institutional open access repositories, university libraries as well as data steward positions operating through the HUN-REN Data Steward Network.

 

The report states, however, that open science competencies have not been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula. Hungary's Digital Education Strategy, launched in 2016, addresses digital competencies across all education levels, but the report indicates that a specific open science framework within research skills curricula remains under development.

 

Open educational resources were reported as supporting open science capacity building,  as illustrated by the Open Science Lecture Series delivered by KIFÜ for higher education institutions in 2022.

 

The report confirms the existence of science communication initiatives in line with open science values, including the World Science Forum, a biennial international conference on global science policy organised in Budapest since 2003 that serves as a platform for dialogue between the scientific community and society.

The above-mentioned national Position Paper on Open Science includes the development of new research evaluation systems as one of the pillars of the open science ecosystem in Hungary. The Position Paper recommends that all research assessment organisations publish freely accessible guidelines, including a description of the criteria and methodology used for assessment, and calls for broader recognition of research outputs and activities, such as the reviewer work, including within career assessment systems. However, in practice, specific clauses recognising open science practices have not yet been incorporated into career evaluation and progression systems. In addition, the Position Paper encourages the creation of new types of rewards and incentives as an additional pillar of open science, particularly through the inclusion of open science as a criterion in domestic research funding mechanisms.

 

The report identifies unintended negative consequences of open science practices, including predatory publishing. In response, the Presidium of the Hungarian Academy of Sciences established a committee in 2023 to develop measures against predatory publishing practices, with its recommendations issued in 2024.

The report indicates the existence of initiatives at both national and institutional levels promoting innovative, participatory approaches at different stages of the scientific process. Areas addressed by these initiatives include open peer review practices, participatory methods valuing inputs from societal actors, such as citizen science, and the development of participatory strategies for identifying the needs of marginalised communities and integrating socially relevant issues into research agendas. While no concrete examples of such initiatives are provided, the national Position Paper on Open Science identifies citizen science as one of the key pillars of the open science ecosystem in Hungary.

Regarding international cooperation on open science, the report refers to the Position Paper on Open Science, a national declaration and strategic framework promoting cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration as one of the pillars of the open science ecosystem in Hungary. In this regard, it encourages Hungarian scientific and research organisations to join EOSC. This is reflected in Hungary’s participation in EOSC-related initiatives and projects discussed above, connecting Hungarian open science infrastructure to European infrastructures. Another reported area of international cooperation is the annual one-day international Focus on Open Science conference, organised as part of the World Science Forum by the Library and Information Centre of the Hungarian Academy of Sciences in collaboration with the Association of European Research Libraries (LIBER) and the Scientific Knowledge Services (SKS).

 

The report also refers to the availability of support for the internationalisation of the Hungarian research community through the NRDI Office's Science Patronage Programme, launched in 2021 with an initial allocation of 1 billion Hungarian forints (approximately 2.6 million euros). The programme supports the Hungarian research community's involvement in international scientific platforms and events, fostering the advancement of open science; a portion of this budget is specifically dedicated to supporting open access publications. In 2024, the programme received increased funding of nearly 2.5 billion forints.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has not been promoted or shared with relevant ministries and institutions in Togo.

Certain awareness-raising activities related to open science have nevertheless been identified, particularly at institutional level, including an information and awareness day on DICAMES, the regional repository of the African and Malagasy Council for Higher Education (CAMES), organised by the University of Kara for its academic community in January 2025.

A national science, technology and innovation (STI) policy and its action plan were validated at a stakeholder workshop in June 2014 but have not been formally adopted by the government. A national research and innovation policy was likewise developed by the Ministry of Higher Education and Research and validated on 22 December 2020, though it too remains unadopted. The report does not describe open science elements within either document. The report also mentions the Togo Digital Strategy (Stratégie Togo Digital), a government initiative supporting digital transformation, including internet access and online services, though it is not reported to contain elements directly related to open science.

 

No national policy or legal and regulatory framework specifically addressing open science is reported.

 

Institutional open science policies are also reported as absent. The report notes that universities and research centres have not yet integrated open science into their internal policies or established dedicated committees. The University of Lomé has, however, begun to encourage open access practices, including the deposit of theses and dissertations in open archives.
