Regarding specific national funding mechanisms for open science, the report indicates that research funding is predominantly of foreign origin and that few national mechanisms specifically support open science, with no further details provided.

The report does not explicitly address the question of the availability of national or institutional open science infrastructures; however, a few enabling developments and relevant initiatives are identified throughout the report. TogoRER serves as the national research and education network, providing internet connectivity and associated services to higher education institutions and research centres. In addition, Togo reports that some of its universities, such as the University of Lomé and the University of Kara, use external regional and international open access repositories/archives (e.g., DECAMES, HAL and Zenodo) for depositing theses and publications.  

  

Togo's involvement in international collaborations related to open science infrastructures includes TogoRER’s membership in the West and Central African Research and Education Network (WACREN), which federates national research and education networks across the region, and its participation in the LIBSENSE project. Coordinated by WACREN with support from UNESCO and the Agence Universitaire de la Francophonie (AUF), LIBSENSE promotes the development of open science infrastructures and services across West Africa, including institutional repositories and their interoperability. DICAMES, a regional open access repository within the CAMES space, also facilitates South-South collaboration.

Capacity building on open science in higher education institutions has taken several forms in Togo. The Universities of Lomé and Kara organised training workshops on the use of open archives, particularly DICAMES and HAL, in partnership with CAMES and WACREN (through TogoRER); these targeted researchers, librarians and doctoral students. The Directorate of Research and Innovation (DRI) at the University of Lomé periodically organises training on online thesis deposit, the use of persistent identifiers for researchers such as Open Researcher and Contributor ID (ORCID) and ResearcherID, and the ethical use of scientific sources and open access publishing. Togo also participated in regional capacity- building activities through the LIBSENSE project and the AfricaConnect programme, which included training on research data management and open licences such as Creative Commons. In addition, the AUF, through the Francophone Digital Campus (Campus Numérique Francophone, CNF), supports training for researchers on open access scientific publishing and the use of open educational resources.

The report states that as of May 2025, no initiative at national level has been formally launched to review research assessment and evaluation processes to align them with open science principles. At institutional level, however, the DRI at the University of Lomé has begun to give greater weight to open access publications in internal evaluation procedures, particularly in decisions related to academic promotion, has encouraged deposit in DICAMES and other open archives, and is engaged in informal discussions on broadening evaluation criteria to include scientific popularisation activities, open data production and the reuse of research results. Through its membership in CAMES, which is currently revising its scientific evaluation criteria, Togo participates indirectly in regional reflections on integrating open science values into researcher assessment. The LIBSENSE project also promotes dialogue on alternative indicators beyond journal impact factor.

The report states that there are several emerging initiatives at institutional level to promote innovative and participatory methodological changes at different stages of the research cycle, although as of May 2025 no structured national approach or explicit national policies exist. For example, some research projects, notably Valorising Research Results and Innovation in West Africa (VaRRIWA), Forest for Future (F4F) and Eba4Goals, integrate local actors in the co-construction of research questions and data collection; the public accessibility of resulting data in findable, accessible, interoperable and reusable (FAIR)-compliant repositories nevertheless remains limited. At the doctoral level, some supervisors encourage students submit preprints (although this remains a rare practice) and to involve communities in validating research hypotheses (particularly in anthropology, forestry and social sciences).

Togo pursues international scientific collaboration on open science through several channels. Key partnerships include CAMES, particularly through its regional repository DICAMES; WACREN, including through the participation in in the LIBSENSE project promoting open science infrastructure development across West Africa; and the AUF, through the Francophone Digital Campus, which supports open science-related training for Togolese researchers.  

 

The report states, however, that Togo does not have a specific strategy or plan for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has not been promoted or shared with appropriate ministries and institutions in Timor-Leste. No awareness-raising activities on open science are reported as having been organised or foreseen by the end of 2025 as well.

No national policy or legal framework addressing open science is reported. The developing research ecosystem, limited institutional capacity, lack of technical infrastructure, funding constraints, and limited awareness and technical expertise on open science among researchers and policymakers are cited as obstacles. The absence of a national science, technology and innovation (STI) policy has also contributed to delays in formalising open science governance. However, such a policy is currently under development by the National Institute of Science and Technology (INCT) in collaboration with the Organisation of African, Caribbean and Pacific States (OACPS) through its Policy Support Facility (PSF), with the Ministry of Higher Education, Science, and Culture (MESCC), the National University of Timor-Leste (UNTL) and the Dili Institute of Technology (DIT) among the stakeholders involved. The policy is expected to incorporate open science elements, including open access to research through the establishment of a national digital repository for storing, managing and sharing research outputs.

 Institutional-level open science policies are reported as under development, though no further details are provided.

 Dedicated national funding mechanisms for open science are reported as being under early development. At the same time, efforts are being made to integrate open science practices into broader research funding mechanisms, including those supported by INCT, although such integration has not yet been formalised owing to the absence of a formal policy framework promoting open science.

 No efforts to build equitable public-private partnerships on open science are reported, with limited collaboration in general between public research institutions and the private sector, as well as the absence of a formal national open science policy, cited as barriers.

 A national monitoring framework for open science is under development as part of the STI policy development process, with no formal indicators yet finalised.

National and institutional open science infrastructures in Timor-Leste are reported as being under development. As noted above, a national repository for research outputs and data is being developed, under the leadership of INCT and with support of OACPS. Several higher education institutions, including UNTL and DIT, have been developing their institutional repositories for research outputs. The Timor-Leste National Library also contributes through archiving and digitising materials.
  
The report states that Timor-Leste does not host or fund any federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science, and that existing international open science infrastructures are not accessible to the country's researchers. No involvement in North-South, North-South-South or South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use or develop joint strategies for shared open science platforms is reported.

The report states that systematic capacity building on open science has not taken place in higher education or research performing institutions in Timor-Leste, citing limited awareness of open science practices, a shortage of specialised expertise, inadequate digital infrastructure and financial constraints. Existing efforts are described as fragmented and ad hoc. Nor have capacity-building programmes specifically for policymakers taken place.

 

Open science competencies have not been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula, owing to a lack of awareness among educators, digital infrastructure limitations and insufficient funding for training materials.

 

The report does not indicate that open educational resources are used for open science capacity building.

The report indicates that no initiatives have been undertaken at the national or institutional level to review research assessment and evaluation processes to align them with open science principles. The country's research ecosystem is described as in its early stages, with no structured mechanism for assessing research performance against open science criteria. No concrete plans for such reviews by the end of 2025 are reported.

 

No specific clauses recognising open science practices are reported as having been incorporated into career evaluation and progression systems. Academic institutions are reported to assess researchers primarily on traditional criteria such as teaching performance and completed research projects, rather than on open science contributions.

 

Regarding recognition and rewards for open science, the report points to the establishment of digital repositories by several institutions, including INCT, UNTL and DIT, as steps towards facilitating open access to research outputs. These initiatives do not yet include formal reward mechanisms but represent a shift toward recognising the value of open science, while the STI policy under development may lead to structured incentives.

 

The report describes unintended negative consequences of open science practices perceived in Timor-Leste, including the risk that research findings may be misused or misinterpreted without proper context, insufficient intellectual property protection for researchers and cybersecurity risks associated with digital repositories under development.

The report states that no initiatives at the national or institutional level have promoted innovative, participatory methodological or procedural changes at different stages of the research cycle to increase the openness of the scientific process. Among reported barriers is the absence of a national framework for open science governance. No formal plans for such initiatives by the end of 2025 are reported.

The report describes Timor-Leste as being in the early stages of promoting international collaboration on open science. INCT supports international research cooperation and knowledge dissemination, while UNTL and DIT engage in collaborations with foreign universities and research organisations involving knowledge exchange, joint research projects and the use of open access platforms for sharing research results. The country has engaged with the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN), the Community of Portuguese-Speaking Countries (CPLP) and United Nations research initiatives, which are described as providing opportunities to expand open science practices.

 

The report states that Timor-Leste does not have a specific strategy or plan for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science.

 

Timor-Leste is not reported as being involved in discussions on the establishment of regional or international funding mechanisms for open science, with limited participation in global and regional research funding frameworks and the absence of a national open science policy cited as barriers.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science have been promoted by several ministries and national entities, including the Ministry of Higher Education and Scientific Research (MoHESR).

Awareness-raising activities on open science have taken place in Egypt. For example, the Egyptian Knowledge Bank (EKB), a national digital library, organises workshops and information days on open science values and principles. The Egyptian Innovation Bank (EIB), which supports open innovation, indirectly raises awareness on open science. In addition, the Open Science Community Egypt (OSCE), a network founded in 2023 by professionals in librarianship and archiving, aims to raising awareness of open science in Egypt.

Egypt's National Strategy for Science, Technology and Innovation 2030, adopted in 2016 and updated in 2019, does not explicitly use the term “open science”, but it incorporates its principles through an emphasis on transparency, collaboration and knowledge sharing. The report states that Egypt does not yet have a national policy or legal framework dedicated to open science fully in accordance with the UNESCO Recommendation. However, a national open science strategy is reported as being under development by MoHESR, aiming to establish legal and institutional frameworks to promote open access to research, encourage data sharing and integrate open science principles into national research policies.

The report indicates the availability of several dedicated national funding mechanisms related to open access. For example, through the Science and Technology Development Fund (STDF), Egypt has funded a transformative agreement with Springer Nature for open access publishing. The Academy of Scientific Research and Technology (ASRT), in collaboration with EKB, has funded hosting and operational services for a national open access publishing platform comprising, at the time of reporting, 1,045 Egyptian journals, whose content is freely accessible to the public. Public funding bodies, such as ASRT and STDF, are increasingly incorporating open science requirements into their guidelines for research grants and are supporting collaborative projects and open access publications.
 
Egypt reports some efforts to foster equitable public-private partnerships on open science. In particular, the City of Scientific Research and Technological Applications (SRTA-City), which operates under MoHESR, supports open science principles by promoting knowledge sharing and collaboration between research institutions, industry and international partners.

Egypt is developing and strengthening its national monitoring framework for open science, within its broader STI policy. This includes the Egyptian Knowledge Graph (EKG), a big-data-driven research analytics platform developed by ASRT in collaboration with Elsevier and Scimago Labs that monitor and analyses national research output, collaboration patterns and research impact, and the Egyptian Science, Technology and Innovation Observatory (ESTIO), which tracks open access publishing and data sharing among its STI indicators.

Egypt has established several national open science infrastructures. The National Science and Technology Grid and Cloud Computing Centre (STGC³) provides high-performance computing capabilities for advanced research and hosts several national infrastructures, including the EKB. The EKB is a national digital library and portal providing access to global scientific literature and educational resources across Egypt. As noted above, there is also a national open access publishing platform, hosting 1,045 Egyptian journals. Egypt also maintains a national platform providing open educational resources for K-12 students. The National Bank for Scientific Laboratories and Equipment (NBSLE) catalogues scientific equipment across universities to facilitate access and collaborative use. The Egyptian National Science, Technology and Innovation Network (ENSTINET) provides internet connectivity to all public universities, research centres and 3,000 secondary schools.

 

Egypt participates in North-South, North-South-South and South-South collaborations related to open science infrastructure. These include collaboration on digital connectivity infrastructure and research and education networks through participation in the EU-funded EUMEDPlus and MEDUSA projects, as well as partnerships with ASREN (Arab States Research & Education Network), GÉANT (Europe) and Internet2 (USA). Future plans include expanding the services of the EKB regionally through partnerships with the Association of Arab Universities and the Federation of Arab Scientific Research Councils.

Systematic capacity building on open science is taking place across Egypt's higher education and research institutions. For example, ASRT conducts training workshops as part of a national initiative to transition Egyptian journals to open access, covering topics such as open access publishing for editors-in-chief and authors, scientific manuscript writing, peer review and plagiarism. The report indicates that standalone capacity-building programmes specifically targeted at policymakers are not developed. However, workshops, seminars and conferences organised by ASRT and STDF, which bring together researchers, policymakers and other stakeholders, provide informal capacity-building opportunities.

 

The report acknowledges that fully incorporating open science competencies into higher education research skills curricula faces challenges, primarily due to resource limitations and funding sustainability.

 

The use of open educational resources for open science capacity building is reported as partial.

 

The report indicates the existence of several science communication initiatives in line with open science values, including conferences and public events organised by ASRT and STDF to disseminate scientific knowledge and promote public engagement.

The report indicates that reviewing research assessment and evaluation processes to align them with open science principles remains an area for future potential development. The report also states that specific clauses recognising open science practices in career evaluation and progression systems are not yet widely implemented, with traditional metrics such as publications in high-impact journals still dominating. No explicit mechanisms to recognise and reward open science practices are described.

 

Regarding unintended negative consequences of open science practices, the report identifies the cost and sustainability of open access publishing as a challenge. Article processing charges (APCs) are described as creating barriers for researchers, particularly in developing countries.

Several initiatives promote innovative approaches at different stages of the research cycle to increase the openness of the scientific process. For example, the Egyptian Innovation Bank (EIB) platform, launched by ASRT, encourages open innovation and participatory methods by creating a space in which the public, researchers, academics and industry partners can engage in problem-solving and the development of solutions.

Egypt promotes international collaboration mainly in relation to open science infrastructure and connectivity services, as noted above. It also actively participates in the UNESCO-UNICEF Gateways to Public Digital Learning Initiative; in May 2024, it facilitated a case study visit under this initiative with the participation of 21 countries, focused on sharing best practices in developing and sustaining the EKB platform, demonstrating its commitment to collaboration and knowledge exchange in the realm of digital learning and open access.

 

Egypt is also involved in discussions on regional and international funding mechanisms for open science, including through the exploration of sustainable funding models for the regional and international expansion of EKB services.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been shared with the Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation (MHESI) and the Office of National Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation Policy Council (NXPO).

 The report states that awareness-raising activities have included forums, seminars and open multi-stakeholder discussions on the benefits and challenges of open science to disseminate scientific knowledge to scholars, decision-makers and the public. The International Synthetic Biology Conference (SynBio Consortium 2024), hosted in Thailand in November 2024, is cited as an example; however, the report does not clarify whether the open science agenda was explicitly addressed within the conference programme.

Thailand's national science, technology and innovation (STI) policy framework, comprising the Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation Policy and the National Science, Research and Innovation Plan 2023–2027, is reported as incorporating some elements associated with open science.

In addition, two NXPO policy instruments are reported as supporting open science principles by promoting open innovation, collaborative policymaking and open engagement of societal actors. The Policy Innovation Platform for the Better Future (PIP) is a crowdsourcing platform piloted during the COVID-19 pandemic that bridges policymakers and the public through a competition-based model aimed at generating innovative policy responses to societal challenges. The Thailand Higher Education and Innovation Policy Accelerator (THIPA) promotes innovation-driven and collaborative policymaking by engaging policymakers and stakeholders in co-developing solutions through design thinking approaches.

According to the report, open science practices have been integrated into and supported through existing research funding mechanisms, including the Program Management Unit for Competitiveness (PMU-C) as part of its broader efforts to fosters collaboration between public and private sectors in Thailand and internationally.

Thailand reports the existence of certain open science infrastructures, including, among others, platforms for exchanges and co-creation of knowledge between scientists and society and STI-related monitoring and information systems. Examples highlighted in the report include the Thailand Research and Education Network (ThaiREN), the above-mentioned PIP crowdsourcing platform and the Talent Utilization Platform. The latter is an information system designed to support research and innovation policy planning through the integration, analysis and dissemination of data on researchers and other high-potential talent across ten strategic sectors, including quantum technology, space technology, energy physics and social sciences.

Thailand reports that systematic capacity building relevant to open science has taken place in higher education, referring to the STEMPlus platform, which brings together industry, academia and government to develop skilled workforces for current and future labour market demands. Since its inception, the platform has certified over 800 training courses in Science, Technology, Engineering and Mathematics (STEM), upskilling 80,000 workers. However, the report does not clarify whether open science is explicitly included in the training programmes offered through the platform.

 The report also indicates that open science competencies has been incorporated into higher education curricula, referring to the Higher Education Plan for Manpower Planning and Development 2023–2027, although no further details are provided.

 Science communication in line with open science values is supported through FameLab Thailand, a programme launched in 2015 with support from what is now MHESI, universities and media partners. The programme promotes public awareness of science and, among its reported outcomes, has contributed to improved research collaborations, the establishment of science communication courses in universities and the creation of a school-level spin-off competition, School Lab Thailand.
The report does not address the questions covered by this section.

The report indicates the existence of initiatives promoting participatory approaches at different stages of the scientific process, particularly in relation to the development of participatory strategies for identifying the needs of marginalised communities and highlighting socially relevant issues to be incorporated into research agendas. As an example, at the time of reporting, NXPO and Thailand Policy Lab were developing a Virtual Platform for Citizen-Centric Policymaking to engage the public throughout the STI policy design process, building on PIP, which had been piloted during the COVID-19 pandemic.

Thailand reports promoting international collaboration on open science through the organisation of multi-stakeholder forums and conferences, participation in global research centres and consortia and collaboration with international organisations, including UNESCO. However, the report provides no further details on the nature or scope of these activities.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted through the Ministry of Higher Education, Scientific Research and Innovation (MESRSI), Mohammed V University of Rabat and Ibn Tofail University of Kénitra.

A range of awareness-raising activities on open science have taken place since early 2024. The Islamic World Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (ICESCO) Chair on Open Education, based at Mohammed V University, delivered training sessions for doctoral students and researchers in January 2024 and April 2024 that covered copyright, open licences and open science. In October 2024, Cadi Ayyad University hosted the first International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Open Science, organised in collaboration with ICESCO. Morocco also hosted and participated in a regional symposium on open science in the Arab region in November 2024, organised by UNESCO in partnership with the Arab League Educational, Cultural and Scientific Organization (ALECSO) and Mohammed V University in Rabat. In addition, the Moroccan Institute of Scientific and Technical Information (IMIST), a division of the National Centre for Scientific and Technical Research (CNRST), organised webinars on research data management, persistent identifiers (in collaboration with DataCite), and copyright and licensing for open access journals (in collaboration with the Directory of Open Access Journals, DOAJ). It also conducted a few sessions on an open-source journal management system used by the Portal of Moroccan Scientific Journals (PRSM), which it maintains.

Morocco's science, technology and innovation policy, the National Strategy for the Development of Scientific Research to 2025, does not include open science elements. However, the MESRSI is developing a new strategy with a dedicated open science axis.
 
Two legal and regulatory instruments partially address open science. The National Scientific and Pedagogical Standards for the Doctoral Cycle (Ministerial Order No. 1886.23, 1 August 2024) requires the publication and indexation of defended theses, except where intellectual property clauses apply. Framework Law 51-17 (9 August 2019) on education, training and scientific research contains provisions (including Article 3 and 5) promoting the dissemination of scientific knowledge.
 
At the time of reporting, no dedicate national implementation plan for open science was in place, although a draft national strategy on open educational resources and open science had been prepared and was undergoing validation through stakeholder consultations with a view to its adoption.
 
At the institutional level, open science policies are being under development.
 
As specific national funding mechanisms for open science, the report indicates that MESRSI, through CNRST, provides free hosting for journals on PRSM and free Digital Object Identifier (DOI) attribution for articles published in PRSM-hosted journals. Open science practices are reported as not being integrated into existing research funding mechanisms, although the draft national strategy on open science envisages such integration. Nevertheless, some research projects managed and partially funded by the CNRST require researchers to publish results in open access journals.
 
No efforts to promote equitable public-private partnerships on open science are reported. No national monitoring framework for open science is reported.

Regarding national and institutional open science infrastructures, the report states that these are generally not yet in place in Morocco. For example, the report notes that several Moroccan universities previously developed open archives, which are no longer operational. At the same time, other sections of the report identify several relevant national-level infrastructures and services provided by the CNRST, through its IMIST division: PRSM, which hosts domestic scientific journals across various disciplines and offers open access digital publishing service; the National Catalogue of Theses (Toubkal), which provides open access archiving of theses from Moroccan public universities; and a national DOI attribution service through DataCite. In addition, a national open access repository is noted as being under development.
 
Morocco does not report hosting or funding any federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science. It notes, however, that existing regional and international open science infrastructures are accessible in the country.
 
Morocco does not report participation in North-South, North-South-South or South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use or develop joint strategies for shared open science platforms.

Systematic capacity building on open science in higher education and research institutions has not been established; the report attributes this to the absence of a dedicated national open science policy in Morocco. Capacity building programmes for policymakers are likewise not reported.

 

Open science competencies have not been incorporated into higher education research skills curricula. The report indicates such integration is planned once the national open science policy is adopted.

 

The use of open educational resources for open science capacity building is reported as partial, without further detail.

No initiatives to review existing research assessment and evaluation processes in line with open science principles are reported. The report notes that such a review is likely to begin after adoption of the draft national open science policy.

 

No specific clauses recognising open science practices in career evaluation and progression systems are reported. However, the report notes that a revised statute governing teacher-researchers in higher education (Decree No. 2-23-545 of 2 August 2023), together with its implementing texts being under development at the time of reporting, may help promote open science practices following the adoption of the national open science policy.

 

No initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices are reported.

 

No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.

The report does not describe any initiatives at the national or institutional level promoting innovative, participatory methodological or procedural changes at different stages of the research cycle. It states that the adoption of open science is relatively recent in Morocco and that it is premature to pursue such changes before the scientific community has been sufficiently familiarised with and trained in open science principles and approaches.

Morocco promotes international collaboration on open science primarily through hosting scientific events and participating in international forums. As mentioned above, the country hosted a regional symposium on open science in the Arab region in November 2024, initiated by UNESCO, while Cadi Ayyad University hosted the first International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Open Science in collaboration with ICESCO in October 2024.

 

No specific strategy for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science is reported. However, the report notes that the draft national open science policy envisages fostering future cooperation, particularly with the EU, African countries, Arab countries, Francophone countries and Latin American countries.

 

Morocco does not report involvement in discussions on the establishment of regional or international funding mechanisms for open science.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted to the Ministry of Education and Research, the National Research Authority, the Executive Agency for Higher Education, Research, Development and Innovation Funding (UEFISCDI), the Romanian National Commission for UNESCO and the UNESCO Chair on Science and Innovation Policies at the National University of Political Studies and Public Administration (SNSPA).

Awareness-raising activities have been carried out since 2020, beginning with a face-to-face meeting convened by UEFISCDI, Romania’s main competitive research funding agency, in March 2020 to launch the consultation process for a national open science strategic framework. Subsequent events, organised predominantly by UEFISCDI through its Open Science Knowledge Hub Romania (OSKH), covered topics including research data management, Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable (FAIR) data practices, the European Open Science Cloud (EOSC), open access, open education and research assessment, often in collaboration with the UNESCO Chair, the National Institute for Research and Development in Informatics (ICI Bucharest) and the National Institute for Research and Development for Physics and Nuclear Engineering "Horia Hulubei" (IFIN-HH). For example, in November 2024, the Rethinking Research Assessment Conference was held in Romania, co-organised with Science Europe, the Coalition for Advancing Research Assessment (CoARA) and the Open and Universal Science project (OPUS).
Continuous dissemination of information on open science and awareness of related developments and events is carried out through the national open science portal, which provides news, resources and guidance, through the OSKH webpage, as well as through the Open Science Community within the BrainMap platform.

Romania's science, technology and innovation policy, the National Strategy for Research, Innovation and Smart Specialisation (SNCISI) 2022-2027, includes a specific objective (OS.1.2) on open science, covering open access to publications, data management plans and FAIR data, digital infrastructures including repositories for the long-term storage of open access publications, citizen science, skills development, research assessment reform, as well as the establishment and implementation of a national support and coordination mechanism for the transition to open science. The White Book of the Transition to Open Science 2023-2030, developed by UEFISCDI, further elaborates SNCISI Objective OS.1.2 by setting out eight strategic directions and related actions.

 

Institutional open science policies are reported as under development. A 2023 survey among members of the Romanian National Open Science Cloud Initiative (RO-NOSCI) found that two institutions (one university and one research institute) had developed an open science policy, while six others were developing one.

 

Regarding national funding mechanisms supporting open science,  the National Plan for Research, Development and Innovation 2022-2027 (PNCDI IV), the SNCISI's main implementing instrument, outlines funding programmes and proposes certain types of open science-related activities to be funded, including institutional capacity building, open access publication costs, research data management, EOSC participation, science communication and citizen science. Beyond these measures, the report highlights that article processing charges are eligible costs in publicly funded research under Government Decision 134/2011. In addition, Anelis Plus, a consortium of universities, research institutes and central university libraries, supports open access through transformative agreements with publishers. Open science practices have also been integrated into existing research funding through the Centres of Excellence instrument, which since 2023 requires applicants to describe open science practices and submit FAIR-compliant data management plans. These requirements are being extended to other research funding instruments.

 

The report does not describe efforts to foster equitable public-private partnerships on open science.

 

A national monitoring framework for open science is under development but not yet in place. The SNCISI, further elaborated by the White Book of the Transition to Open Science 2023–2030, envisages the development of open science monitoring mechanisms, including indicators such as the share of Web of Science-indexed open access publications and the number of scientific articles with open access research datasets.

Romania has a range of national and institutional open science infrastructures and related services. These include the above-mentioned national open science portal, which serves as an open science awareness-raising platform, and the BrainMap platform, which supports interaction, collaboration and the sharing of research outputs among researchers and innovators. Several digital repositories operate in Romania, among them a doctoral thesis repository, the Romanian Social Data Archive (RODA) and institutional repositories of the National Research and Development Institute for Industrial Ecology and the Lucian Blaga Central University Library of Cluj-Napoca. Citizen science platforms have been established by Babeș-Bolyai University and the West University of Timișoara. Cloud computing and high-performance computing infrastructures include CLOUDIFIN, the high-performance computing infrastructure at IFIN-HH, and CLOUDUT, the research cloud infrastructure at the Technical University of Cluj-Napoca. RO-NOSCI, established in June 2021 by UEFISCDI, ICI Bucharest and IFIN-HH, is a consortium supporting data-sharing among Romanian research institutions. The PNCDI IV envisages support for Romania's participation in and integration into the EOSC, though implementation has not yet begun.

 

The report states that Romania does not host or fund any federated regional or international information technology infrastructure for open science, and that existing international open science infrastructures are not accessible to all relevant Romanian actors. Romania does not report involvement in North-South or North-South-South collaborations for shared open science platforms.

Systematic capacity building on open science has taken place in higher education and research institutions. For example, UEFISCDI, a partner in the Horizon Europe FAIR-IMPACT project, provided structured support, expert guidance and training related to FAIR implementation plans. However, no capacity building programmes targeted at policymakers are reported.

 

Courses on open science for doctoral candidates have been offered through the UNESCO Chair on Science and Innovation Policies at SNSPA since 2022. A framework of open science competencies to be incorporated into higher education curricula is foreseen through a recently launched national project financed through the European Social Fund (ESF), Adapting the Educational Offer to Labour Market Demands through Evidence-based Policy Making. Under Activity 4.4, the project is expected to assess current national training offerings and develop recommendations for an open science competency framework.

 

The report states that open educational resources are not used as an instrument for open science capacity building.

 

Science communication initiatives in line with open science values include the Science in Schools programme, which promotes Science, Technology, Engineering, Arts and Mathematics (STEAM) education. The Research in the Spotlight podcast, produced by UEFISCDI with European Economic Area (EEA) and Norway Grants funding, has been recognised by Science Europe as a best practice in science communication.

Objective 7 of the White Book of the Transition to Open Science 2023-2030 outlines specific actions for reviewing research evaluation processes, developing new criteria and ensuring transparency of evaluation methods. Twenty-five Romanian institutions are CoARA members, and UEFISCDI has developed a CoARA Action Plan for 2025-2028 covering narrative CVs, responsible use of metrics and recognition of open science in funding calls. UEFISCDI is also examining performance-based research funding mechanisms within the project Romania on the Horizon 2030.

 

Career evaluation and progression systems partially recognise open science. Recently adopted through a ministerial order, national minimum standards for academic titles acknowledge open access publishing, though journals charging mandatory publication fees are often excluded from evaluation criteria. Other practices such as open data, open peer review and citizen science are not incorporated, with the exception that preprints on arXiv are recognised in mathematics.

 

Unintended negative consequences of open science have been reported. In 2021, debates arose over the quality of publications in the Multidisciplinary Digital Publishing Institute (MDPI), with concerns about rapid expansion and the proliferation of special issues. Discussion also addressed the financial burden of article processing charges on institutions and a potential shift from research quality to quantity.

The report identifies initiatives promoting innovative, participatory methodological changes to increase the openness of the scientific process. The areas addressed are participatory methods involving societal actors, such as citizen science, and the development of participatory strategies for identifying the needs of marginalised communities for incorporation into research agendas. For example, as noted above, PNCDI IV supports projects with citizen science components among the proposes types of open science-related activities to be funded.

Romania promotes international collaboration on open science primarily through UEFISCDI's OSKH, which serves as the OpenAIRE National Open Access Desk (NOAD), the Research Data Alliance (RDA) Node, and the main national contact point for open science. This coordination role is exercised through participation in Horizon Europe projects such as the next Generation Research Assessment to Promote Open Science project (GraspOS), OPUS, Sustainable Careers for Research Empowerment (SECURE) and FAIR-IMPACT. Romania is a member of the EOSC Association, the Council for National Open Science Coordination (CoNOSC) and Science Europe working groups on open science and research culture. In the area of research assessment reform, Romanian institutions are CoARA members. Collaboration with UNESCO is maintained through the UNESCO Working Groups on Open Science and the UNESCO Chair at SNSPA.

 

Romania does not report, however, having a specific strategy or plan for stimulating cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science. It does not report involvement in discussions on the establishment of regional or international funding mechanisms for open science.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been disseminated among the national authorities and entities, including the Ministry of Education and Higher Education (MEHE), the Qatar National Research Fund (QNRF), the Qatar Research, Development and Innovation (QRDI) Council and Qatar Foundation.

Several awareness-raising activities on open science have taken place. The QRDI Council, collaborating with ministries, universities, research institutes and industry partners, organised national consultations and workshops to build a shared understanding of open science. Qatar University (QU) Library promotes open science awareness through workshops on open access and copyright and through activities organised during the International Open Access Week. In 2024, Qatar National Library (QNL) hosted the third annual Forum for Open Research in MENA (FORM) to raise awareness of open science policies and practices across the Arab region. In addition, Springer Nature and QNL held open access awareness events linked to a QNL agreement supporting book processing charges for researchers at higher education and non-profit research institutions.

Qatar's science, technology and innovation policy framework is anchored in Qatar National Vision 2030, formulated in 2008, which promotes innovation, research and international collaboration without explicitly referencing open science. The QRDI 2030 Strategy, adopted by the QRDI Council in 2020, more explicitly supports elements of open science, including open access to publicly funded research, the development of open science infrastructure such as digital repositories and open engagement of societal actors. The report notes that no standalone national policy or legal framework specifically addressing open science exists, though several instruments embed its principles within the broader research ecosystem.

As an example of an institutional open science policy, the report refers to QU's Institutional Repository Policy, which establishes a framework for systematic collection, preservation and open dissemination of the university’s scholarly outputs.

Dedicated national funding mechanisms supporting open science are implemented through programmes under the QRDI Council’s Scientific Research, Innovation and Talent and Capability Building tracks. At the institutional level, QU provides internal funding for open access publishing. Open science practices have also been integrated into the policies of major research funding bodies. QNRF requires data management plans in grant proposals, including commitments to share data as early as possible, and encourages researchers to make research findings openly accessible through journals or repositories. In addition, the QRDI Council’s Data Management Policy requires funded researchers to develop data management plans adhering to Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable (FAIR) principles.

Qatar has taken steps to foster equitable public-private partnerships related to open science. For example, Qatar Biobank applies a collaborative data-sharing model within ethical guidelines, engaging both public and private stakeholders.

The report states that no standalone national monitoring framework for open science currently exists. The QRDI Council describes the incorporation of open science indicators into the national science, technology and innovation monitoring and evaluation framework as a priority.

Qatar maintains several national and institutional open science infrastructures. The QRDI Council has developed the Innolight platform, a national hub of information on researchers, projects, outputs, facilities, equipment and other resources, while also offering a digital collaboration space connecting key actors in the research, development and innovation ecosystem. QNL provides Qatar-based researchers with an open repository, Manara, built on Figshare, for the sharing and preservation of research outputs, while QU operates its institutional open access repository, QSpace, built on the DSpace platform. QU also maintains the “QU Press Open Journal System” platform, which hosts its diamond open access peer-reviewed journals, with authors retaining copyright. The Qatar National Research and Education Network (QNREN), managed by QU, provides high-speed connectivity linking research and educational institutions to global research infrastructure.
 
The report states that Qatar does not currently host a regional or global federated information technology infrastructure for open science but supports initiatives that contribute to the global open science infrastructure landscape. In particular, the QRDI Council has established strategic partnerships with institutions and governments in Singapore, Sweden, the United Kingdom and Spain through joint funding programmes, including the Qatar-Spain Innovation Programme (QASIP), supporting collaborative use of research infrastructures and the creation of shared platforms for research and innovation. In addition, QNL is a member of the Global Sustainability Coalition for Open Science Services (SCOSS), which promotes a collaborative funding model to support eligible open science infrastructures worldwide.

Capacity building on open science in higher education takes several forms. For example, QU runs a programme enabling young researchers to develop their research portfolio and share outputs through QSpace repository. QU also provides capacity building for the creation and use of open educational resources (OER) in educational and research practices, including support for course design and development, through the established Office for Digital Leaning and Online Education and digital learning ambassadors assigned across most colleges. QU also reports that open science competencies have been incorporated into its higher education curricula.   

 

Capacity building for policymakers is delivered primarily through the QRDI Council's Mumakin programme. The programme trains policymakers in intellectual property management and covers, among other areas, the integration of open science principles into broader research, development and innovation policy design.

 

Science communication in line with open science values is supported by several initiatives, including QU's tad Matters® programme, which trains graduate students to communicate research to general audiences, and the QRDI Council's Research Outcome Seminars (ROS), which provide a platform for grant holders to present research findings to peers and the public.

The report does not identify a formal initiative to review research evaluation processes, though it notes that QU has the potential to bring research assessment closer to open science principles in the future.

 

The report does not identify open science practices being explicitly recognised in career evaluation and progression systems as formal promotion criteria. However, the report notes that at QU co-authored publications carry significant weight in promotion guidelines and that some QU colleges include services to the community among evaluation criteria, reflecting broader collaborative and public engagement dimensions implicitly related to open science values.

 

Several initiatives recognise and reward open science practices, including QNL’s annual Open Initiative Award for best practices in open scholarship, presented during International Open Access Week.

 

Regarding unintended negative consequences of open science, the report refers to several potential challenges, including the risk that early-career researchers may be discouraged from prioritising open science due to research evaluation systems favouring traditional metrics such as publication counts and impact factors. Other potential challenges include data privacy risks and language barriers limiting the accessibility of open science for Arabic-speaking audiences. The report also notes concerns that international competitors may publish similar findings first when researchers share early findings and that premature dissemination through preprints could undermine trust in research outputs.

The report identifies initiatives promoting innovative approaches to increase the openness of the entire scientific process. For example, QNRF encourages open science practices across all stages of the research process in the projects it funds, starting from the proposal stage. QNRF also funds large interdisciplinary collaborative projects involving academia, industry and the public, and encourages researchers to use preprint servers such as arXiv or bioRxiv before formal peer review in order to accelerate dissemination and enable early community feedback. The QRDI Council encourages funded researchers to use social media and other non-traditional channels to disseminate research to broader audiences beyond academia. The report also notes that Qatar is increasingly recognising the importance of sharing negative research results, particularly at QU. The Qatar Science and Technology Park (QSTP) promotes open innovation by connecting academia with industry.

Qatar promotes international collaborations on open science, including through initiatives such as the Forum for Open Research in MENA (FORM) for the Arab region and partnerships related to open science infrastructure, as noted above. The report also states that cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration aligned with open science principles is supported through the QRDI 2030 Strategy.  

 

Qatar participates in discussions on regional and international funding mechanisms for open science. In November 2023, the QRDI Council hosted the Global Research Council's (GRC) Middle East and North Africa Regional Meeting in Doha, convening stakeholders to discuss collaborative research funding strategies and the advancement of open science. In addition, Qatar contributes to SCOSS, a collaborative funding initiative for international open science infrastructures.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science was promoted by the Ministry of Education, Science and Youth of Georgia, the Institute for Scientific and Technical Information (TECHINFORMI) of the Georgian Technical University, the Ivane Javakhishvili Tbilisi State University (TSU) National Science Library and the Horizon Europe National Office of Georgia.

The report identified several awareness-raising activities on open science. The Open Science Forum, held from 2 to 4 November 2022 and organised by the Ministry of Education, Science and Youth with the participation of the European Commission and the European Open Science Cloud (EOSC) Association, aimed to discuss the development of a national policy and strategy for open science and the alignment of research infrastructure with EOSC. In April 2023, the Georgian Research and Educational Networking Association (GRENA) and the National Science Library co-organised a workshop, "Libraries as enablers of scientific research", through the EU-supported Eastern Partnership Connect (EaP Connect) project, which covered the role of libraries in promoting open science practices.

Georgia reports that several national-level policies partly support open science principles, including the National Research and Innovation Strategy. However, a formal comprehensive national implementation plan, strategy or roadmap aligned with the 2021 UNESCO Recommendation does not yet exist and is currently under development. Several policy instruments and initiatives promoting open science are nevertheless in place, largely linked to Georgia's EU integration strategy and efforts to align with EU standards requiring open science practices through participation in the Horizon Europe (previously Horizon 2020) research and innovation framework and engagement with the EOSC.

At institutional level, open science policies are generally reported as being under development. As an example of a formal institutional policy, the report refers to an open access policy adopted by TSU in 2014, promoting free access to research publications through its repository. The report notes that, although some Georgian universities still lack formal open science policy documents and strategies, they actively participate in international initiatives and promote open science practices, especially open access to publications, including through the maintenance of their institutional repositories.
 
A dedicated national funding mechanism for open science has not yet been properly established but is reported as being under development. However, the report highlights initiatives under the Ministry of Education, Science and Youth's "Open Science" Development Support Programme, administered by the Shota Rustaveli National Science Foundation of Georgia (SRNSFG), which supported the collection of data on research activities, scientific publications and research infrastructure generated through grants funded by local or international donors since 2001, as well as making these data openly accessible. According to the report, open science practices are integrated into existing research funding primarily through EU-funded projects and SRNSFG grants.

A national monitoring framework for open science is reported as under development. The report also refers to the Erasmus+-funded BE-OPEN: Advancing Open Science in the Eastern Partnership Region project, which aims to collect and analyse statistical data on various aspects of open science implementation in Georgia and Azerbaijan at national and institutional levels.

Georgia reports the availability of several open science infrastructures. Among them, Openscience.ge is a DSpace-based national-level digital repository of Georgian scientific publications, managed by the National Science Library of Georgia and supported through funding from the DataCite Global Access Fund. The National Science Library also operates openjournals.ge, an Open Journal Systems (OJS)-based platform for domestic open access academic journals, and openlibrary.ge, a DSpace-based repository of digitised materials, including photos and historical documents. SRNSFG operates a digital library providing open access to research publications funded through state grants. Some universities, including TSU, operate institutional open access repositories. The Caucasus Research Resource Centres (CRRC)-Georgia provides a platform for open survey data on the South Caucasus, together with an analysis tool. The Georgia Research and Education Network (GRENA), connected to the pan-European GÉANT, provides information technology services, including cloud services, for the research and education community.

 

Georgia contributes to federated regional and international open science infrastructure through several projects, including participation in the EOSC. Under the National Initiatives for Open Science in Europe (NI4OS-Europe) project (2019–2022), several Georgian resources were integrated into EOSC, including the GRENA cloud infrastructure and the National Science Library’s openscience.ge repository.

Capacity building on open science has taken place in Georgian higher education and research institutions. In particular, Georgia has participated in several EU-backed projects facilitating open science capacity building in research institutions, including EOSC-driven initiatives such as NI4OS-Europe (funded through Horizon 2020) and broader regional programmes such as BE-OPEN (funded through Erasmus+). The report does not record, however, capacity-building programmes specifically dedicated to policymakers on integrating open science values into science, technology and innovation policies.
 
Regarding open science competencies in higher education curricula, the report does not confirm formal integration but suggests that through ongoing partnerships with EU institutions and programmes such as Horizon Europe, Georgian universities may incorporate such competencies by 2025.

​

The report does not identify initiatives to review research assessment and evaluation processes to align them with open science principles, though it notes ongoing conversations within academic and research community.

 

Regarding open science practices in career evaluation and progression systems, the report states that Georgia has not yet fully integrated such provisions at the national level, while noting a move towards greater integration, linked in part to Georgia’s participation in Horizon Europe, with expectations that this integration may gain momentum by the end of 2025.

 

Regarding recognition and rewarding open science practices, the report notes that recognition may grow as the country is progressively aligning with EU standards, including through participation in Horizon Europe; however, no specific initiatives are cited.

 

No unintended negative consequences of open science practices are reported.

The report indicates the existence of initiatives at both national and institutional levels that promote innovative, participatory changes at different stages of the research cycle; however, no further details are provided.

Georgia promotes international collaboration on open science through various channels, including participation in the EOSC and EU-backed projects (e.g., NI4OS-Europe and BE-OPEN), as noted above. Through these initiatives, Georgia is also involved in discussions on the establishment of regional and international funding mechanisms for open science.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted in Malaysia. Key promoters and advocates include the Ministry of Science, Technology and Innovation (MOSTI), the Academy of Sciences Malaysia (ASM), agencies such as the National Institutes of Health (NIH) Malaysia, public and private universities and the International Science, Technology and Innovation Centre for South-South Cooperation under the auspices of UNESCO (ISTIC), based in Kuala Lumpur and funded by MOSTI, among others. The report notes that dissemination efforts should be expanded to institutions in the social sciences and humanities.


Awareness-raising activities on open science take several forms, including a Symposium on Open Science (2023), the workshop “Enhancing and Strengthening Regional Open Science Implementation Mechanisms” (2023), open science webinars such as “Empowering Research through Open Science: Why This Matters Now More Than Ever”, organised by Universiti Putra in collaboration with ASM and the Malaysia Open Science Platform (MOSP), and dedicated open science pages on the websites of several universities, including Perpustakaan Sultanah Nur Zahirah Universiti Malaysia Terengganu and Universiti Sains Malaysia.

The National Science, Technology and Innovation Policy 2021-2030 (NSTIP) and its Action Plan include open science elements, encouraging open data sharing among various actors involved in research, development and innovation, and explicitly support the development of the Malaysia Open Science Platform (MOSP) as an initiative advancing this objective. In addition, the report refers to the General Circular on Open Data (2015) issued by the Prime Minister’s Department and the National Data Sharing Policy (2023), which establish a regulatory framework primarily focused on open public-sector data while also being relevant, to some extent, to open research data sharing.

 The National Guidelines on Open Science for Public Funded Research (2022), developed by ASM and the Malaysia Open Science Alliance (MOSA), a multi-stakeholder body involving representatives from ministries, agencies and higher education and research institutions, serve as a specific policy instrument guiding the implementation of open science across several dimensions, including research data management and sharing and open access to scholarly publications.

 At the institutional level, several universities have adopted open science policies, and some have formed open science committees. For example, Universiti Malaysia Sarawak (UNIMAS) has adopted the Open Science Research Data Management Policy, which clarifies responsibilities for both researchers and the institution in relation to research data management. Universiti Malaya has developed a similar policy.

 Open science practices have been integrated into existing research funding. Malaysian funding agencies, such as the Ministry of Higher Education, often include requirements in grant applications for making publications open access and for data sharing. The NIH Malaysia requires that datasets from Ministry of Health-funded research be deposited in its institutional repository. Universiti Malaysia Terengganu has data sharing requirements for recipients of its internal funding.

Malaysia has undertaken several initiatives to foster equitable public-private partnerships on open science, including through the multi-stakeholder MOSA and the MOSP.

Regarding a national monitoring framework for open science, the report mentions the NSTIP and its Action Plan, as well as the National Guidelines on Open Science for Public Funded Research, though no further details are provided as to whether dedicated open science indicators and systematic monitoring mechanisms have been established.

At the national level, the MOSP, launched in 2023 by MOSTI in collaboration with ASM, connects institutional research data repositories and provides public access to data from publicly funded research. The Malaysian Research and Education Network (MYREN) connects all public and private universities and provide high-speed network services. At the institutional level, the report indicates that Malaysian universities and research institutes, such as Universiti Sains Malaysia, Universiti Malaya and UNIMAS, maintain open data repositories. Discipline-specific repositories are also available, including the Malaysian Biodiversity Information System (MyBIS).

The report indicates Malaysia’s involvement in North-South and South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure, including through the Asia-Pacific Advanced Network (APAN), the Trans-Eurasia Information Network (TEIN) and ISTIC. In addition, through the MOSP, Malaysia collaborates with the Australian Research Data Commons (ARDC), the International Science Council’s Committee on Data (CODATA), including through its Global Open Science Cloud Initiative, and the African Open Science Platform.
​

Systematic capacity building on open science takes place in higher education and research performing institutions, focusing on librarians and researchers. Formal programmes include workshops, seminars and training on open access, Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable (FAIR) principles and data management, often organised in collaboration with MOSTI and the MOSP. The Data Stewardship for Open Science training, conducted by the ASM since 2020, has trained 310 data stewards. Informal initiatives include peer-to-peer learning, knowledge-sharing sessions and community events within universities, supported by collaborations through MYREN and APAN.

 Capacity building programmes for policymakers exist, but the report notes limited exposure, highlighting the need for further capacity building, including for legal officers, to strengthen awareness and support incorporation of open science into future policies.

 Open science competencies have been partially incorporated into higher education curricula. Research ethics is included in undergraduate studies at most higher learning institutes, and the practice of making research open and accessible is taught to the students. A more structured integration of open science curriculum is expected through the Erasmus+ Open Asia project, in which Universiti Malaya participates.

 Open educational resources are used for open science capacity building. Many institutes publish educational materials on open science through institutional platforms, social media and massive open online courses.

 Science communication initiatives in line with open science values include, for example, activities under the Universiti Malaya Community Engagement Centre (UMCares) and the Universiti Malaya Sustainable Development Centre (UMSDC), as well as the Young Scientist Network of the ASM.

The Malaysian Research Assessment (MyRA) instrument, introduced in 2006, evaluates research capabilities and performance at all higher education institutions. Since 2014 all institutions, both public and private, participate in an annual assessment through a 6-Star rating system involving document and site audits. Discussions are ongoing on integrating open science elements into this system; however, no specific initiatives have yet been implemented, and the report highlights the need for capacity-building activities to support a shift in mindset in this direction. The report also indicates that specific clauses recognising open science practices have not yet been incorporated into career evaluation and progression systems.

 Initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices include research awards with categories for research that embraces open science, such as publishing in open access journals and engaging with the community; however, the report provides no specific examples of such awards. Institutes also encourage researchers to publish in open access journals, and those who do often gain recognition in performance evaluation systems.

 The report states that no unintended negative consequences of open science practices have been observed in Malaysia.

Several initiatives promote innovative, participatory approaches at different stages of the research cycle. Preprints are being promoted, and open peer review practices are being explored at institutions such as Universiti Putra Malaysia. Awareness activities promote the benefits of sharing negative scientific results and associated data. Participatory methods include citizen science projects, such as the UM Water Warriors programme at Universiti Malaya, which engages local communities. The Stakeholder consultations are used to identify the needs of marginalised communities for incorporation into research agendas. The Forest Research Institute Malaysia has also documented traditional knowledge of indigenous peoples from five sub-ethnic groups in Peninsular Malaysia. Open innovation practices are being promoted through the concept of a Malaysian Open Innovation Platform, which would facilitate collaboration between researchers and industry; the MOSP would serve as one of its data sources.

Malaysia promotes international collaboration on open science through several channels. In addition to the above-mentioned collaborations related to shared open science infrastructure, Malaysia participates in broader international and regional networks and initiatives, including through UNESCO and the Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation (APEC). Malaysia has proposed the formation of the APEC Open Science Alliance (APECOSA) at the APEC Policy Partnership on Science, Technology and Innovation to share open science best practices among APEC economies. As noted above, several Malaysian universities, including Universiti Malaya, participates in the Erasmus+-funded Open Asia project, which facilitates open science knowledge exchange between EU countries, Malaysia and India and aims to strengthen the open science capacities of higher education institutions in Malaysia and India.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted across a wide range of national entities. At the federal level, promotion has involved several ministries, including the Federal Ministry of Innovation, Science and Technology (FMIST), the Federal Ministry of Education (FME) and the Federal Ministry of Innovation, Communication and Digital Economy (FMICDE). The National Universities Commission (NUC), the National Library of Nigeria (NLN) and the Committee of Vice Chancellors of Nigerian Universities (CVCNU) have been engaged, alongside learned societies including the Nigeria Academy of Science (NAS) and the Nigerian Academy of Engineering (NAE). The National Office for Technology Acquisition and Promotion (NOTAP) and the National Centre for Technology Management (NACETEM), the Nigerian National Commission for UNESCO and state-level bodies in Cross River and Taraba States have been also involved.


 Three major awareness-raising activities have been organised. A March 2021 workshop in Abuja on science, technology and innovation (STI) policy design aimed to strengthen the capacity of national STI experts and introduced the then-draft 2021 UNESCO Recommendation. In May–June 2023, a training session on implementation of the 2021 UNESCO Recommendation and gender-inclusive STI policymaking covered enabling factors for open science policy and encouraged use of the UNESCO Open Science Toolkit. The National Open Science Symposium under theLibrary Support for Embedded National Research and Education Network Services and E-infrastructure (LIBSENSE), organised by the West and Central African Research and Education Network (WACREN) in September 2023, sought to raise awareness of open science in Nigeria and presented avenues to amplify the presence of Nigerian journals and books the Directory of Open Access Journals (DOAJ) and the Directory of Open Access Books (DOAB).

Nigeria's National Science, Technology and Innovation Policy (NSTIP) 2022–2026 serves as the major policy framework supporting open science implementation in the country, explicitly addressing open science in section 3.13 through provisions covering open access to scientific knowledge, open science infrastructure, open engagement of societal actors and open dialogue with other knowledge systems, with these objectives further operationalised through the National Science, Technology and Innovation Roadmap. Among national-level policies addressing open science, the report also identifies the National Policy on Open Education Resources (OER) for Higher Education in Nigeria.
 
Among the policy instruments supporting open science, the report refers to the National Research and Innovation Council (NRIC), which provides strategic direction for research and innovation, including the promotion of open access to publicly funded research outputs and support for national research infrastructure.

The report indicates that no institutional-level open science policies currently exist.
 
No dedicated national funding mechanisms for open science are reported. The report attributes this to limited awareness of open science, noting that past activity in this area has been more accidental than deliberate. Open science practices are also not reported to have been integrated into existing research funding, as these practices are still described as relatively new.
 
Regarding public-private partnerships related to open science, the National Information Technology Development Agency (NITDA) under FMICDE has launched open innovation challenges encouraging public-private research collaborations in areas such as artificial intelligence, health informatics and smart agriculture, with findings shared openly
while ensuring fair licensing models. Another example reported is the Nigeria Open Data Initiative (NODI), led by FMICDE, which promotes transparency and accessibility of government and scientific data with support from private-sector partners.
 
A national monitoring framework for open science is under development, with efforts to integrate specific open science indicators, including open-access publication rates and data-sharing metrics, into the broader national STI monitoring framework.

The report indicates the existence of various types of open science infrastructures in Nigeria, including the Nigeria National Repository, which preserves and provides open access to scholarly, literary and cultural resources of Nigeria.

 

Nigeria contributes to federated regional and international open science infrastructures and participates in North-South and South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use. The Eko-Konnect, a cluster of the Nigerian Research and Education Network (ngREN), has developed an Open Science Cloud to bridge infrastructure deficits for open science in Nigeria and the broader West African region, targeting researchers, educators and institutions through community-driven governance. Nigeria participates in LIBSENSE and WACREN programmes promoting open science infrastructures and services across West Africa, with funding support from the United Kingdom's Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office (FCDO) and the AfricaConnect3 project, while also collaborating beyond the region with open science and research infrastructures such as LA Referencia and RedCLARA. Through the EU Horizon 2020-funded Sci-GaIA project and a partnership with the Conference of Italian University Rectors, Nigeria-based Eko-Konnect was among the first organisations in Africa adopted DataCite Digital Object Identifiers (DOIs) to improve the visibility of Nigerian research outputs.

Regarding capacity building on open science in higher education and research performing institutions, the report refers to two cohorts of digital literacy training for information professionals organised by the National Library of Nigeria, covering research data management and repository implementation. In addition, the National Universities Commission conducted training in the context of a 2018 ranking of Nigerian universities based on their OER holdings. OER are used partly as an instrument for open science capacity building. The report states, however, that no structured nationwide capacity-building programmes specifically for policymakers have taken place.

 

Nigeria reports the existence of efforts supporting science communication in line with open science values, referring in particular to the NSTIP, which encourages open science practices among researchers along with open engagement of societal actors and wider dissemination of scientific knowledge to diverse audiences.

The report indicates that efforts have been undertaken in Nigeria to encourage the review of existing research assessment and evaluation processes to align with open science. For example, early career researchers within the LIBSENSE and WACREN programmes have conducted awareness-raising activities aimed at encouraging university authorities to undertake such reviews, involving key Nigerian stakeholders such as the CVCNU, the Association of University Librarians in Nigerian Universities (AULNU) and the Eko-Konnect.  

 

The report states, however, that specific clauses recognising open science practices have not yet been incorporated into career evaluation and progression systems. Nor are specific initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices reported. Universities and research institutions continue to prioritise traditional output metrics, such as journal impact factors, while tenure and promotion criteria do not formally recognise open science contributions.

 

The report states that no unintended negative consequences of open science practices have been officially documented in Nigeria. It nonetheless identifies potential concerns associated with open science adoption, including fears among researchers of losing patent or commercial opportunities when findings and data are shared openly, risks linked to predatory journals charging fees without proper peer review and disparities in internet access and digital literacy that may limit participation by researchers in rural and underfunded settings.

Nigeria reports the existence of national and institutional initiatives promoting innovative, participatory approaches at different stages of the research cycle, including open innovation challenges launched by NITDA that encourage public-private research collaborations in areas such as artificial intelligence, health informatics and smart agriculture, with findings shared openly while ensuring fair licensing models.
