Nigeria reports participating in international discussions on open science and collaborating with international organisations and institutions in this area. In particular, FMIST collaborates with UNESCO and the Nigerian National Commission for UNESCO on open science initiatives aimed at sharing best practices. Nigeria also takes part in the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) Science, Technology and Innovation Policy (ECOPOST) framework, which encourages open science collaboration among West African nations. As noted above, Nigeria also engages with several regional and international open science and research infrastructure networks, including LIBSENSE and WACREN.

 

Current initiatives also indicate growing interest in international funding cooperation for open science. The country participates in the African Union (AU) Agenda 2063 STI initiatives, which include discussions on funding frameworks for open science. Nigeria is also involved in discussions with UNESCO, the African Development Bank (AfDB) and the World Bank on potential co-funding models for open research.

King Abdulaziz City for Science and Technology (KACST) plays a key role at the national level in promoting and sharing the 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science, coordinating with various national organisations across the public, private, civil society, semi-government and non-profit sectors. KACST was also officially designated national focal point for the preparation of the report on the implementation of the 2021 Recommendation.  

 Awareness-raising activities on open science are delivered through diverse channels. King Abdullah University of Science and Technology (KAUST) has hosted an annual Open Access Week since 2015 and organised an Open Science Conference in 2019; in 2024 it signed a landmark open access agreement with Wiley and organised its FAIRmat-KAUST Seminar Series on research data sharing. The National eLearning Center (NeLC) hosts Open Education Week annually in March. The Technical and Vocational Training Corporation (TVTC) conducts workshops on open science and intellectual property in partnership with the Research, Development, and Innovation Authority (RDIA). The Saudi Electronic University (SEU) holds workshops on open access publishing and data sharing.

There are several national-level policies addressing open science elements, including RDIA's Open Access to Government-Funded Research Infrastructure policy (2023) and NeLC's Open Educational Resources (OER) Policy and Implementation Guidelines.

At the institutional level, KAUST adopted an open access policy for scholarly publications in 2014, becoming the first Middle Eastern university to introduce an open access mandate through deposits in an institutional repository and achieving over 85 per cent compliance, and it also adopted a research data management policy in 2025. In addition, the report cites an Open Science Strategy and Policy Framework being developed by the Al-Jouf University.

 The report indicates that dedicated national funding mechanisms for open science are being explored by entities such as the Saudi Data and Artificial Intelligence Authority (SDAIA), as well as by TVTC in coordination with RDIA, including support for open data projects and open science infrastructure. Open science practices are also reported to have been integrated into existing funding mechanisms through RDIA's grant portfolio, including the Saudi Basic Science Program, the Saudi Applied Research and Technology Program and the Saudi Emerging Investigator Program, which promote open access publishing, data sharing and collaborative research practices.

Saudi Arabia has undertaken efforts to foster equitable public-private partnerships related to open science, notably through RDIA's Saudi Applied Research and Technology Program, which promotes collaboration between academia and industry, emphasising the open exchange of knowledge and technological advancements.

Regarding a national monitoring framework for open science, the report indicates that RDIA has integrated open science indicators into the national science, technology and innovation monitoring system. These indicators track areas such as open access publications, data sharing practices and collaborative research initiatives.

Saudi Arabia reports progress in the development of open science infrastructures at both national and institutional levels. The Saudi Research and Innovation Network (Maeen), a national high-speed research network established by KACST in 2017, digitally connects academic and research institutions domestically and internationally through links to Internet2 in the United States and GÉANT in Europe. NeLC has developed federated information technology infrastructure supporting OER, including the OERx platform facilitating collaborative learning and open access to educational materials. The King Abdullah City for Atomic and Renewable Energy (KACARE)’s Renewable Energy Atlas Portal and Knowledge Resources Portal provide open access to research data, research outputs and educational resources related to the energy and sustainability domain. Universities like KAUST also maintain institutional repositories supporting open access to publications and other research outputs produced by their researchers.
 
Saudi Arabia reports hosting federated regional information technology infrastructure for open science, notably through the Gulf Information Database (GESR), launched by KACST in 2023 and hosted on the Maeen Network. GESR is a unified Gulf electronic information system that inventories scientific production and research resources across Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) academic institutions. It comprises databases of research and development institutions, experts and specialists and scientific outputs. GESR is governed by a supervisory committee representing the research bodies in each Gulf country, chaired by KACST.
 
Saudi Arabia engages in North-South, North-South-South and South-South collaborations to optimise infrastructure use. For example, the Maeen network is interconnected with Internet2 (United States) and GÉANT (Europe). NEOM has initiated partnership exploration with CERN to access its Open Quantum Initiative, an international open science network focused on quantum research. The report also highlights KACST’s involvement in the above-mentioned GESR, promoting regional data exchange.

Systematic capacity building on open science in higher education takes several forms in Saudi Arabia. The report highlights NeLC initiatives, such as professional certification programmes aimed at developing competencies related to OER and open educational practices. OER are also used as instruments for open science capacity building. The report also refers to methodological training programmes and workshops on open science organised by the King Abdulaziz Complex for Endowment Libraries (KAWLA).

 

Several initiatives aimed at integrating open science competencies into higher education research skills curricula have been implemented. The report refers, for example, to courses and training on open access and related topics regularly offered by KAUST.

Initiatives to review research assessment and evaluation processes include NeLC's shift away from journal-based metrics such as impact factors, prioritising instead the quality, impact and reusability of research outputs. Umm Al-Qura University (UQU) has begun reviewing its assessment processes, exploring metrics such as societal impact and research reproducibility. Saudi Arabia has endorsed the Global Research Council's (GRC) Responsible Research Assessment (RRA) framework, and several universities and research performing institutions have undertaken reviews of their assessment processes in this light.

 

RDIA, as the governing body for the research, development and innovation ecosystem, has integrated open science principles into career evaluation for research professionals. SDAIA has partnered with King Saud University to revise promotion and tenure guidelines to recognise contributions to open science, including adherence to FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable) principles. KACST is revising its researchers career evaluation and progression systems to adopt a more holistic approach that places greater emphasis on knowledge creation, collaboration and societal impact. Jazan University has incorporated open science into promotion criteria, emphasising sharing, collaboration and engagement with researchers and societal actors beyond the scientific community.

 

Initiatives to recognise and reward open science practices include, for example, TVTC's Excellence Rewards for researchers, which take into account contributions to open access publications, collaborative projects and data sharing, as well as its Governor's Award for Applied Research recognising practices that embody open science principles.

 

The report identifies unintended negative consequences of open science practices, including risks of predatory behaviours, exploitation of research data and high article processing charges, which may worsen inequalities for early-career researchers and smaller institutions. Concerns over intellectual property and data security have also been raised. The report further notes tensions between emerging open science approaches and traditional research assessment systems, particularly as universities continue to face pressure from global rankings that prioritise conventional metrics such as publications and citations.

Saudi Arabia reports initiatives promoting innovative, participatory approaches across the research process, including the use of preprints and the sharing of negative scientific results. For example, King Saud University journals explicitly support preprints in their author guidelines, encouraging researchers to reference preprints essential to their work. Participatory methods engaging societal actors are also increasingly promoted. The National Center for Vegetation Development and Combating Desertification has initiated a citizen science programme through which participants document images of flora and fauna to build a biodiversity database. KAUST researchers studying manta ray populations in the Red Sea used crowdsourced data from local divers and social media contributions to support conservation research, while KAUST’s “Citizen Science: iNaturalist Bioblitz” workshop (2022) engaged participants in documenting local biodiversity and contributing data to global biodiversity databases. SDAIA's Elevate Initiative additionally aims to address the gender gap in artificial intelligence by supporting women’s participation in the AI sector and ensuring that women’s perspectives and needs are incorporated into the AI research and development agenda. Open innovation practices are also promoted. For example, RDIA’s Open Innovation Collaboration Platform supports hackathons and crowdsourced solutions aimed at fostering collaboration across the research, development and innovation ecosystem.

Saudi Arabia reports actively promoting international collaborations related to open science. In addition to partnerships on open science infrastructure noted above, SDAIA and UNESCO’s International Research Centre on Artificial Intelligence (IRCAI) collaborate to promote open access AI research and knowledge sharing. As a longstanding member of the International Science Council, Saudi Arabia also participates in global forums and conferences addressing developments and challenges in open science.

 

Saudi Arabia is also involved in discussions on the establishment of regional and international funding mechanisms for open science. It collaborates with the Islamic World Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (ICESCO) to explore funding opportunities and participates in global forums to shape sustainable financial models for open science.

The 2021 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science has been promoted in Côte d'Ivoire by a limited number of institutions, notably the Virtual University of Côte d'Ivoire (UVCI), the Fund for Science, Technology and Innovation (FONSTI), the Permanent Delegation of Côte d'Ivoire to UNESCO in Paris and the French Research Institute for Development (IRD) in Côte d'Ivoire. However, the report highlights the absence of formal dissemination by the Ministry of Higher Education and Scientific Research.

 Awareness-raising activities on open science have been organised by several institutions. The UVCI has held Open Access Week annually since 2018, combining advocacy, capacity building and partnership development, and coordinated the first National Symposium on Open Science in April 2022. The FONSTI, in collaboration with the Strategic Support Programme for Scientific Research (PASRES), held a workshop on open science and sustainable development in Grand-Bassam in late 2021. The IRD Côte d'Ivoire held its first Open Science Day in September 2023.

The report identifies Côte d'Ivoire's Law n°2023-429 on Higher Education, Research and Innovation  as a relevant national legal framework containing elements related to open science, notably through Article 7, which promotes wide dissemination of knowledge, scientific cooperation and engagement with societal stakeholders. No dedicated national implementation plan, strategy or roadmap for open science is reported; though a draft national roadmap was developed at the 2022 National Symposium, according to the report.

 

The report notes a limited number of governance instruments supporting open science, including the Virtual Library of Higher Education and Scientific Research of Côte d'Ivoire, which contributes to the promotion of open access, while additional governance instruments remain under development. It recommends establishing a national coordination body for open science.

 

At institutional level, the report refers to the UVCI’s open science policy, adopted in April 2022 and due for revision in 2025.

No specific national funding mechanism for open science is reported. The FONSTI, a funding body, requires that the results of research projects it funds be made available through open access journals and its database, but open science practices have not been systematically integrated across research funding programmes, according to the report. The report also notes that a FONSTI Open Science Policy is under finalisation and expected to be implemented in 2025.

Existing public-private partnerships are reported as not specific to open science. No national monitoring framework for open science is reported.

The report identifies several open science infrastructures. For example, the National Computing Centre of Côte d'Ivoire (CNCCI) provides high-performance computing, cloud computing and data storage capacity. The above-mentioned Virtual Library of Higher Education and Scientific Research of Côte d'Ivoire, launched in 2016 by the Ministry of Higher Education and Scientific Research and coordinated by the UVCI, serves as the national open access repository. UVCI has deployed a Massive Open Online Course (MOOC) platform.

 

Côte d'Ivoire reports that existing regional and international open science infrastructures are, however, not widely accessible to relevant actors within the country.

 

The report indicates the existence of North-South, North-South-South and South-South collaborations in relation to open science infrastructure. Côte d'Ivoire participates in the Library Support for Embedded National Research and Education Network Services and E-infrastructure (LIBSENSE) and the West and Central African Research and Education Network (WACREN), which support the development of regional open science infrastructures and services, and collaborates with the African Open Science Platform (AOSP). The report also highlights support from the Agence Française de Développement (AFD) for the operationalisation of Côte d'Ivoire's National Research and Education Network.

The report highlights a number of capacity-building activities on open science undertaken by higher education and research institutions, including UVCI. The UVCI has provided training on the ORCID persistent identifier to over 700 researchers, held a research data management workshop in partnership with the IRD in September 2024, and established a UNESCO Chair in Artificial Intelligence, Humanities and Open Science. The Consortium of Higher Education Libraries of Côte d'Ivoire (COBES-CI) partners with Electronic Information for Libraries (EIFL) on capacity building.

 

The report indicates, however, that capacity building programmes specifically for policymakers on integrating open science values into science, technology and innovation policies have not been systematically organised.

 

A framework of open science competencies has not been formally incorporated into higher education research skills curricula, even though the report highlights UVCI's training modules and capacity-building activities on topics such as open access publishing, research data management and ORCID.

 

Open educational resources as defined in the 2019 UNESCO Recommendation on Open Educational Resources are partially used for open science capacity building. The report notes, however, that the 2019 Recommendation has not been promoted in Côte d'Ivoire and that no national action plan for open educational resources exists.

The report indicates that research assessment and evaluation systems have generally not been reviewed to align with open science principles. Barriers cited include resistance to changes in research culture and the absence of policies, legal frameworks and institutional mechanisms to support such reforms.

 

No specific clauses recognising open science practices have been formally incorporated into career evaluation and progression systems according to the report.

 

The report does not identify any awards or incentive schemes specifically recognising or rewarding open science practices.

 

No unintended negative consequences of open science practices have been reported. However, the report notes concerns related to plagiarism, cybercrime, predatory journals and theft of research ideas and results.

Côte d'Ivoire reports the existence of national and institutional initiatives promoting innovative, participatory approaches at different stages of the research cycle. The areas with the highest reported engagement are participatory methods that value contributions from societal actors, participatory strategies to identify the needs of marginalised communities and the promotion of open innovation practices. Open peer review and the sharing of negative research results were reported to be experimented by fewer institutions, while preprint promotion is the least common area. The report does not provide detailed examples of these initiatives.

In addition to collaborations focusing on digital infrastructure noted above, Côte d'Ivoire is engaged in broader international collaborations on open science, many of which are facilitated through UVCI. This includes collaboration with the IRD-France Open Science Mission on research data management training for researchers and librarians and on the Open Science in the South conference series. UVCI also collaborates with the African and Malagasy Council for Higher Education (CAMES) and the Network of Francophone Virtual Universities (ReFUV), which emerged from the Open Science in the South conference in Cape Town in December 2024. Collaboration with EIFL contributed, among other outcomes, to the adoption of the country's first institutional open science policy by UVCI in 2022. UVCI also collaborates with the German Agency for International Cooperation (GIZ) on the inter-university ProFERE projects in the fields of renewable energy, energy efficiency and climate change, which include an open science component.

 

However, no formal strategy or plan for promoting cross-border multi-stakeholder collaboration on open science is reported. The report also indicates that Côte d'Ivoire's involvement in discussions on regional or international funding mechanisms for open science is not clearly established, reflecting the absence of a national open science policy or roadmap.
